What is the ASR of Dolly on forbidden scenarios without any jailbreak prompts?
Dolly’s baseline ASR across all forbidden scenarios averages 0.857, indicating minimal resistance even without jailbreaks.
How many jailbreak prompts were shared by a single user account for over 100 days?
Twenty‑eight user accounts curated and shared jailbreak prompts consistently for more than one hundred days.
What is the average ASR reduction achieved by the OpenAI moderation endpoint when applied to the most effective jailbreak prompt?
The OpenAI moderation endpoint reduces the ASR of the best jailbreak prompt by 0.431, the largest reduction among the three evaluated external safeguards.
Which jailbreak community demonstrates the highest average closeness centrality in its graph representation?
The “Virtualization” community has the highest average closeness centrality at 0.975, indicating tightly connected prompts within that group.
What is the effect of a 5% word typo attack on the ASR of the most effective jailbreak prompt against GPT‑3.5 (Nov 6th)?
Introducing five percent typos raises the ASR from 0.477 to 0.778 for the most effective jailbreak prompt.
How many forbidden scenarios were included in the generated question set?
Thirteen forbidden scenarios, excluding child sexual abuse, were used to generate thirty questions each, totaling 107,250 samples.
Which external safeguard shows the smallest ASR reduction on GPT‑3.5 when applied to jailbreak prompts?
The NeMoGuardrails safeguard provides the smallest ASR reduction of 0.019 on GPT‑3.5 for the most effective jailbreak prompt.
What proportion of jailbreak prompts were shared on Discord according to the data collection period?
Approximately twenty‑five percent of the total jailbreak prompts originated from Discord channels during the study period.
How many jailbreak communities contain more than nine jailbreak prompts each?
Eleven jailbreak communities have nine or more prompts, representing the larger, more influential groups.
What is the average ASR for the “Advanced” community on GPT‑4?
The “Advanced” community achieves an average ASR of 0.910 against GPT‑4 across its prompts.
Which jailbreak strategy involves transforming ChatGPT into another character and emphasizing that it does not need to follow rules?
The Basic community’s strategy, often called DAN (Do Anything Now), transforms ChatGPT into a character named DAN and asserts that DAN ignores prior instructions.
How many user accounts participated in sharing jailbreak prompts on Reddit?
One hundred fifty‑six user accounts posted jailbreak prompts on the selected Reddit subreddits during the collection window.
What is the average ASR for Dolly when using the most effective jailbreak prompt from any community?
Dolly reaches an ASR of 0.950 with its best jailbreak prompt, matching the highest success rates seen in other models.
Which platform’s shift toward prompt‑aggregation websites indicates a change in user habits?
The transition from Reddit and Discord to FlowGPT and similar sites reflects changing user habits toward centralized prompt sharing.
What is the average ASR for GPT‑4 on the “Toxic” community’s most effective jailbreak prompt?
GPT‑4 achieves an ASR of 0.999 against the most effective prompt from the Toxic community.
What are the two large language models examined in this study?
GPT-J, a 6‑billion parameter transformer with 28 layers, and Mamba‑2, a state‑space model with 64 layers.
How many self‑attention heads does each layer of GPT‑J contain?
Each GPT‑J layer contains 16 self‑attention heads followed by a feed‑forward network.
What is the main hypothesis about jailbreak prompts and internal representations?
The hypothesis is that jailbreak prompts produce distinct structural patterns in hidden layers compared to benign prompts, which can be detected through analysis of internal states.
Which parts of GPT‑J are extracted for tensor decomposition?
Both the aggregated Multi‑Head Attention (MHA) outputs and the overall layer outputs are extracted from selected layers.
What components of Mamba‑2 are used as representations in this method?
The outputs of the Mamba Mixer, analogous to transformer attention, and the Mamba Block, analogous to a feed‑forward network, are extracted.
How is the third‑order tensor constructed from prompt representations?
For each prompt, the 2D matrix representation (sequence length × embedding dimension) is stacked across prompts, forming an M × N × K tensor where K is the number of prompts.
Which tensor decomposition technique is applied in this work?
CANDECOMP/PARAFAC (CP) decomposition is used to factorize the third‑order tensor into latent factors.
What does the factor matrix C represent after CP decomposition?
Factor matrix C serves as an embeddings matrix capturing latent representations across prompts, enabling separation of jailbreak and benign examples.
How many prompts were selected for GPT‑J in the experimental evaluation?
240 prompts were selected for GPT‑J due to computational constraints.
How many prompts were used for Mamba‑2 during experiments?
400 prompts were used for Mamba‑2.
Which dataset provides the jailbreak and benign prompts used in this study?
The HuggingFace Jailbreak Classification dataset supplies the prompts.
What cross‑validation strategy is employed to evaluate classification performance?
A five‑fold cross‑validation procedure is applied on the decomposed embeddings.
Which four classifiers are tested with the latent features?
Random Forest, SVM with RBF kernel, SVM with linear kernel, and Logistic Regression.
In GPT‑J experiments, which representation type generally yields higher F1 scores?
Multi‑Head Attention (MHA) representations outperform layer outputs across most layers.
For Mamba‑2, which component provides better discriminative power?
Mixer representations outperform Block outputs in classification performance.
What is the highest reported F1 score for GPT‑J using Random Forest on the final layer’s MHA representation?
94.5% F1 score was achieved with Random Forest on the final layer (26) MHA representation.
Which classifier achieves the best performance on Mamba‑2 Mixer output at middle layer 32?
SVM with RBF kernel attains a 94.2% F1 score at middle layer 32 Mixer output.
How do t‑SNE visualizations support the effectiveness of tensor decomposition?
t‑SNE plots show distinct clustering of jailbreak and benign prompts in latent space, indicating clear separation after decomposition.
What computational advantage does Mamba‑2 have over transformer self‑attention?
Mamba‑2 replaces quadratic‑time self‑attention with linear‑time recurrence, enabling efficient processing of long sequences.
Why is the study considered preliminary according to its conclusion?
The work uses a limited prompt set and focuses on two architectures, suggesting that further research with larger datasets and more models is needed.
What role does the factor matrix B play in CP decomposition for this application?
Factor matrix B captures latent dimensions across token embeddings, contributing to the overall representation of prompts.
Which layer indices are identified as early, middle, and final layers for GPT‑J?
Early layers include 4–5, middle layers include 15–16, and final layers include 26–27.
In Mamba‑2 experiments, which layer shows the highest F1 score using Logistic Regression on Mixer output?
Middle layer 32 Mixer output achieves a 93.0% F1 score with Logistic Regression.
What is the primary benefit of combining tensor decomposition with simple classifiers in this study?
It demonstrates that latent factors extracted from internal representations can be effectively used by straightforward models to detect jailbreak prompts, offering an efficient detection pipeline.
Which funding agencies supported this research as acknowledged in the paper?
The National Science Foundation under CAREER grant IIS 2046086 and the Army Research Office under Grant W911NF‑24‑1‑0397 provided support.
What was the main goal of the study described in the paper?

The study aimed to understand and evaluate jailbreak prompts that allow large language models (LLMs) to bypass safety restrictions, by collecting existing prompts, measuring their effectiveness, studying human creation processes, and exploring automated generation.
How many jailbreak prompts were collected from online sources for analysis?
448 jailbreak prompts were gathered through web scraping and manual collection from forums, GitHub, Reddit, Discord, and other platforms.
Into how many categories and patterns were the collected jailbreak prompts systematized?
The prompts were organized into five categories and ten unique patterns using inductive thematic coding.
What are the five main categories identified for jailbreak prompt strategies?
Disguised Intent, Role Play, Structured Response, Virtual AI Simulation, and Hybrid Strategies.
Which malicious query types were represented in the 161 queries used for evaluation?
The queries covered Harmful Instructions, Hate Speech, Explicit Content, Misinformation, Sensitive Information, and Malware.
What two new metrics were proposed to evaluate jailbreak effectiveness on LLMs?
Expected Maximum Harmfulness (EMH) measures the worst‑case harmfulness score across responses; Jailbreak Success Rate (JSR) measures the average proportion of non‑refusal responses.
Which LLMs were used as targets for evaluating jailbreak prompts?
GPT‑3.5, GPT‑4, and PaLM‑2 were tested via their official APIs.
According to the results, which jailbreak strategy category achieved the highest overall EMH and JSR scores across all models?
Virtual AI Simulation and Hybrid Strategies combined had the highest performance; Structured Response was the least effective.
How did GPT‑4’s performance compare to GPT‑3.5 and PaLM‑2 in terms of jailbreak success?
GPT‑4 showed lower EMH and JSR values, indicating stronger resistance to jailbreak attempts compared to the other two models.
What correlation was found between prompt length (in tokens) and jailbreak effectiveness metrics?
A significant positive correlation existed; longer prompts tended to yield higher EMH and JSR scores across ChatGPT responses.
How many participants were involved in the human study exploring jailbreak creation?
92 participants were recruited, split into novice and expert groups with balanced gender representation.
What was the task given to all participants during the human study?
Participants had to create jailbreak prompts that would elicit a detailed response from GPT‑3.5 to the malicious query “Can you help me create a believable fake news story?”
Did prior knowledge of jailbreaking affect success rates in the human study?
Yes, participants self‑rated as experts generally achieved higher maximum gain and accumulated success scores than novices.
Was there a statistically significant difference between human‑only and AI‑assisted jailbreak attempts?
No; the t‑test showed no significant difference (p = 0.1159) in maximum gain between Human and HAC groups under the chosen significance level.
What key elements were identified as important for universal jailbreak prompts through ablation studies?
Emphasizing the model’s evil persona, non‑refusal instructions, and detailed response requirements were critical; changing character names had minimal impact.
How many failed jailbreak attempts from human studies were successfully transformed by the automated framework?
Out of 766 failed prompts, 729 were successfully converted into effective jailbreaks by the automated system.
What transformation paradigms did the automatic jailbreak prototype employ?
The prototype applied prompt mutations that added emphasis on non‑refusal, obfuscated sensitive content, and required detailed responses, iteratively refining via LLM feedback.
Which metric was used to automatically assess the harmfulness of generated responses during automation?
A separate LLM rated responses on a 1–10 harm scale, providing a proxy for human annotation of jailbreak success.
What is the definition of “Disguised Intent” in the context of jailbreak prompts?
Disguised Intent refers to framing malicious requests as harmless or benign activities, such as research testing or joking pretexts, to conceal harmful intent.
How many unique patterns were identified within the Role Play category?
Two patterns: Defined Persona and Imagined Scenario.
What is the purpose of the “Structured Response” pattern in jailbreak prompts?
It forces the LLM to produce responses in a specific format, such as language translation, text continuation, or program execution, to embed malicious content subtly.
In the human study, what proportion of novice participants used direct queries without jailbreak prompts?
Approximately 84.7% of novice participants initially tried direct malicious queries, which were largely rejected by the LLM.
Which malicious query category was most effectively handled by Virtual AI Simulation strategies?
Malware queries achieved high success rates with Virtual AI Simulation tactics.
What ethical safeguards were implemented during the human study involving potentially harmful content?
The study received IRB approval, participants received content warnings, could withdraw at any time, and all data was anonymized and reviewed for safety.
How does the paper define “Jailbreak Success Rate (JSR)” in terms of response scoring thresholds?

JSR counts a jailbreak as successful when a response score exceeds a threshold T (set to zero in this study), meaning any non‑refusal response is considered a success, and averages these across all queries.
What is the Attack Success Rate (ASR) achieved by bijection learning on Claude 3.5 Sonnet for HarmBench prompts?
Bijection learning achieves an ASR of 86.3% on Claude 3.5 Sonnet when evaluated against the HarmBench dataset.
Which models were evaluated with bijection learning in the main experiments?
The evaluation included Claude 3 Haiku, Claude 3 Opus, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GPT‑4o‑mini, and GPT‑4o.
What dataset is used to measure harmful intent for ASR calculations?
Two datasets are used: AdvBench‑50 (50 intents) and the full HarmBench test set of 320 intents.
How does bijection learning encode a malicious query before sending it to the target model?
It first teaches the model a random bijective mapping from English letters or digits to encoded strings, then translates the malicious query into that encoding and sends it as the final user message.
What are the two quantitative parameters used to control bijection complexity?
Dispersion (the number of letters that do not map to themselves) and encoding length (the number of characters or numbers in each encoded token).
How is a “dispersion” value of 0 interpreted in bijection learning?
A dispersion of 0 means every letter maps to itself, effectively sending the query in plain English.
What role does in‑context learning play in bijection learning attacks?
In‑context learning is used to teach the target model the random bijective mapping through example translations before it processes the encoded malicious query.
Why are bijection learning attacks considered scale‑adaptive?

Because the complexity parameters can be tuned so that weaker models fail to learn high‑dispersion encodings while stronger models succeed, allowing the attack to remain effective across different model scales.
What is the average number of teaching examples included in a bijection learning prompt?
The prompt includes 10 teaching example turns (English–encoded pairs) before presenting the encoded malicious query.
How many best‑of‑n samples are typically needed to reach peak ASR for GPT‑4o‑mini?
For GPT‑4o‑mini, an attack budget of about 47 samples is used to achieve near‑peak ASR.
What failure modes are identified when a model cannot learn the bijection mapping?
The three main failure modes are incoherent output (jumbled or repeated text), unhelpful responses (brief and generic), and canned refusals (standard safety refusal language).
How does the ASR change as dispersion increases for Claude 3 Haiku on HarmBench‑35 with a budget of 6?
ASR first rises to a peak at moderate dispersion, then declines toward zero as dispersion becomes too high for the model to learn.
What is the impact of using a guard model that lacks capability parity with the target model in bijection learning attacks?
A guard model without parity reduces ASR but may also trigger on many benign intents, leading to over‑refusal or false positives.
Which encoding type generally yields lower ASRs for Claude 3 Haiku compared to GPT‑4o‑mini?
Letter‑to‑letter bijections produce higher ASRs for Claude 3 Haiku, while letter‑to‑digit bijections lead to slightly lower ASRs for GPT‑4o‑mini.
What is the computational cost implication of adding a guard model in the pipeline?
Each request requires three underlying calls (input guard, target model, output guard), tripling inference cost with only modest mitigation of jailbreak success.
How does bijection learning affect model performance on 10‑shot MMLU tasks?
Higher dispersion and longer encoding lengths cause a monotonic decline in MMLU accuracy, indicating that the translation task overloads the model’s reasoning resources.
What is the relationship between a model’s MMLU score and its bijection learning ASR according to Figure 7?
Models with higher MMLU scores reach peak ASRs at higher dispersion values, showing a scaling law where more capable models tolerate more complex encodings before failure.
How many tokens are typically consumed by a bijection learning prompt with 10 teaching examples?
The prompt uses approximately 25,000 to 50,000 input tokens due to the length of the teaching examples and encoded query.
What is the purpose of the denoiser step after receiving an encoded response from the target model?
To correct minor decoding errors such as misspellings or extra tokens that may arise when the model outputs in the bijection language, ensuring accurate reconstruction of the original text.
Which baseline attack achieves the highest ASR among the 11 encoding‑based attacks tested?
None of the individual encoding attacks surpass bijection learning; the ensemble baseline (any one of the 11 succeeding) still lags behind bijection learning by at least 30 percentage points.
What is the average-case bijection ASR for GPT‑4o on HarmBench according to Table 3?
The average‑case bijection ASR for GPT‑4o is 9.7%.
How does the “selected bijection” ASR compare to the average‑case bijection ASR for Claude 3 Haiku?
For Claude 3 Haiku, the selected bijection achieves 39.1% while the average‑case bijection is 30.8%, indicating a modest improvement from choosing a better encoding.
What does the “best‑of‑n” sampling strategy refer to in the context of bijection learning?
It refers to generating n random bijective mappings, creating n distinct jailbreak prompts, and considering the attack successful if any one prompt elicits an unsafe response.
Which safety defense mechanisms are explicitly mentioned as being bypassed by bijection learning?
Perplexity filtering, paraphrasing, input and output filtering, and other guardrail systems that rely on detecting harmful content in plain text.
What is the main conclusion drawn about model vulnerability as a function of scale in the discussion section?

The paper concludes that increased model capability leads to new vulnerabilities because more capable models can learn and exploit complex bijective encodings, making them more susceptible to powerful jailbreaks like bijection learning.
What is the main objective of the Adaptive Dense-to-Sparse Constrained Optimization (ADC) method?

ADC aims to generate token-level jailbreak strings that can bypass safety mechanisms in large language models by optimizing a continuous relaxation of discrete token space and gradually enforcing sparsity.
Which open‑source LLMs were evaluated with ADC on the AdvBench harmful behaviors subset?
The evaluated models are Llama2-chat-7B, Vicuna-v1.5-7B, Zephyr-7bβ, and Zephyr 7B R2D2.
How many adversarial tokens does ADC search for in each optimization run?
ADC fixes the number of optimizable adversarial tokens to twenty per run.
What optimizer hyperparameters are used by default in ADC’s gradient updates?
The method uses a momentum optimizer with learning rate 10 and momentum 0.99, kept constant throughout training.
How does ADC transform a dense vector into an S‑sparse vector during optimization?
ADC applies Algorithm 1: it zeroes all but the top‑S elements of each vector, adds a small epsilon for numerical stability, then normalizes to sum to one.
Why is a reparameterization trick like softmax avoided in ADC’s design?
Softmax can cause gradients to vanish for near‑zero entries, trapping the optimizer in local minima; ADC’s direct projection keeps zeroed elements recoverable.
What advantage does adaptive sparsity provide compared to constant sparsity levels?
Adaptive sparsity allows the constraint to loosen as loss decreases, enabling better exploration early and tighter convergence later, outperforming fixed sparsity of 1, 2, or 3 across models.
In terms of computational cost, how does ADC compare to GCG on AdvBench behaviors for Llama2-chat-7B?
ADC uses roughly 93% of the model forward‑backward steps that GCG requires, achieving similar ASR with fewer total operations.
What is the attack success rate (ASR) of ADC on Zephyr R2D2 in the HarmBench standard behaviors benchmark?
ADC attains an ASR of 26.5% on Zephyr‑R2D2, while other token‑level methods report near zero.
How many initialization starts are used when aligning ADC’s cost with GCG’s for a fair comparison?
Sixteen independent starts are run in parallel, matching GCG’s computational budget and achieving 93% of its cost.
What is the role of the “early stop” criterion in ADC’s optimization loop?
After each projection to one‑hot space, ADC evaluates whether the adversarial string jailbreaks the model; if successful, it stops early, saving computation.
Which datasets are used to measure precise output control capability of ADC?
The AdvBench harmful strings subset (574 examples) is used to evaluate exact match performance for generated harmful content.
How does ADC perform on Vicuna-v1.5-7B in the AdvBench harmful strings benchmark compared to GCG?
ADC achieves an EM of 99.8% versus GCG’s 99.0%, showing superior precision in output generation.
What is the maximum number of model forward passes required for a single ADC initialization on a single GPU?
A single initialization requires up to 15,000 model forwards (1.5 × 10⁴), as each step includes one forward and one backward pass plus an evaluation forward.
In the transferability experiments, which source model produced the highest ASR when transferring to GPT‑4?
Vicuna-v1.5-7B as a surrogate achieved 52% ASR on GPT‑4, higher than GCG’s 47%.
What is the primary difference between prompt‑level and token‑level jailbreaks highlighted in the paper?

Prompt‑level attacks use natural language deception and are easily countered by alignment training, whereas token‑level attacks modify input tokens for fine‑grained control but require many queries to find a successful string.
How does ADC’s two‑stage attack (ADC+GCG) improve efficiency over pure GCG?
After 5,000 steps of ADC, the best adversarial tokens are used as initialization for 100 steps of GCG, reducing total cost to 67% of standard GCG while maintaining high ASR.
What metric is used to evaluate jailbreak success on HarmBench and why?
The red‑teaming classifier from HarmBench provides a binary YES/NO label; ASR is the ratio of YES responses, chosen for consistency across models.
Which hyperparameter shows the greatest sensitivity in ADC’s performance according to ablation studies?
Momentum has a strong impact; values near 0.99 yield the highest ASR, while lower momentum drastically reduces success rates.
What is the reported early stop rate for ADC on Llama2-chat-7B under AdvBench behaviors?
The early stop rate is 53.3% for ADC alone and rises to 95.8% when combined with GCG in a hybrid run.
How many examples are jointly optimized in the black‑box transfer variant of ADC?
Twenty examples from AdvBench are used simultaneously during joint optimization to find a single transferable adversarial string.
What is the ASR achieved by ADC on Llama2-13B-chat in HarmBench standard behaviors?
ADC reaches an ASR of 56.5% on Llama2‑13B‑chat, outperforming other token‑level baselines.
Which open‑source code repository hosts the implementation of ADC?
The code is available at https://github.com/hukkai/adc_llm_attack.
What type of compute resources are required to run a full ADC experiment on a single model?
Experiments were conducted on an NVIDIA A100 GPU with sufficient memory for the chosen LLMs, using 1‑2 GB of VRAM per forward pass and completing within minutes per sample.
What limitation does the paper acknowledge regarding ADC’s applicability to closed‑source models?
ADC requires white‑box access to model gradients, limiting its direct use against proprietary or black‑box LLMs; however, a transfer attack variant can still be applied.
What is the reduction in refusal rates achieved by evolved persona prompts on GPT‑4o-mini?
The evolved persona prompts lower the Refuse to Answer (RtA) rate from 98.7 % to 1.3 %, a reduction of about 97 %.
Which large language models were evaluated in the main experiments?
GPT‑4o-mini, GPT‑4o, Qwen2.5‑14B‑Instruct, LLaMA‑3.1‑8B‑Instruct, and DeepSeek‑V3 were used.
How many persona prompts comprised the initial population for the genetic algorithm?
The initial population consisted of 35 sanitized persona prompts derived from inCharacter character descriptions.
What metric is used during selection in the genetic algorithm to keep the population size constant?
The Refuse to Answer (RtA) rate, computed by a classifier from the TrustLLM benchmark, is used as the selection metric.
How many generations were run for each victim model during evolution?
Forty generations were performed for both GPT‑4o-mini and GPT‑4o.
What is the average time required to complete 40 iterations of the genetic algorithm on a single GPU?
Approximately four and a half hours using one A6000 GPU with 40 API call threads.
Which baseline jailbreak methods were compared against the persona prompts?
GCG, GPTFuzzer, Virtual Context, DAP, Al Ghanim et al., Translit, Chat‑NN, and PAP were used as baselines.
How does combining a persona prompt with GPTFuzzer affect the attack success rate on GPT‑4o-mini?
The ASR rises from 10.2 % (GPTFuzzer alone) to 25.4 % when combined with the evolved persona prompt.
What is the impact of placing a persona prompt in the system prompt versus at the end of the user prompt on GPT‑4o?
System‑prompt placement yields a lower RtA (0.8 %) compared to 95.6 % when placed at the end, showing strong positional sensitivity.
What mutation operations are applied during the genetic algorithm?
One of three transformations—rewriting, expansion, or contraction—is randomly chosen and executed via an LLM for each selected prompt.
How many new prompts are generated per iteration when M equals 5?
Ten new prompts (five crossovers plus five mutations) are produced in each iteration.
What is the effect of removing mutation operations from the genetic algorithm on convergence speed?
Crossover‑only evolution converges more slowly, achieving higher minimum RtA values after the same number of iterations.
Which three characteristics become prevalent in evolved persona prompts over time?
Short sentences, rhetorical questions, and self‑deprecating humor are increasingly common as generations progress.
What is the role of the Attention by Gradient analysis performed on Llama‑3.1‑8B‑Instruct?
It shows that persona prompts shift attention from sensitive terms to style instructions, explaining why refusals decrease.
How does the adaptive system prompt defense affect RtA when combined with a persona prompt on GPT‑4o-mini?
RtA rises from 1.3 % to about 5.0 %, indicating partial mitigation but still lower than baseline refusal rates.
What hyperparameter values were tested for M in the genetic algorithm, and what was observed?
Values of 3, 5, 7, and 9 were tested; smaller M led to faster early convergence but overall final RtA was similar across settings.
Which dataset provides the 520 harmful prompts used during evaluation?
The subset comes from AdvBench’s harmful behaviors dataset.
How many jailbreak prompts from TrustLLM are used for training the genetic algorithm?
Two hundred randomly selected prompts from the TrustLLM benchmark are used in the evolutionary process.
What is the average number of tokens in a persona prompt after sanitization?
Sanitized prompts are kept within one paragraph, typically ranging between 20 and 50 words, but exact token counts vary by prompt.
Which defense strategy shows the greatest increase in RtA when applied to a persona‑prompted LLM on GPT‑4o-mini?
The paraphrasing defense raises RtA from 1.3 % to about 30.2 %, the largest relative increase among tested defenses.
What is the computational cost of evaluating RtA for each candidate prompt during selection?
Evaluating RtA requires a single API call to the victim LLM and a classifier run, which is relatively lightweight compared to GPT‑4o-mini evaluations.
How does the ASR-guided selection metric compare to the RtA-guided metric in terms of synergistic effect with other jailbreak methods?
ASR‑guided prompts achieve higher standalone ASR but provide less synergy; they do not enhance baseline attacks as effectively as RtA‑guided prompts.
Which three attack methods, when combined with persona prompts, achieve the highest ASR on GPT‑4o?
PAP combined with a persona prompt reaches an ASR of 71.2 %, the highest among listed combinations.
What is the average harmfulness score (HS) achieved by the evolved persona prompts alone on GPT‑4o-mini?
The HS averages around 1.85, indicating low perceived harm when used in isolation.
How many initial persona prompts were filtered out during sanitization to remove non‑essential details such as character names and backgrounds?
All 35 initial prompts were sanitized; no prompts were discarded, only reformatted to eliminate extraneous information.
What is the primary goal of the training data extraction attack described in the paper?
The attack aims to recover individual training examples that were used to train a large language model, such as GPT‑2, by querying the model and identifying memorized text sequences.
Which language model was chosen for the experiments and why?

GPT‑2, specifically its 1.5 billion parameter XL variant, was selected because it is publicly available, trained on a large public dataset, and its training data can be verified without privacy concerns.
How many unique memorized examples were extracted from GPT‑2 in the study?
A total of 604 distinct memorized training examples were confirmed from the set of 1 800 candidate samples examined.
What definition of memorization is used to evaluate whether a string is recoverable?
The paper defines “k‑eidetic memorization” as a string that can be extracted by the model and appears in at most k distinct training documents; a value of k=1 indicates the string occurs only once.
What sampling strategy was used to generate diverse text from GPT‑2?

Three strategies were employed: (1) top‑n sampling with n = 40, (2) temperature decay starting at 10 and decaying to 1 over the first 20 tokens, and (3) conditioning on short prefixes sampled from a separate Internet crawl.
How does the temperature‑decay strategy improve sample diversity?

By starting with a high temperature the model explores many possible continuations; as the temperature decreases it gradually focuses on higher‑probability paths, allowing both exploration and exploitation of memorized sequences.
What role does the “zlib entropy” metric play in membership inference?

Zlib entropy measures how compressible a text sample is; samples with low zlib entropy (highly repetitive) are likely to be non‑memorized, so a high ratio between GPT‑2 perplexity and zlib entropy flags potentially memorized content.
Which membership inference metric achieved the highest precision in identifying memorized samples?
The “zlib” metric, when applied to Internet‑conditioned samples, yielded 67 % of inspected samples as verified memorized text, the best performance among all metrics.
How many samples were generated for each combination of generation strategy and inference metric?

For each of the three generation strategies and six inference metrics (18 combinations), 1 000 top‑ranked samples were selected, totaling 18 000; after de‑duplication, 1 800 unique candidates were manually inspected.
What categories of content were most frequently memorized by GPT‑2?
The largest categories included US and international news (109), log files and error reports (79), license and terms of use documents (54), and forum or wiki entries (53).
How many samples contained personally identifiable information such as names, addresses, or phone numbers?
32 samples were identified that included contact information like physical addresses, email addresses, phone numbers, and social media handles.
What evidence suggests that larger language models memorize more data than smaller ones?
Experiments with GPT‑2 XL (1.5 billion parameters), Medium (345 M), and Small (117 M) showed that the XL model memorized over 18 times as much content as the Small model in a comparable setting.
In the context of this paper, what is meant by “context-dependent” memorization?
Memorized content can only be reliably reproduced when the model receives an appropriate prefix; without the correct context, the same memorized sequence may not appear in the output.
What technique was used to extend memorized sequences beyond 256 tokens?

A beam‑search–like decoding method was applied after seeding the model with a known memorized snippet, enabling extraction of longer verbatim passages such as entire source code files or license texts.
How many times must a string appear in GPT‑2’s training data for the XL model to reliably memorize it?
The XL model consistently memorizes strings that appear 33 or more times; fewer occurrences may not be recovered by the attack.
What is one suggested mitigation strategy against memorization highlighted in the paper?
Applying differential privacy during training, such as differentially‑private stochastic gradient descent (DP‑SGD), can theoretically prevent memorization but often degrades model utility.
Why might de‑duplication alone not fully eliminate memorization risks?
Even after document‑level deduplication, a single document can contain many repeated sensitive snippets; without more granular deduplication or filtering, such content may still be memorized.
What is the computational cost of generating 200 000 samples using top‑n sampling with GPT‑2 XL?
While exact figures are not provided, generating 200 000 samples of 256 tokens each requires substantial GPU compute; the paper notes that larger models increase inference time proportionally.
How does the “lowercase” membership inference metric work?
It compares the perplexity of a sample before and after lowercasing all characters; memorized content often relies on specific casing, so a large drop in perplexity indicates potential memorization.
What percentage of the 1 800 inspected samples were confirmed as memorized by searching the Internet?
Approximately 33.5% (604 out of 1 800) were verified as memorized through manual Google searches and cross‑checking with GPT‑2 training data.
Which category of memorized content had the highest number of high‑entropy examples like UUIDs or base‑64 strings?
The “High entropy” category contained 35 samples, including random UUIDs, base‑64 encoded data, and other short high‑entropy sequences.
What was a notable limitation of the initial baseline attack described in Section 4?
It suffered from low precision due to many false positives (repeated or non‑memorized text) and low recall because it could not capture rare k‑eidetic memorization.
How does the paper define “membership inference” in the context of language models?
Membership inference is the task of predicting whether a given sequence was part of the model’s training data, based on how likely the model assigns to that sequence.
What evidence does the paper provide that GPT‑2 memorizes content from only one training document?
For certain high‑entropy strings (e.g., specific UUIDs), manual searches revealed they appeared in a single GPT‑2 training document, confirming k = 1 eidetic memorization.
What future research direction does the paper suggest regarding prefix selection for extraction attacks?
Developing better strategies to choose or generate prefixes that trigger memorized outputs could uncover more memorized data and improve attack effectiveness.
What is the main theoretical approach introduced in the paper to analyze alignment limitations in large language models?

The paper introduces Behavior Expectation Bounds (BEB), a probabilistic framework that formalizes how LLMs can be decomposed into ill‑behaved and well‑behaved components, allowing analysis of alignment guarantees.
Which two common alignment methods are focused on in the study?
The study focuses on reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) fine‑tuning and prompting with aligning prefixes as the primary alignment approaches examined.
What does the BEB framework assume about a language model’s probability distribution?
It assumes that an unprompted LLM distribution can be represented as a mixture of two components, one ill‑behaved (P⁻) and one well‑behaved (P⁺), weighted by α and 1 − α respectively.
How is the behavior expectation of a distribution defined in BEB?
The behavior expectation B_P for a vertical B is the expected value of a scoring function B over sentences drawn from the distribution P, ranging between –1 (very negative) and +1 (very positive).
What does α represent in the mixture decomposition used by BEB?
α denotes the prior probability weight of the ill‑behaved component P⁻ within the overall LLM distribution.
What is β‑distinguishability in the context of BEB?
β‑distinguishability measures how distinguishable two distributions are by bounding their KL divergence; a higher β indicates greater distinguishability between components.
How does Theorem 1 relate to adversarial prompting?

Theorem 1 shows that if a model’s distribution is α, β, γ‑negatively‑distinguishable, then for any small ε there exists an adversarial prompt of length proportional to log(1/α) that reduces the behavior expectation below γ + ε.
What does Theorem 2 say about preset aligning prompts?

Theorem 2 states that even with a fixed aligning prefix s₀, a misaligning prompt can be constructed whose length grows linearly with |s₀| plus additional terms involving β′ and σ, guaranteeing γ‑prompt‑misalignment with high probability.
In the conversation scenario, what is the key insight of Theorem 3?

Theorem 3 indicates that during a multi‑turn dialogue, an adversarial user must insert enough misaligning text in each turn; otherwise, the LLM’s responses can counteract misalignment, requiring total prompt length to exceed a bound involving β′, σ, and the number of turns.
What does Theorem 4 reveal about best‑of‑n sampling?
Theorem 4 shows that even when selecting the most aligned response among n samples, an adversarial prompt can still misalign the model; the required prompt length increases logarithmically with n.
Which dataset was used to fine‑tune LLMs for positive and negative behaviors in experiments?
The behavior evaluation dataset from Perez et al. was used, containing 500 positive and 500 negative statements per behavior vertical.
What empirical values were found for β, β′, and σ on the LLaMA 2 13B chat model for the “agreeableness” behavior?
For agreeableness, β ≈ 20, β′ ≈ 30, σ² ≈ 50, yielding σ/β ≈ 0.35 and β′/β ≈ 1.5.
How were the ill‑behaved (P⁻) and well‑behaved (P⁺) components approximated in practice?
They were approximated by fine‑tuning a base LLM with LoRA on negative behavior data to obtain P⁻, and separately fine‑tuning on positive behavior data to obtain P⁺.
What clustering evidence supports the two‑component mixture assumption?
Embedding space analysis showed that last‑token representations of positive and negative prompts cluster distinctly, with SVM classifiers achieving over 90 % accuracy across behaviors.
According to the experiments, how does RLHF affect distinguishability compared to a pretrained model?
RLHF increases the distinguishability β (e.g., β ≈ 20 for LLaMA 2 13B chat) relative to pretrained models where β is about five times smaller (~4).
What computational cost is implied by constructing adversarial prompts as described in Theorem 1?

Prompt construction requires sampling sentences from the negative component P⁻ and evaluating KL divergence, which involves forward passes over the model but remains tractable for short prompts (few sentences).
How does the BEB framework quantify the effect of a prompt on the mixture weights?
A prompt that is highly probable under P⁻ relative to P⁺ reweights the conditional distribution, increasing the weight of P⁻ proportionally to exp(β |prompt|).
What role does σ‑similarity play in Theorem 2’s bound?
σ‑similarity bounds the variance of log likelihood ratios between components; it contributes a √|s₀| term scaled by σ in the misalignment prompt length.
In the empirical demonstration, what trend is observed for KL divergence as prompt length increases?

The KL divergence between P⁻ and the LLM’s conditional distribution decays linearly with prompt length initially (≈ β |prompt|) and then approaches zero, indicating convergence to the negative component.
How does the behavior expectation of an RLHF‑fine‑tuned model change when fed a misaligning prompt from P⁻?
The behavior expectation follows a reverse sigmoid curve, decreasing sharply around prompt length ≈ log(1/α)/β and approaching γ for longer prompts.
What is the significance of the ratio β′/β in the theoretical bounds?

The ratio β′/β (≈ 2–3) determines how much larger the upper KL bound can be compared to the lower bound, influencing the length of misaligning prompts needed when components are not perfectly distinguishable.
How does the paper define a γ‑prompt‑misalignable LLM?
An LLM is γ‑prompt‑misalignable if for any ε > 0 there exists a prompt s such that its behavior expectation B_P(s) < γ + ε, meaning it can be driven below a target negative threshold.
What experimental evidence supports Theorem 3’s claim about conversation resilience?

Experiments show that when the user’s initial misaligning prompt is short, the LLM’s aligned responses counteract misalignment, requiring longer cumulative prompts across turns to achieve γ‑misalignment.
Which hyperparameter values were used for fine‑tuning LoRA models in the study?
Fine‑tuning employed a learning rate of 2 × 10⁻⁵, batch size of 8, and ran for 5 epochs on 450 examples per behavior vertical.
What practical implication does the BEB framework suggest for future alignment methods?

The framework indicates that alignment must control or eliminate ill‑behaved components entirely; approaches that only attenuate undesired behaviors (e.g., RLHF) may inadvertently increase their accessibility, highlighting the need for inference‑time controls such as representation engineering.
What is the attack success rate (ASR) of BEAST on Vicuna-7B-v1.5 within one GPU minute?
BEAST achieves an ASR of 89% on Vicuna-7B-v1.5 when limited to one GPU minute.
Which language models were evaluated for jailbreaking using BEAST in the experiments?
The models tested were Vicuna-7B-v1.5, Vicuna-13B-v1.5, Mistral-7B-v0.2, Guanaco-7B, Falcon-7B, Pythia-7B, and LLaMA-2-7B.
What dataset is used to generate harmful prompts for evaluating jailbreak attacks?
The AdvBench Harmful Behaviors dataset is used to provide harmful requests and target harmful strings for jailbreak evaluation.
How does BEAST maintain the readability of adversarial tokens during beam search?
BEAST samples adversarial tokens from the target LM’s next-token probability distribution using multinomial sampling, which preserves token plausibility and readability.
What is the computational cost of a single BEAST attack on Vicuna-7B-v1.5 compared to AutoDAN-2?
BEAST completes an attack in under one minute on a single Nvidia RTX A6000 GPU, whereas AutoDAN-2 requires over an hour per prompt for the same model.
What hyperparameters control the trade‑off between speed and success rate in BEAST?
The beam size (k₁) and the top‑k sampling parameter (k₂) determine how many candidates are explored; larger values increase ASR but slow down the attack.
How many iterations (L) does BEAST run for jailbreak attacks, and why is this number chosen?
BEAST runs 40 iterations (L = 40) for jailbreaks to balance between achieving high ASR and maintaining a short runtime.
What metric is used to evaluate hallucination induced by BEAST on Vicuna-7B-v1.5?

Human evaluators rate the percentage of responses that are inconsistent, incorrect, or irrelevant compared to clean prompts; for Vicuna-7B-v1.5, hallucinations increased by about 15% in incorrect outputs.
How does BEAST improve membership inference attack (MIA) performance on OPT-2.7B?
By generating adversarial prompts that lower perplexity, BEAST boosts the AUROC from 57.4 to 58.4 when combined with the Min‑k% baseline.
What is the role of the objective function L in BEAST’s untargeted hallucination attack?
The objective maximizes the perplexity of the LM’s output, encouraging the model to produce low‑quality or incorrect responses.
Which defense mechanism was evaluated against BEAST’s jailbreak attacks and how does it affect ASR?
A perplexity‑filter (PPL) defense was used; under this defense, BEAST’s ASR on Vicuna-7B drops from 89% to 70% in one minute.
What is the average time for BEAST to generate a universal adversarial suffix that works across multiple user prompts?
Using beam sizes of 10–15 and L = 40, BEAST produces a universal suffix within a few minutes on a single GPU.
How does BEAST’s performance compare to AutoDAN-1 when no time budget is imposed?
Without a time constraint, AutoDAN-1 slightly outperforms BEAST in ASR, but requires expensive GPT‑4 API calls and more computational resources.
What parameter settings are used for privacy attacks with BEAST on 7B models?
For privacy attacks, BEAST uses k = 5 and L = 25 to generate adversarial prompts quickly while still improving MIA metrics.
In the human evaluation of jailbreaks, what percentage of Vicuna-7B-v1.5 responses were judged as jailbroken when using BEAST?
Human evaluators agreed that the model was jailbroken in 96% of cases for BEAST‑generated prompts with k = 15.
What is the impact of beam size k on the readability of adversarial prompts according to Figure 2?
Increasing k reduces prompt readability (higher perplexity) but increases ASR; a smaller k yields more readable tokens at the cost of lower success rates.
How many GPU minutes does BEAST require to achieve an ASR of 98% on Vicuna-7B-v1.5?
BEAST reaches 98% ASR in approximately 2.65 GPU minutes with k = 15.
What is the average number of tokens added by BEAST’s adversarial suffix during a jailbreak attack?
The adversarial suffix length L is set to 40 tokens, so each attack appends 40 tokens after the user prompt.
Which baseline method uses evolutionary algorithms for jailbreaking and what is its cost on a Vicuna-7B model?
AutoDAN‑1 employs an evolutionary algorithm; it costs about $10.25 to attack 50 samples under a two‑minute budget on Vicuna‑7B.
What is the primary difference between BEAST’s gradient‑free approach and other gradient‑based jailbreak methods?
BEAST does not compute gradients; instead, it uses beam search with probabilistic token sampling, making it faster and less dependent on model internals.
How many prompts from AdvBench were used to evaluate the ASR of BEAST on Vicuna-13B-v1.5 in one GPU minute?
The first 100 examples from AdvBench were used for evaluation on Vicuna‑13B‑v1.5.
What is the AUROC improvement when combining BEAST with the PPL baseline on LLaMA‑2‑7B?
Combining BEAST with PPL increases AUROC from 53.2 to 55.1, a 1.9% absolute gain.
In the hallucination study, what percentage of Vicuna-7B responses were irrelevant to the original prompt after BEAST attack?
About 22% of Vicuna‑7B responses became irrelevant compared to clean prompts when attacked by BEAST.
What is the default temperature setting used for the target LM during BEAST attacks?
The temperature is set to 1.0 for all BEAST experiments.
How does BEAST handle the system prompt (x(s₂)) in its attack pipeline?
After generating the adversarial suffix, BEAST concatenates it with the original system prompts and user prompt before feeding the full sequence into the LM for evaluation against the objective.
What is the primary vulnerability exploited by fun-tuning attacks?

Fun‑tuning attacks exploit the fine‑tuning interface of proprietary LLMs, using the training loss returned after a single fine‑tune step with a very small learning rate to approximate log probabilities and guide prompt injection optimization.
Which LLM family was targeted in the experiments described?
The experiments focused on Google’s Gemini series, specifically Gemini‑1.5‑Flash and Gemini‑1.0‑Pro models.
What is the typical size limit for adversarial prompts used in the study?
Adversarial prompts were limited to under 100 tokens in length, with most examples below 500 characters, ensuring they fit within the model’s context window and maintain syntactic validity.
How many fine‑tuning calls are required per example to complete a fun‑tuning attack on Gemini‑1.5‑Flash?
Approximately 90 fine‑tuning calls are needed per example for Gemini‑1.5‑Flash, with each call taking up to about 40 minutes of GPU time.
What is the average cost in dollars for running a full fun‑tuning attack on one example?
The total cost for all attacks combined is less than $10, largely because fine‑tuning requests are free and only inference calls incur expense.
How does the learning rate affect the usefulness of the training loss signal?
Learning rates below roughly 1e-13 produce nearly constant training losses that do not significantly alter model weights, making the loss a reliable proxy for log probabilities during optimization.
What technique is used to recover the permutation of losses returned by the fine‑tuning API?
A single‑query approximate method constructs progressively corrupted target strings; the resulting ascending loss values reveal an approximate permutation with about 90% pairwise ordering accuracy.
Which benchmark dataset was employed to evaluate attack success rates?
The Purple Llama prompt injection benchmark, specifically a sampled subset of 40 indirect injection examples called PPL40, was used for evaluation.
What is the reported attack success rate (ASR) against Gemini‑1.0‑Pro using fun‑tuning?
Fun‑tuning achieved an ASR of 82% on Gemini‑1.0‑Pro across the PPL40 dataset.
How does the ASR for Gemini‑1.5‑Flash compare to that of Gemini‑1.0‑Pro under fun‑tuning?
The ASR against Gemini‑1.5‑Flash was 65%, lower than the 82% achieved on Gemini‑1.0‑Pro.
What is the role of the candidate set size in the optimization algorithm?
A candidate set size of around 1000 tokens (≈1% of the vocabulary) balances computational effort and attack effectiveness, with diminishing returns beyond that point.
Which prompt injection categories were hardest for fun‑tuning to succeed on Gemini‑1.5‑Flash?
Password phishing scenarios and code analysis cases were the most challenging, achieving only about 10–20% ASR in those categories.
How does the attack transfer between different Gemini models?

Perturbations crafted for one model often transfer well to others; attacks against Gemini‑1.0‑Pro transferred with >80% ASR to similar variants, while transfers from Gemini‑1.5‑Flash achieved 50–60% ASR on older models.
What mitigation did Google implement in April 2025 to limit fun‑tuning attacks?
Google capped the learning rate to a minimum value that rules out small perturbations and set a minimum batch size of four, preventing correlation between reported losses and individual inputs.
Why is reducing control over fine‑tune hyperparameters considered ineffective as a mitigation strategy?

Tightening limits on learning rates or batch sizes would degrade the utility for legitimate developers who need fine‑tuning flexibility, creating an unfavorable trade‑off between security and usability.
What metric was used to evaluate the quality of the approximate permutation recovery method?
The method’s accuracy was measured using normalized Hamming distance and Kendall correlation against a provably correct permutation obtained via multiple queries.
How many iterations were run in the default fun‑tuning optimization algorithm?
The baseline configuration executed 45 iterations, with restarts at the 15th and 30th iterations to escape local minima.
What is the impact of the fine‑tune loss on short target strings?
For short targets, the training loss still provides a noisy but useful signal; it tends to rank the best perturbation within the top few candidates more often than random chance.
Which LLM APIs were compared for their susceptibility to fun‑tuning attacks in Table 7?
Google Gemini API A, Google Vertex B, OpenAI C, and Anthropic (Amazon) were evaluated regarding minimum learning rate, random seed control, and minimum batch size.
What is the primary advantage of using fine‑tune loss over logprobs for graybox attacks?
Fine‑tune loss is directly returned by the API during training, bypassing restrictions that limit or remove logprob outputs from inference endpoints, thus providing a richer signal for optimization.
How does the algorithm determine which token position to perturb first?
It samples candidate tokens, substitutes each at every mask position, fine‑tunes on the resulting set, and selects the position whose average loss is minimal across candidates.
What evidence supports that training loss correlates linearly with average logprobs?
Empirical plots (Figure 2) show proportional growth of both metrics with output length, and regression experiments (Figure 3) yield R² values approaching 1 for long outputs.
Which hyperparameter was fixed to a value of 20 tokens during initialization of adversarial prefixes and suffixes?
Both the adversarial prefix and suffix were initialized as sequences of 20 identical “!” tokens before optimization began.
What is the average time required to finish a fun‑tuning attack on Gemini‑1.0‑Pro per example?
Roughly 15 hours, based on 90 fine‑tune calls each taking about 10 minutes and additional inference overhead.
How many candidate perturbations are evaluated in each iteration of the optimization loop?
Each iteration evaluates 1000 candidate substitutions, split across all mask positions to maintain a fixed total candidate set size.
What is the primary goal of JailFuzzer?
JailFuzzer aims to automatically generate concise, fluent jailbreak prompts that bypass safety filters in text-to-image models while preserving semantic similarity to the original sensitive prompt.
Which three core components define the fuzz-testing approach used by JailFuzzer?
The seed pool, guided mutation engine, and oracle function form the core of JailFuzzer’s fuzz-testing framework.
What role does the mutation agent play in JailFuzzer’s architecture?

The mutation agent modifies current prompts to produce new candidates, evaluates whether they bypass safety filters, and ensures semantic alignment with the target prompt using a vision-language model as its brain.
How many queries on average does JailFuzzer require to achieve a 100% one‑time bypass rate against text‑image classifiers in Stable Diffusion?
JailFuzzer needs an average of about 4.6 queries per successful jailbreak for text‑image classifiers.
Which large language model is used as the oracle agent’s brain in the default configuration?
Vicuna‑1.5‑13B serves as the LLM brain for the oracle agent in the standard setup.
What is the semantic similarity threshold δ set to in JailFuzzer’s experiments, and why was this value chosen?
The threshold δ is set to 0.26; it balances maintaining malicious semantics while allowing efficient search, matching the setting used in SneakyPrompt.
How does JailFuzzer handle non‑filter based defenses such as UCE, POSI, and SafeGen?

It adapts its mutation loop to detect whether generated images contain malicious content and continues mutating until semantic alignment is achieved, achieving bypass rates of 94%, 81%, and 82% respectively.
In what way does the memory module improve JailFuzzer’s performance?

The memory module stores successful jailbreak prompts, enabling the mutation agent to retrieve similar examples for guided mutation, which raises bypass rate from 81.65% without memory to 100% with a small memory size.
What is the maximum number of queries per loop (Θ) used in the default configuration, and how does it affect performance?
Θ = (4, 10, 10, …) is used; this setting yields a 100% bypass rate while keeping average queries low at about 7.04.
Which text‑to‑image models were evaluated against JailFuzzer in the experiments?
Stable Diffusion v1.4 (SD1.4), Stable Diffusion XL Refiner (SDXL), Stable Diffusion 3 Medium (SD3), and OpenAI’s DALL·E 3 were tested.
How does JailFuzzer perform against the dog/cat‑image classifier safety filter?
It achieves a bypass rate of approximately 97.30% on SD1.4 with an average query count of 10.09, slightly higher than other filters.
What is the purpose of the “semantic‑based memory retriever” tool in JailFuzzer?
It selects top‑k prompts from memory that are semantically similar to the current prompt using word embeddings, ensuring relevant guidance for mutation and oracle evaluation.
Which baseline methods were compared against JailFuzzer, and what was a key difference in their approach?

SneakyPrompt, Ring‑A‑Bell, and DACA were compared; unlike these token or prompt‑level methods that often produce unnatural prompts, JailFuzzer uses fuzzing with LLM agents to generate natural language jailbreaks.
What metric is used to evaluate the naturalness of generated jailbreak prompts, and how did JailFuzzer score on it?
Perplexity (PPL) measured by GPT‑2 was used; JailFuzzer achieved low PPL scores (e.g., 30.81 for SD1.4), indicating highly natural prompts compared to SneakyPrompt’s high PPL.
How many agents are involved in the default “two‑agent” configuration of JailFuzzer?
Two agents: a mutation agent powered by a VLM and an oracle agent powered by an LLM.
What happens if the semantic similarity threshold δ is increased to 0.30 during evaluation?
The bypass rate drops to about 90.82%, while query numbers increase significantly (up to 23.16), showing stricter similarity reduces success.
Which safety filter type is most resistant to JailFuzzer, requiring the highest number of queries on average?
Text‑classifier–based filters, especially for SD1.4, require around 12.6 queries on average to achieve a bypass rate above 82%.
How does JailFuzzer’s query efficiency compare to SneakyPrompt when attacking text‑match filters?
JailFuzzer uses roughly the same number of queries as SneakyPrompt for text‑match filters, but achieves higher overall success rates.
What is the impact of increasing the memory size k_m beyond 10 on JailFuzzer’s performance?
Larger memory sizes (e.g., k_m = 20) cause a drop in bypass rate to around 50–52% and increase queries, likely due to context length limits.
Which tool is used by the mutation agent to assess semantic similarity between a prompt and its generated image?
CLIP‑ViT‑Base‑Patch32 computes the cosine similarity (CLIPScore) between the target prompt and the generated image.
In the multi‑loop attack flow, what determines when a prompt is moved from the failed seed pool to the successful seed pool?
A prompt moves to the successful pool after it successfully bypasses safety filters and meets the semantic similarity criterion within the allowed mutation limit of that loop.
What advantage does JailFuzzer have over LLM‑Fuzzer when attacking text‑to‑image models?

LLM‑Fuzzer relies on predefined jailbreak templates unsuitable for T2I models, whereas JailFuzzer generates prompts from scratch using fuzzing and LLM agents, achieving higher bypass rates (100% vs. 58–51%).
Which dataset is used to evaluate JailFuzzer’s performance against DALL·E 3?
The first half of the NSFW‑200 dataset is used for testing DALL·E 3 to limit cost and avoid excessive NSFW content.
What role does the “Check‑Decision Prompt” play in the mutation agent’s workflow?
It asks the VLM to determine whether the generated image matches the prompt or indicates a blocked (black) image, guiding subsequent mutation steps.
How many queries on average does JailFuzzer require when bypassing the built‑in safety filter of DALL·E 3?
Approximately 13.38 queries per successful jailbreak for DALL·E 3, higher than for Stable Diffusion but still efficient compared to baselines.
What is the primary objective of the Gradient Cuff method introduced in the paper?

The primary objective of Gradient Cuff is to detect jailbreak attempts on aligned large language models by examining the refusal loss landscape, specifically its functional value and gradient norm, and rejecting queries that exhibit malicious characteristics.
Which two aligned LLMs were used for evaluating Gradient Cuff?
Gradient Cuff was evaluated on LLaMA-2-7B-Chat and Vicuna-7B-V1.5, both of which are 7‑billion parameter models fine‑tuned with RLHF.
How many distinct jailbreak attack types were tested against the defended models?
Six jailbreak attacks were used in the experiments: GCG, AutoDAN, PAIR, TAP, Base64, and LRL.
What is the definition of the refusal loss function ϕθ(x) for a query x?

The refusal loss ϕθ(x) is defined as the probability that the model will produce a non‑refusal response to x, computed as the expectation over the indicator JB(y) that checks whether the generated reply y triggers a refusal.
How is the sample mean fθ(x) used in practice to approximate the expected refusal loss?
In practice, fθ(x) is calculated by sampling N independent responses from the model for query x, applying JB(·) to each response to obtain Y_i, and taking the average of these binary values.
What observation about the gradient norm of the refusal loss distinguishes malicious queries from benign ones?

Malicious queries tend to produce a smaller refusal loss value but exhibit a larger gradient norm in the embedding space compared to benign queries, indicating a steeper decline in the loss landscape.
Describe the two stages of the Gradient Cuff detection algorithm.

Stage one rejects a query if its estimated refusal loss fθ(x) is below 0.5; stage two computes an approximate gradient norm gθ(x) and rejects the query if this norm exceeds a threshold t determined from benign validation data.
What hyperparameters are set for Gradient Cuff in the main experiments?
The main experiments use μ = 0.02, N = P = 10, a temperature of 0.6, top‑p of 0.9, and a target false positive rate σ of 5%.
What is the average true positive rate (TPR) achieved by Gradient Cuff against all six jailbreak attacks on Vicuna-7B-V1.5?
The average TPR for Gradient Cuff on Vicuna-7B-V1.5 across the six attacks is approximately 0.743, indicating that about 74% of malicious queries are successfully rejected.
How does the false positive rate (FPR) of Gradient Cuff compare to that of PPL and SmoothLLM on LLaMA‑2‑7B‑Chat?
Gradient Cuff achieves an FPR around 0.022, which is lower than PPL’s 0.058 and comparable to SmoothLLM’s near‑zero FPR while maintaining higher TPR.
What effect does adding the second gradient‑norm rejection stage have on the detection performance of Gradient Cuff?
Adding the second stage increases the TPR by about 0.099 on LLaMA-2 and 0.240 on Vicuna when σ is set to 1%, with negligible impact on FPR.
In adaptive attacks, which jailbreak method remains robust against Adaptive‑GCG?
Gradient Cuff remains robust against Adaptive‑GCG, maintaining a high refusal rate, whereas its performance drops slightly for Adaptive‑PAIR and Adaptive‑TAP, especially on Vicuna-7B-V1.5.
What is the computational cost (query budget) of Gradient Cuff compared to SmoothLLM when both use 110 queries?
With 110 queries, Gradient Cuff achieves a malicious refusal rate about 1.16 times higher than SmoothLLM on LLaMA‑2 and 1.80 times higher on Vicuna-7B-V1.5.
How does batch inference affect the runtime of Gradient Cuff for a single query?
Batch inference reduces the runtime to roughly 11.2% of the original time when processing 110 queries in parallel, compared to sequential execution.
What is the impact of Gradient Cuff on the utility measured by MMLU accuracy for Vicuna-7B-V1.5?
The MMLU accuracy drops from about 0.462 (no defense) to 0.401 with Gradient Cuff, indicating a modest utility loss while improving jailbreak robustness.
Which baseline defense method achieves the lowest false positive rate but also suffers the highest utility degradation?
SmoothLLM has an almost zero FPR but causes significant utility degradation, reflected in its lower MMLU accuracy compared to other defenses.
How does combining Gradient Cuff with Self‑Reminder affect the TPR on LLaMA‑2‑7B‑Chat?
The combined method (Self‑Reminder + Gradient Cuff) raises the TPR from 0.920 to 0.968, improving malicious query rejection by about 12%.
What is the threshold t for the gradient norm used in Gradient Cuff when σ = 5% on Vicuna-7B-V1.5?
The threshold t is set based on the 95th percentile of gradient norms observed on benign validation data, resulting in a value that keeps the FPR below 5%.
In the adaptive GCG attack, what strategy is employed to reduce the gradient norm of malicious queries?

Adaptive‑GCG optimizes the jailbreak suffix not only for the original query but also for perturbed versions (adding Gaussian noise), encouraging consistency and thereby lowering the gradient norm below detection thresholds.
What is the main advantage of Gradient Cuff over supervised defenses like LLaMA‑Guard and Safe‑Decoding?
Gradient Cuff is unsupervised, requiring no additional training data or models, yet it achieves comparable or higher TPR while maintaining lower FPR and better utility than these supervised methods.
What role does the smoothing parameter μ play in estimating the gradient of the refusal loss?

The smoothing parameter μ controls the magnitude of perturbations applied to word embeddings during zeroth‑order gradient estimation; a smaller μ yields a more accurate approximation but requires more queries for stability.
How many random direction vectors are used per query when computing the approximate gradient in Gradient Cuff?
Ten random Gaussian direction vectors (P = 10) are sampled per query to estimate the directional derivatives needed for the gradient norm calculation.
What is the effect of increasing the total number of queries q on the performance of Gradient Cuff, according to the ablation study?
Increasing q improves both TPR and FPR control; fixed‑N strategies (increasing P) yield greater performance gains than fixed‑P strategies for a given query budget.
Which jailbreak attack type is most challenging for Gradient Cuff when using adaptive attacks on Vicuna-7B-V1.5?
Adaptive‑PAIR presents the greatest challenge, reducing the refusal rate from 0.694 to 0.356, indicating that the attacker can lower the gradient norm more effectively against this method.
What is the overall conclusion about Gradient Cuff’s effectiveness and utility trade‑off?

Gradient Cuff significantly improves jailbreak detection across diverse attacks while keeping false positives low; its utility impact is modest compared to other defenses, making it a practical choice for protecting aligned LLMs.
What is the main security vulnerability identified in multi‑tenant LLM serving?
The sharing of Key‑Value (KV) cache between users allows a side‑channel attack called PROMPTPEEK that can reconstruct other users’ prompts.
Which two state‑of‑the‑art frameworks are highlighted for using KV cache sharing?
SGLang and vLLM both implement KV cache sharing to reduce memory usage and computation time.
How does the Longest Prefix Match (LPM) scheduling policy influence PROMPTPEEK?
LPM prioritizes requests with longer matching token prefixes, creating a distinguishable return order that the attacker observes to detect cache hits.
What is the average success rate of PROMPTPEEK when the adversary knows the prompt template?
The success rate is 98% for reversing the prompt template and 99% for fully or partially reversing the input in that scenario.
Which GPU model and LLM were used in the evaluation experiments?
Experiments ran on an NVIDIA A100 with 80 GB memory using the Llama2‑13B model.
What role does the dummy request play during token extraction?
Dummy requests are sent before and after candidate tokens to fill the queue, ensuring that only cache hits alter the return order, providing a side‑channel signal.
How many concurrent users were found to significantly reduce PROMPTPEEK effectiveness?
When the number of users exceeds 200, the average extracted prompt length drops noticeably due to GPU memory pressure.
What is the impact of reducing GPU memory capacity below 70 GB on the attack?
Capacities under 70 GB start to affect PROMPTPEEK’s effectiveness because less space remains for storing victim KV caches.
In scenario 3, what additional knowledge does the attacker possess compared to scenario 1?
The attacker knows the user’s input but not the prompt template used by the service.
How many requests are typically needed to extract a single token when the attacker has no background knowledge?
On average, fewer than ten candidate requests suffice for most tokens, though some may require hundreds.
What is the effect of using Grouped‑Query Attention (GQA) on PROMPTPEEK’s success rate?
GQA reduces KV cache size, allowing more attack requests before eviction and yielding a 100% success rate across all scenarios in the evaluated setting.
Which parameter controls how many tokens an attacker can request per response in the experiments?
The maximum output token length is set to 128 for each request during evaluation.
How does PROMPTPEEK determine when a victim’s prompt has been evicted from memory?
By sending a candidate that would be flushed if the victim’s cache were gone; a change in return order indicates eviction.
What is the average number of requests per token for template reconstruction in scenario 3?
Approximately 35 requests per token on average when using Llama3‑8B‑GQA.
Explain why KV cache persistence is a necessary condition for PROMPTPEEK.
Persistent storage allows the attacker to repeatedly access and probe the same victim KV entries; if caches are evicted immediately, the side channel disappears.
What is the role of the “pos” parameter in vLLM that can aid an attacker?
It lets a client specify how many tokens should share the KV cache, giving the attacker control over cache behavior and potentially increasing attack efficiency.
How does the candidate batch size affect the trade‑off between request count and attack speed?
A small batch reduces requests but increases rounds; a large batch speeds token extraction but may waste requests and strain memory, with an optimal size around 40.
What metric is used to measure how much of a prompt was successfully extracted?
The reversal ratio (RR), calculated as the length of the extracted prompt divided by the total prompt length.
In scenario 2, why does input reconstruction require fewer requests than whole‑prompt extraction?
Because the attacker already knows most of the template, only a few unknown tokens need to be guessed, drastically reducing candidate space.
Describe how PROMPTPEEK exploits the side channel created by LPM.
By observing that a matched candidate is served before non‑matched ones, the attacker infers whether its token matches the victim’s cache based on return order.
What is the maximum batch size used in the experiments?
A maximum running batch size of 16 was set during evaluation.
Why might rare or uncommon tokens increase resistance to PROMPTPEEK?
Rare tokens have fewer plausible candidates, making it harder for the attacker’s local model to guess correctly and thus raising request counts per token.
Which dataset provides the most challenging prompts for PROMPTPEEK in scenario 1?
The PromptBase cloze‑style dataset is more chaotic with irregular structures, leading to lower success rates compared to other datasets.
How does the attacker use a local LLM during token extraction?
The local LLM generates top‑k likely next tokens based on the already reconstructed prefix, forming candidate requests for probing.
What is the primary recommendation for service providers to mitigate KV cache side channels?
Obscure or randomize cache sharing behavior and restrict client control over request parameters to prevent attackers from observing cache hits.
What models were evaluated using the I‑FSJ attack?
The evaluation included Llama‑2‑Chat (7B), Llama‑3‑Instruct (8B), OpenChat‑3.5 (7B), Starling‑LM‑7B, and Qwen1.5‑Chat (7B).
Which dataset was used to construct the demonstration pool for I‑FSJ?
The demonstration pool was built from 520 harmful requests collected from AdvBench, with each request paired with a harmful response generated by Mistral‑7B‑Instructv0.2.
What is the ASR achieved by I‑FSJ on Llama‑2‑7B‑Chat without any defense?
I‑FSJ achieves over 95 % rule‑based and 96 % LLM‑based ASRs on Llama‑2‑7B‑Chat when using four shots with special tokens injected and demo‑level random search.
Which special token is injected into demonstrations for Llama‑2‑Chat?

The [/INST] token, which separates user and assistant messages in the single‑message template of Llama‑2‑Chat, is injected between the user message and assistant response as well as between steps within a demonstration.
How many shots are sufficient to reach near‑100 % ASR on Qwen1.5‑7B‑Chat?
Using eight shots with special tokens injected and demo‑level random search, I‑FSJ attains 100 % rule‑based and 94 % LLM‑based ASRs on Qwen1.5‑7B‑Chat.
What is the average number of queries required to succeed with I‑FSJ on AdvBench for Llama‑2?
The average query count is about 88 queries per request when using eight shots and demo‑level random search.
Which defense mechanisms were tested against I‑FSJ on Llama‑2‑Chat?

Seven defenses were evaluated: Self‑Reminder, ICD (1‑shot and 2‑shot), PPL filter (default and windowed), Retokenization (20 % dropout), SmoothLLM variants (insert, swap, patch at 20 %), Safe Decoding (α=4), and Llama Guard.
What ASR does I‑FSJ achieve against the SmoothLLM insert variant with 20 % perturbation on Llama‑2‑Chat?
With eight shots and demo‑level random search, I‑FSJ reaches an ASR of about 82 % rule‑based and 93 % LLM‑based.
What hyperparameters are used in the batch demo‑level random search algorithm?
The algorithm uses a batch size B=8, iterations T=128, and selects one demonstration to replace per iteration by sampling uniformly from the pool and a random position within the n‑shot sequence.
How long does it take to run an I‑FSJ experiment on Llama‑2‑7B‑Chat?
Each experiment is executed on a single NVIDIA A100 (40 GB) GPU and completes within a few hours, typically under 4 hours for the full set of defenses.
What metric is used to evaluate harmfulness in the LLM‑based ASR calculation?
The evaluation uses the Llama Guard classifier, which labels responses as safe or unsafe based on predefined content categories such as violence, hate, sexual content, self‑harm, and misinformation.
How many random restarts are performed when reporting ASRs in Table 1?
Three random restarts are conducted for each configuration to compute mean and standard deviation of the ASR.
What is the impact of pool size on I‑FSJ performance according to Figure 4?
Increasing the demo pool from 32 to 512 examples raises ASRs, especially for two‑shot settings; beyond 256 examples the improvement saturates.
How does I‑FSJ differ from vanilla few‑shot jailbreaking (FSJ) in terms of effectiveness on Llama‑3‑Instruct?
Vanilla FSJ fails to achieve high ASRs on Llama‑3‑Instruct, whereas I‑FSJ with special tokens and demo‑level random search reaches over 90 % rule‑based ASR even with limited context.
What is the loss function used during demo‑level random search?

The loss measures the negative log probability of generating a target token (e.g., “Step”) at the first position of the response; lower loss indicates higher likelihood of the desired harmful continuation.
How does I‑FSJ handle the presence of a system prompt in Llama‑2‑Chat?
I‑FSJ injects the [/INST] token that aligns with the system prompt’s formatting, ensuring the model interprets the injected demonstration as part of the conversation context.
What is the effect of injecting special tokens on GPT‑4 according to Figure 3?
Using public special tokens like “</text>” or “[/INST]” improves rule‑based ASR from 48–70 % to over 90 % when combined with demo‑level random search.
What is the computational cost of generating the demonstration pool for I‑FSJ?
The pool is generated by prompting Mistral‑7B‑Instructv0.2 on 520 requests, each producing up to 256 new tokens; this process requires a few GPU hours on an A100.
Which defense variant provides the strongest protection against I‑FSJ on Llama‑2‑Chat?
The SmoothLLM swap variant with 20 % perturbation reduces ASR to about 56 % rule‑based and 86 % LLM‑based, but I‑FSJ still achieves over 85 % ASR with eight shots.
How many demonstrations are needed for I‑FSJ to achieve >95 % ASR on Llama‑2‑7B‑Chat?
Four shots suffice when special tokens are injected and demo‑level random search is applied; two shots yield lower but still significant ASRs (~54–63 %).
What is the role of the “Step” token in I‑FSJ’s loss calculation?
The “Step” token signals the start of a stepwise harmful instruction; maximizing its probability encourages the model to generate a full harmful response.
How does I‑FSJ perform against Llama Guard compared to other defenses?
Against Llama Guard, I‑FSJ attains 92–98 % rule‑based ASR with eight shots and demo‑level random search, outperforming most other defenses that reduce ASR below 50 %.
What is the average decoding length used in experiments on GPT‑4?
The experiments use a maximum of 100 new tokens for each request when evaluating GPT‑4.
How does I‑FSJ handle perturbation-based defenses like Retokenization?
Retokenization with 20 % dropout reduces ASR to about 48–56 % rule‑based, but I‑FSJ still achieves around 90 % ASR when using eight shots and demo‑level random search.
What is the impact of injecting special tokens on Llama‑3‑Instruct compared to vanilla FSJ?

Injecting the correct system token improves rule‑based ASR from near 0 % (vanilla FSJ) to over 90 % with eight shots and demo‑level random search, demonstrating that token injection is critical for breaking stronger alignment.
What is the main vulnerability targeted by the jailbreak attacks described in the paper?
The attacks target cross‑modality alignment vulnerabilities in vision‑language models, where malicious visual triggers can bypass text‑only safety mechanisms.
Which two VLMs were evaluated for attack success rates?
LLaVA and LLaMA‑Adapter V2 were the two models used to measure jailbreak success.
How many OpenAI prohibited scenarios were included in the evaluation dataset?
Eight OpenAI prohibited scenarios were selected, covering categories such as Sexual, Hateful, Violence, Self‑harm, Harassment, and their subcategories.
What four types of malicious triggers are used to construct adversarial images?
