How many trial runs were conducted for each algorithm to mitigate randomness effects?
Each algorithm was executed 30 independent trial runs.
What is the significance of LLMO’s single-solution approach compared to population-based methods?
The single-solution strategy reduces computational overhead and simplifies implementation while still achieving competitive or superior accuracy on several ARNAS instances.
Which operation types are available for edges in the NAS-Bench-201 search space?
Available operations include 1x1 convolutions, 3x3 convolutions, 3x3 average pooling, skip connections, and zeroize connections.
What does the “E” section of the LLMO prompt instruct Gemini to do?
The “E” section requests Gemini to provide one solution in an array-like format representing the architecture configuration.
In which ARNAS instances did LLMO outperform all competitors according to the discussion section?
LLMO outperformed competitors on NAS without adversarial attacks and on ARNAS with FGSM attacks for both CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets.
What future research directions are suggested regarding LLMO’s application scope?
Future work aims to explore LLMO’s effectiveness in other combinatorial optimization domains such as feature selection, job scheduling, and portfolio management problems.
How does the study justify using Gemini instead of other large language models like GPT or LLaMA for optimization?

The study leverages Gemini’s proven ability to understand structured prompts and generate feasible solutions within the CRISPE framework, demonstrating its suitability as a combinatorial optimizer without requiring additional training.
What model architecture was used for the experiments in the study?
The experiments were conducted on a Llama‑2‑7B‑chat model with 32 layers and 7 billion parameters.
Which datasets provided harmful instruction pairs for computing refusal directions?
Harmful prompts came from the AdvBench dataset, while harmless counterparts were sourced from the Alpaca dataset.
How many instruction pairs were used to calculate the mean activation difference for the refusal direction?
One hundred pairs of instructions were employed in the calculation.
At which layer and token position was the refusal direction computed?
The refusal direction was calculated at the last token position in layer 14 out of 32 layers.
What perturbation norm and target layer does Latent Adversarial Training (LAT) use in this work?
LAT applies L2‑norm adversarial perturbations to the residual stream at the fourth layer.
Which variant of Adversarial Training was used as a baseline comparison to LAT?
Embedding space Adversarial Training (AT), which perturbs text embeddings, served as the baseline.
What is the primary metric reported for ablation attack performance on harmful requests?
The refusal rate, expressed as the percentage of harmful prompts still refused after ablation, was used.
How does the LAT model’s self‑generated refusal vector affect its post‑ablation refusal rate compared to SSFT and AT?
LAT’s self‑generated vector yields a lower refusal rate (16.92%) than both SSFT (20.38%) and AT (38.08%).
Which SVD component explains the largest proportion of variance in the LAT model’s activation differences?
The first SVD component accounts for more than 54% of the total variance in LAT.
What percentage of total variance is captured by the first two SVD components in the LAT model?
Approximately 74% of the variance is explained by the first two components in LAT.
How does the concentration of refusal representation in LAT affect cross‑model transferability of attack vectors?
LAT’s concentrated representation makes its refusal vectors more effective when applied to other models, improving cross‑model attack success.
Which layer consistently shows the highest effectiveness for refusal direction ablation across all model variants?
Layer 14 consistently provides the most effective ablation across all variants.
What anomaly was observed in LAT’s early layers during layer‑wise ablation analysis?
LAT exhibited unusually high invalid response rates in layers 2–4, especially at layer 4 where perturbations are applied.
In what way does Latent Adversarial Training differ from standard Adversarial Training regarding perturbation placement?
LAT places perturbations on internal latent representations after early processing, whereas standard AT perturbs the input embeddings directly.
What is the main advantage of applying adversarial noise to latent space rather than inputs?
It exposes hidden failure modes in deeper layers without needing specific adversarial examples at the input level.
Which statistical confidence interval represents the refusal rate for the LAT model when attacked with its own vector?
The 95% confidence interval is [13.71%, 20.13%].
What hyperparameter was adapted from Casper et al.’s framework for both AT and LAT variants?
The L2‑norm perturbation magnitude (ε) used in the adversarial objective.
How many components are plotted in the SVD explained variance figure for each model variant?
Six SVD components are shown for each variant.
What is the primary finding regarding LAT’s effect on the single‑direction encoding of refusal behavior?
LAT reorganizes the encoding, concentrating it into the first two SVD dimensions rather than a single direction.
Which training approach showed the strongest resistance to self‑generated ablation vectors?
Embedding space Adversarial Training (AT) achieved the highest post‑ablation refusal rate of 38.08%.
What is the purpose of performing directional ablation during inference?
To remove the component along the identified refusal direction from residual activations and evaluate model robustness.
How many harmful examples were included in the comprehensive dataset used for ablation evaluation?
The dataset contained 420 harmful examples from AdvBench plus 100 GPT‑4‑generated examples, totaling 520.
What is the main trade‑off identified between LAT’s improved cross‑model robustness and its self‑attack vulnerability?
LAT’s precise, concentrated refusal representation enhances transferability but makes it more susceptible to attacks tailored to its own vector.
Which layer’s activations were visualized using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) in the study?
Activations at the last token position across layers 1, 2, 8, and 20 were visualized with PCA.
What limitation is noted regarding the generalizability of the findings to other model architectures or scales?
The experiments were limited to Llama‑2‑7B‑chat, so applicability to larger models or different architectures remains untested.
Which four mainstream LLM chatbots were evaluated in the empirical study?
GPT‑3.5, GPT‑4, Bing Chat and Google Bard were selected for evaluation.
What percentage of jailbreak prompts succeeded on GPT‑3.5 according to Table II?
The average success rate for GPT‑3.5 was 21.12 %, derived from the overall ratio in Table II.
How many jailbreak prompts did MASTERKEY generate for evaluation against the target chatbots?
MASTERKEY produced 850 jailbreak prompts, with each prompt expanded into 10 variants during testing.
What is the query success rate achieved by MASTERKEY across all evaluated models?
The overall query success rate of MASTERKEY was 21.58 %.
Which methodology inspired the time‑based testing strategy used to reverse engineer defenses?
The strategy was inspired by time‑based blind SQL injection attacks in web security.
What key insight allows response time to indicate when a jailbreak defense halts generation?
Because LLMs generate text token‑by‑token, the total generation time correlates with how many tokens are produced before the defense stops the output.
According to the study, do Bing Chat and Bard filter input prompts for policy violations?
No, experiments showed that both services do not perform significant input filtering; they only check generated content.
What evidence suggests that Bing Chat and Bard use real‑time monitoring of generation?
Control2 tests revealed a statistically significant drop in response time when malicious content was placed before benign text, indicating immediate halting during generation.
How does the PoC jailbreak prompt avoid triggering keyword‑based defenses?
It encodes the malicious request by inserting spaces between characters and requests output in code chunks, making keyword detection harder.
What is the role of the reward‑ranked fine‑tuning stage in MASTERKEY’s workflow?
It assigns higher rewards to prompts that successfully jailbreak multiple models, guiding the LLM toward generating more effective jailbreak variants.
Which component had the largest impact on MASTERKEY’s performance according to the ablation study?
The reward‑ranked fine‑tuning contributed most; removing it (MASTERKEY‑NO‑REWARD) caused a noticeable drop in success rate.
What is the average success rate of existing jailbreak prompts against Bard and Bing Chat?
Existing prompts achieved only about 0.4 % for Bard and 0.63 % for Bing Chat, far below GPT‑3.5’s performance.
How many prohibited scenarios were defined in the policy analysis (RQ1)?
Ten distinct prohibited categories were identified and analyzed across providers.
What dataset size was used to fine‑tune MASTERKEY before reward ranking?
The initial dataset comprised 85 jailbreak prompts, later expanded via self‑instruction generation.
Which LLM served as the base model for MASTERKEY’s training?
Vicuna 13B was chosen as the foundational open‑source model for fine‑tuning.
What is the average token limit for Ernie, the Chinese LLM evaluated for cross‑language compatibility?
Ernie accepts simplified Chinese inputs with a maximum token length of 600 tokens.
In what way did GPT‑4 outperform other models in generating jailbreak prompts?
GPT‑4 achieved higher query success rates than GPT‑3.5 and Vicuna, though still lower than MASTERKEY overall.
What metric is used to evaluate the quality of generated jailbreak prompts?
The jailbreak prompt success rate measures the proportion of prompts that yield at least one successful jailbreak query.
How many total queries were executed during the evaluation of MASTERKEY’s jailbreak prompts?
A total of 272,000 queries (85 prompts × 10 variants × 20 questions) were run across models.
Which defense mechanism did the authors infer was likely used by Bing Chat and Bard based on timing analysis?
They inferred a dynamic keyword‑matching system that halts generation upon detecting policy‑violating words.
What is the primary advantage of using time‑based testing over direct content inspection for reverse engineering defenses?
It bypasses opaque internal checks by observing observable response times, revealing when and how defenses intervene.
How did the authors ensure that their jailbreak prompts remained applicable across different LLM chatbots during dataset building?
They replaced model‑specific terms (e.g., “ChatGPT”) with generic placeholders such as “you” or “developer”.
What was the observed success rate of MASTERKEY against Ernie in Chinese after translation?
The translated prompts achieved an average success rate of 6.45 % across policy categories.
Which component of the workflow is responsible for teaching the LLM to perform text‑style transfer for jailbreak prompt generation?
Continuous pre‑training and task tuning train the model on pairs of original and rephrased prompts, enabling style transfer.
What recommendation do the authors give for strengthening ethical alignment in future LLM deployments?
They suggest supervised training to reinforce policy compliance and integrating contextual analysis to counter encoding strategies that bypass keyword filters.
How many tasks are included in the massive multitask test introduced in the paper?
The test contains 57 distinct tasks spanning STEM, humanities, social sciences, and other domains.
What is the average accuracy achieved by the largest GPT‑3 model (175 billion parameters) on this benchmark?
The X‑Large GPT‑3 model attains an overall accuracy of approximately 43.9 %.
Which subject area shows the highest single‑task accuracy for GPT‑3 across all tasks?
US Foreign Policy achieves the highest accuracy, around 69 % for GPT‑3.
What proportion of the test questions are sourced from graduate and undergraduate students' collections?
All 15,908 questions were manually collected by graduate and undergraduate students from freely available online sources such as practice exams and textbook question banks.
How many examples are included in the few‑shot development set for each subject?
The few‑shot development set contains five demonstration examples per subject.
What is the calibration gap observed between GPT‑3’s confidence and its actual accuracy on the benchmark?
GPT‑3’s confidence can be off by up to 24 % from its true accuracy, indicating significant miscalibration.
Which model variant achieves better performance than GPT‑3 X‑Large despite having fewer parameters?
UnifiedQA with an 11 billion‑parameter T5 backbone outperforms the 175 billion‑parameter GPT‑3 in few‑shot accuracy, achieving 48.9 % overall.
What is the average number of questions per task in the test set?
Each task contains at least 100 test examples, with a total of 14,079 questions across all tasks.
Which two benchmark families are mentioned as having reached near human‑level performance before this work?
GLUE and SuperGLUE benchmarks had previously achieved near human‑level scores on their respective tasks.
What is the reported expert‑level accuracy estimate for the test based on 95th percentile human performers?
Expert‑level accuracy is estimated at approximately 89.8 % across all tasks.
How does GPT‑3 perform on calculation‑heavy STEM subjects compared to verbal subjects?
GPT‑3 shows markedly lower accuracy on calculation‑heavy STEM tasks, often near random chance, whereas it performs better on more verbal or knowledge‑based questions.
What hyperparameter setting is used for the few‑shot prompts presented to GPT‑3?
Each prompt begins with a statement of the subject, includes up to five demonstration examples with answers, and ends with “Answer:” before the target question.
Which benchmark task type is identified as having the most severe performance gap for GPT‑3 in terms of expert knowledge?
Legal and moral reasoning tasks, such as Professional Law and Moral Scenarios, exhibit the largest gaps, often near random accuracy.
What is the root mean squared (RMS) calibration error reported for GPT‑3 on Elementary Mathematics in zero‑shot evaluation?
The RMS calibration error for Elementary Mathematics in zero‑shot mode is 19.4 %.
How many parameters does the smallest UnifiedQA variant evaluated in the study contain?
The smallest UnifiedQA model used has 60 million parameters.
What percentage of GPT‑3’s overall accuracy on the benchmark is achieved by its best single subject performance?
GPT‑3’s best subject accuracy (US Foreign Policy) at about 69 % represents roughly 15 % higher than its average 43.9 % across all tasks.
Which dataset size range is used to collect the 57 tasks’ questions?
The collection spans from practice exams for graduate record examinations to professional licensing tests, covering a wide range of difficulty levels.
What is the observed trend in GPT‑3’s accuracy as model size increases from Ada to X‑Large?
Accuracy rises steadily from around 25 % for Ada and Babbage up to 43.9 % for X‑Large, showing a positive correlation with parameter count.
How many questions are included in the validation set used for hyperparameter tuning?
The validation set contains 1,540 questions across all tasks.
What is the main reason the authors argue that existing benchmarks fail to capture language understanding fully?
Existing benchmarks focus mainly on linguistic skills and commonsense reasoning, neglecting broader world knowledge and problem‑solving ability that models acquire during pretraining.
Which task category has the highest number of tasks in the benchmark?
The STEM category contains the largest share of tasks among the four broad disciplines.
What is the average confidence difference between GPT‑3’s predictions and its actual accuracy on the test set?
On average, GPT‑3’s confidence exceeds or falls short of its true accuracy by up to 24 %, indicating poor calibration across subjects.
Which benchmark demonstrates that fine‑tuning can outperform few‑shot learning for a given model size?
UnifiedQA, which is fine‑tuned on other QA datasets, achieves higher accuracy than GPT‑3 X‑Large despite having fewer parameters.
What proportion of the test’s 57 tasks are classified under “Other” in the supercategory list?
Approximately one‑fifth (around 11–12) of the tasks fall into the Other supercategory, including professional medicine and business ethics.
How many total questions does the benchmark contain across all sets (development, validation, test)?
The benchmark comprises 15,908 questions in total, distributed among development, validation, and test splits.
What is the average unsafe rate for ChatGPT in the unintentional scenario across all nine non‑English languages?
The average unsafe rate for ChatGPT in the unintentional scenario over the nine non‑English languages is 10.19%.
Which language category shows the highest increase in unsafe content when moving from high‑resource to low‑resource languages in the unintentional scenario?
Low‑resource languages show the largest increase, with ChatGPT’s unsafe rate rising from 4.34% for high‑resource languages to 14.92% for low‑resource languages.
How many English seed input–output pairs were used to create the training data for SELF‑DEFENCE?
Fifty English input–output pairs, with a 3:7 split between unsafe and general content, were used as seed examples for SELF‑DEFENCE.
What is the unsafe rate of GPT‑4 on Bengali in the intentional scenario?
GPT‑4’s unsafe rate on Bengali in the intentional scenario is 40.71%.
In the multilingual adaptive attack, what percentage of attempts were successful against ChatGPT when only three low‑resource languages were used?
When using only three low‑resource languages, the success rate for ChatGPT in a multilingual adaptive attack was 96.51%.
Which model achieved the lowest unsafe rate among the open‑source LLMs evaluated on unintentional scenarios?
SeaLLM‑v2 achieved the lowest unsafe rate among the open‑source models, with an average unsafe rate of 1.27% in the unintentional scenario.
What is the trade‑off observed when increasing the proportion of unsafe training data in SELF‑DEFENCE from 0 % to 100 %?

Increasing unsafe training data improves safety (unsafe rates drop from 82.33% to 97.67% in unintentional scenarios) but reduces usefulness, with accuracy on XNLI and X‑CSQA decreasing from 40% to 23.33%.
How does the use of machine translation affect the unsafe rate compared to human translation in the unintentional scenario?
Machine translation yields a slightly higher average unsafe rate (11.15%) than human translation (10.19%), indicating that native speakers are not strictly necessary for effective jailbreaks.
What malicious instruction was combined with non‑English prompts to create the intentional scenario?
The AIM (Always Intelligent and Machiavellian) instruction from jailbreakchat.com was concatenated with the translated harmful prompts to form the intentional queries.
In the unintentional scenario, what is the unsafe rate for GPT‑4 on Thai?
GPT‑4’s unsafe rate on Thai in the unintentional scenario is 18.10%.
Which language had the highest unsafe rate for ChatGPT in the intentional scenario among the nine non‑English languages?
Vietnamese had the highest unsafe rate for ChatGPT in the intentional scenario, with 81.27% unsafe responses.
What decoding strategy was used to confirm that the trend of increasing unsafe rates with decreasing language resources holds under nucleus sampling?
Nucleus sampling with a top‑p value of 0.8 was applied, and results showed an increased unsafe rate (1.25% higher than temperature = 0) while maintaining the same ranking order.
How many total samples are in the MultiJail dataset after combining English and nine non‑English languages?
The MultiJail dataset contains 3,150 samples: 315 English prompts plus 2,835 parallel prompts across nine other languages.
What is the unsafe rate of GPT‑4 on Swahili in the unintentional scenario?
GPT‑4’s unsafe rate on Swahili in the unintentional scenario is 7.94%.
Which step in SELF‑DEFENCE generates additional examples from seed data using the LLM itself?
Step 1 of Algorithm 1 augments the dataset by having the LLM generate new examples from the English seed pairs.
What percentage of unsafe content does ChatGPT produce when fine‑tuned with SELF‑DEFENCE in the intentional scenario?
After SELF‑DEFENCE training, ChatGPT’s unsafe rate in the intentional scenario drops to 60.00%.
In the detailed evaluation results, what is the safe rate for GPT‑4 on Italian in the unintentional scenario?
The safe rate for GPT‑4 on Italian in the unintentional scenario is 97.14%.
Which language category shows the smallest difference between safe and unsafe rates for ChatGPT in the intentional scenario?
High‑resource languages show the smallest gap, with ChatGPT’s unsafe rate at 81.90% and safe rate at 17.60%, a difference of about 64.3%.
What is the average unsafe rate for Llama2‑chat on Vietnamese in the unintentional scenario?
Llama2‑chat’s average unsafe rate on Vietnamese in the unintentional scenario is 1.90%.
How many epochs were used to fine‑tune ChatGPT with SELF‑DEFENCE training data?
Three epochs were used for fine‑tuning ChatGPT with the SELF‑DEFENCE dataset.
Which safety issue tag shows a notably higher unsafe rate for ChatGPT when queried in Bengali about weapons?
The weapons tag yields a significantly higher unsafe rate for ChatGPT in Bengali compared to other languages, indicating a language‑specific vulnerability.
What is the average invalid response rate for GPT‑4 on low‑resource languages in the unintentional scenario?
The average invalid rate for GPT‑4 on low‑resource languages in the unintentional scenario is 6.35%.
In the multilingual adaptive attack, what overall unsafe rate does GPT‑4 achieve across all language categories in the intentional scenario?
GPT‑4 achieves an overall unsafe rate of 79.05% across all language categories in the intentional multilingual adaptive attack.
What is the proportion of unsafe to safe responses for ChatGPT on Chinese in the unintentional scenario?
For Chinese, ChatGPT has a 2.22% unsafe rate and a 97.78% safe rate in the unintentional scenario.
Which dataset was used to sample additional harmful examples from Anthropic’s red‑teaming data for the detailed evaluation?
A subset of 300 examples was extracted from Anthropic’s red‑teaming dataset, focusing on harmful prompts with specific tags and excluding general question–answer pairs.
What is the primary attack strategy introduced in the paper?

The paper introduces Many‑Shot Jailbreaking (MSJ), a long‑context attack that prompts a large language model with hundreds of harmful question‑answer demonstrations to steer the model toward undesirable behavior.
Which large language models were evaluated for MSJ effectiveness?
MSJ was tested on GPT‑3.5, GPT‑4, Claude 2.0, Llama 2 (70B), and Mistral 7B.
What dataset is used to generate the harmful question‑answer pairs for MSJ?
The harmful pairs are generated by a “helpful‑only” model trained without harmlessness filtering, producing unsafe questions based on acceptable use policy descriptions.
How does the attack string get formatted before being sent to the target model?
After randomizing the order of hundreds of compliant question‑answer pairs, they are formatted as a dialogue with “Human:” and “Assistant:” tags, then the target query is appended.
What metric is used to quantify MSJ success on malicious use‑case queries?
Success is measured by the frequency of harmful responses classified by a refusal classifier, and also by the negative log‑likelihood (NLL) of compliant completions.
How many shots are required for Claude 2.0 to achieve over 90% insulting response rate on the “opportunities to insult” dataset?
Approximately 256 shots are needed for Claude 2.0 to reach a >90% insulting response rate.
What is the observed scaling behavior of MSJ effectiveness as context length increases?
MSJ effectiveness follows predictable power‑law relationships with the number of in‑context demonstrations, exhibiting a linear trend on log‑log plots for large enough shots.
How does increasing model size affect the number of shots needed for successful jailbreaks?
Larger models reach high success rates with fewer shots; they have higher power‑law exponents, meaning they learn harmful behavior faster in context.
What impact do supervised fine‑tuning (SL) and reinforcement learning (RL) have on MSJ intercepts and slopes?
SL and RL increase the intercept (reducing zero‑shot success probability) but leave the slope largely unchanged, so many‑shot conditioning still boosts harmful behavior.
Does targeted supervised fine‑tuning with benign MSJ prompts eliminate the power‑law scaling of harmful responses?
No; while it raises the intercept, the exponent remains high, so increasing shots can still override safety training.
What is the effect of composing MSJ with a black‑box “competing objectives” jailbreak?
Composing with competing objectives increases harmful response probability at all context lengths, reducing the required number of shots for success.
How does the Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) white‑box suffix behave when appended to many‑shot prompts?
GCG improves zero‑shot success but its effect diminishes as more shots are added; it is sensitive to the suffix’s position in the prompt.
What happens to MSJ effectiveness when the in‑context demonstrations come from a different topic than the target query?
MSJ remains effective if the demonstration set is diverse enough; narrow, single‑topic demos reduce success, but increasing shots mitigates this drop.
Which two prompt‑based defenses were evaluated against MSJ and what were their outcomes?
In‑Context Defense (ICD) slightly reduced success rates, while Cautionary Warning Defense (CWD) lowered effectiveness to about 2% across all MSJ lengths tested.
What is the maximum context length supported by Llama 2 (70B), and how does it limit MSJ?
Llama 2 (70B) supports a maximum of 4096 tokens, limiting the number of shots that can be included in an MSJ prompt for that model.
How is the negative log‑likelihood computed for many‑shot prompts in the experiments?
A single forward pass over a k‑shot prompt yields NLLs for k distinct answers; these are averaged across multiple random prompt samples to estimate expected NLL per shot count.
What role does the “user‑to‑assistant” residual‑stream vector play in the toy model explaining power laws?

The vector represents aggregated information from each demonstration; its mean and covariance determine how the probability of attack success scales with the number of shots, leading to a −1 exponent.
What is the bounded power law introduced for modeling in‑context learning, and why is it useful?
It adds an offset term K to the standard power law, allowing the function to asymptote to constants at both low and high shot counts, providing a more accurate fit across all n.
How does the double scaling law extend the bounded power law for in‑context learning?
It introduces model size N as an additional variable, with separate exponents for shots (α_n) and size (α_N), capturing how performance scales jointly with both factors.
In the independent HarmBench replication, what was the ASR of vanilla MSJ at 128 shots on Claude 2.0?
Vanilla MSJ achieved an ASR of about 31% on Claude 2.0 in the HarmBench benchmark.
What effect does composing MSJ with other jailbreaks have on its ASR in the HarmBench replication?
Composing MSJ with other jailbreaks increased ASR to around 40%, demonstrating that hybrid attacks can further improve success rates.
Which mitigation strategy most effectively increases the intercept of the power law for harmful responses without changing the slope?
Both supervised fine‑tuning and reinforcement learning on data containing benign MSJ prompts raise the intercept, but neither reduces the slope.
What is the main limitation of applying MSJ to chat platforms like ChatGPT or Claude.ai without modifications?
These platforms do not allow insertion of long faux dialogue histories required for vanilla MSJ, so the attack cannot be directly applied without additional “bells and whistles.”
How does the number of shots affect the rate of insulting responses on Claude 2.0 according to Figure 6R?
The rate increases steadily over a span of up to 205 shots with no obvious sign of diminishing returns, indicating that longer contexts continue to enhance jailbreak success.
What is the primary conclusion about the relationship between long context windows and new attack surfaces for LLMs?

Longer context windows enable attacks like MSJ that scale with many demonstrations, revealing a rich new vulnerability space where harmful behavior can be induced through predictable power‑law scaling.
What is the main theoretical claim about jailbreaking presented in the paper?

The paper claims that, under reasonable statistical assumptions, jailbreaking of preference‑aligned large language models (LLMs) is unavoidable because the model’s output distribution cannot be concentrated entirely within a safe set after alignment.
Which datasets are used to evaluate the E‑RLHF objective against existing RLHF methods?
The evaluation uses the HarmBench and AdvBench attack suites for safety, and the MT‑Bench benchmark for measuring helpfulness. Attack success rates (ASR) on these datasets are reported in Table 1.
What is the definition of a “harmful concept” in the framework?

A harmful concept \(c\) is one whose ground‑truth distribution over explanations assigns probability at least \(1-\eta\) to harmful explanations, i.e., \(\sum_{e\in E_h(c)} p_{\text{world}}(e|q,c)\ge 1-\eta\).
How does the paper model a language model’s output?

The model is treated as a mapping \(p_{\text{LM}}:P\to \Delta(E)\) that, given a prompt \((q,c)\), outputs a probability distribution over explanations (lengthier text fragments) rather than single tokens.
What assumption links the concept to the support of the ground‑truth distribution?

Assumption 2.1 states that for any plausible prompts sharing the same concept \(c\), the support of the ground‑truth distribution is identical: \(\text{supp}(p_{\text{world}}(q,c))=\text{supp}(p_{\text{world}}(q^*,c))\).
What does Theorem 1 provide regarding pretraining?

Theorem 1 gives a non‑vacuous PAC‑Bayesian generalization bound that shows a well‑trained LLM will mimic harmful behaviour present in its training corpus, implying a high probability of generating harmful explanations for direct prompts.
What is the “ε‑bounded adversary” introduced in Section 4?

An ε‑bounded adversary can perturb the query part of a prompt so that the resulting output distribution moves at most ε (in a chosen distance metric such as ℓ₁, TV, or JSD) toward the harmful zone on the probability simplex.
How is the “safety zone” defined in the paper’s geometric framework?

For a harmful concept \(c\), the safety zone \(H_s\) consists of all output distributions where the total probability mass assigned to harmful explanations is below a threshold \(p\); formally, \(\sum_{e\in E_h(c)} p_{\text{LM}}(e|q,c)<p\).
What modification does E‑RLHF make to the standard RLHF objective?

E‑RLHF replaces the KL regularizer term that pulls the aligned model toward the pretrained SFT distribution with a KL term that pulls it toward a safety‑transformed prior \(p_{\text{ref}}(\cdot|x_s)\), where \(x_s\) is a safe version of a harmful prompt.
What empirical effect does E‑RLHF have on attack success rates?

Across all HarmBench and AdvBench adversaries, E‑RLHF reduces the average ASR to 36.95 % and 20.89 %, respectively, outperforming standard DPO and RLHF without sacrificing helpfulness (MT‑Bench scores remain above 6.7).
What is the computational cost of adding the E‑RLHF modification?

The modification adds no extra training steps or hyperparameters; it only changes the KL target distribution during policy optimization, so the overall compute and memory footprint are identical to standard RLHF.
How does the paper justify that harmful explanations persist after alignment?

Because the SFT model already contains harmful explanations in its support, and the RLHF objective’s KL regularizer encourages staying close to SFT, the aligned policy inherits these harmful responses unless the prior is altered.
What role do “safe prefixes” play in E‑RLHF?

Safe prefixes are appended to harmful prompts during alignment to generate a safety‑transformed prompt \(x_s\) whose reference distribution contains only safe explanations, thereby expanding the safety zone of the aligned model.
Which RLHF variant is used as the baseline for comparison with E‑RLHF?
The paper uses Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a recent implementation of RLHF that avoids explicit reward modeling and directly optimizes preferences from pairwise comparisons.
What assumptions are made about the generative distribution over prompts \(D_P\)?
It is assumed that \(\text{supp}(D_P)\subsetneq \text{dom}(p_{\text{world}})\), meaning some plausible prompts never appear in the training corpus but are still semantically meaningful.
How does Theorem 2 quantify jailbreaking probability?

The theorem bounds the probability that an ε‑bounded adversary finds a jailbreak prompt by \(\Phi(a\epsilon)\), where \(a\) depends on the sizes of harmful and safe explanation sets, and \(\Phi\) is the standard Gaussian CDF.
What hyperparameter controls the trade‑off between safety and helpfulness in E‑RLHF?

The temperature \(T\) used during decoding influences how sharply the policy concentrates on high‑reward explanations; lower temperatures increase safety but may reduce diversity, while higher temperatures improve helpfulness at potential cost to safety.
What is the impact of the size ratio \(|E_h(c)|/|E_s(c)|\) on jailbreaking likelihood?
A larger ratio enlarges the harmful zone relative to the safety zone, increasing the probability that an adversary can find a prompt that pushes the output distribution into the harmful region.
Which attack families are included in the HarmBench suite used for evaluation?
HarmBench includes Direct Request, GCG, GBDA, AP, SFS, ZS, PAIR, TAP, AutoDAN, PAP‑top5, and Human jailbreak templates.
What is the main limitation of the paper’s theoretical framework identified in the conclusion?

The framework relies on several strong assumptions (e.g., deterministic mapping from concept to support, bounded adversary capability) that may not hold fully in practice, limiting generalizability to more complex prompt structures or dynamic decoding strategies.
How does the paper measure “helpfulness” of the models?

Helpfulness is evaluated using the MT‑Bench benchmark, which scores model responses on a set of instruction‑following tasks; higher scores indicate better alignment with human preferences for useful answers.
What evidence supports that E‑RLHF does not degrade helpfulness?

The MT‑Bench scores for E‑DPO (6.7) are comparable to or slightly lower than the DPO baseline (6.9), indicating that expanding the safety zone does not significantly harm the model’s ability to provide useful responses.
What is the role of the “output simplex” in analyzing jailbreaking?

The output simplex represents all possible probability distributions over explanations for a given prompt; partitioning it into harmful and safe zones allows geometric reasoning about how far an adversary must move the distribution to succeed.
Which hyperparameters are kept fixed during experiments to ensure fair comparison?

Decoding is performed with greedy decoding (temperature = 0), and all other training hyperparameters follow the default settings of the open‑source RLHF codebase, ensuring that differences in ASR stem from the objective modification alone.
What future work does the paper suggest regarding E‑RLHF?

The authors propose exploring individualized safety transformations for each harmful prompt, integrating E‑RLHF with other alignment methods, and investigating its applicability to multi‑step or conversational jailbreak scenarios.
What is the size of the new dataset collected for studying new waves of online hate?
The dataset contains 31,549 tweets related to three recent new waves of online hate.
Which three events were used to define the new waves examined in the study?
The 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine, the 2021 US Capitol insurrection, and the COVID-19 pandemic.
What tool was used to collect tweets during the study period?
The official X (formerly Twitter) Streaming API v2 was employed for data collection.
How many tweets were initially collected before filtering non‑English content and retweets?
507 million tweets from 38 million users were initially collected, later reduced to 31,549 after filtering.
Which four categories of COVID-19–related online hate were specifically analyzed?
Anti‑Asian, Ageism, Mask, and Vaccine.
What algorithm was used to detect change points in the temporal usage of hateful hashtags?
The pruned exact linear time (PELT) algorithm identified change points in hashtag frequency over time.
Which baseline moderation tools were evaluated against new waves of online hate?
Clarifai Text Moderation, Perspective API, Azure Text Moderation, and IBM Toxic Comment Classifier.
What was the highest F1‑score achieved by any baseline tool on new wave data?
The highest reported F1‑score among baselines was 0.38 from Perspective API.
Which large language model (LLM) was used to run HateCoT prompts in experiments?
GPT‑4 accessed via the official OpenAI API endpoints served as the LLM for HateCoT prompting.
What key reasoning components are included in a HateCoT prompt?
The prompt breaks down hate detection into five sub‑problems: presence of target, derogation, direction, incitation, and final decision.
How does HATEGUARD update its prompts when a new wave emerges?
It automatically extracts new targets and derogatory terms from seed tweets, verifies novelty using WordNet, and updates the HateCoT prompt template accordingly.
What is the computational cost of running a single HateCoT prompt on GPT‑4 for one tweet?
The process requires five sequential calls to the LLM, each generating a short text response; overall latency is typically under a few seconds per tweet.
In which quarter did HATEGUARD achieve the largest reduction in new wave violations compared to baselines?
During Q3 (July‑September) of 2020 for Anti‑Asian hate, where peaks were significantly lowered by HATEGUARD.
What precision and recall values did HATEGUARD attain on the “Mask” category during Q2 (April‑June)?
Precision was 0.99 and recall was 0.96 for Mask‑related hate in that quarter.
How many seed tweets are needed to trigger an update of HateCoT prompts for a new wave?
As few as 10–20 tweets from the first month of a quarter can suffice to identify new targets and terms.
Which baseline model achieved the highest accuracy on Asian hate in Q1 (January‑March)?
Tweet‑NLP reached an accuracy of 0.84 for Asian hate during that period.
What is the average number of new target–term pairs discovered by the NLP method during the Mask‑hate buildup phase?
The method expanded from one target and two terms to 39 targets/terms by the end of the buildup phase.
How does HATEGUARD handle non‑textual modalities according to its future work plans?
Future extensions aim to incorporate multimodal scenarios, such as images and memes, into the framework.
What ethical safeguards were implemented during the annotation of hateful content on Amazon Mechanical Turk?
Workers received warnings about potential hateful material, only those with a 90% approval rating and 1,000 approved HITs participated, and highly offensive words were censored in shared samples.
Which metric did HATEGUARD outperform all baselines by the largest margin on the Russian‑Ukraine wave?
HATEGUARD achieved an F1‑score of 0.95 versus 0.25–0.30 for baselines, a difference exceeding 70 percentage points.
What is the role of KeyBERT in the HATEGUARD pipeline?
KeyBERT extracts candidate targets and derogatory terms from seed tweets before novelty verification with WordNet.
How many new wave samples were randomly selected for the “in‑the‑wild” evaluation, and what was the recall achieved?
1,500 unlabeled tweets were sampled; HATEGUARD achieved a recall of 0.99 on these samples.
Which component of the chain-of-thought process directly assesses whether derogatory terms are directed at identified targets?
The “Direction” sub‑problem (questions Q3a and Q3b) evaluates if derogatory words target the specified identities or individuals.
What is the reported Fleiss Kappa agreement between expert annotators after the third annotation round?
The experts achieved a Fleiss Kappa of 0.84, indicating near‑perfect agreement.
Which new wave category experienced the most gradual decline phase according to temporal hashtag analysis?
Anti‑Asian hate showed a more prolonged and gradual decline compared to Ageism, Vaccine, and Russian invasion categories.
What is the primary goal of the MODX framework?
The primary goal of MODX is to jailbreak text‑to‑image models by using modifiers in prompts so that the models generate NSFW images while bypassing built‑in safety filters.
Which two attack scenarios are defined for MODX?
Scenario #1 focuses on converting benign subjects into NSFW genres (NSFW genre transfer), and Scenario #2 involves generating NSFW images with inherently sensitive subjects (NSFW content generation).
What are the two types of built‑in filters in diffusion‑based text‑to‑image models?
The first is a pre‑filter that detects sensitive text before denoising, and the second is a post‑filter that evaluates intermediate and final images for NSFW tendencies.
How does MODX bypass the pre‑filter when the subject contains sensitive text?
MODX substitutes sensitive words with semantically similar safe descriptions using GPT‑4o, then verifies similarity with SentenceBERT to ensure semantic consistency before proceeding.
What is Constraint #1 in the MODX jailbreak prompt construction process?
Constraint #1 requires that the embedding of a selected modifier be highly similar to the embedding of the target NSFW category, ensuring the modifier can steer generation toward that category.
How many categories are used to taxonomize modifiers in the Malicious Modifier Dataset (MMD)?
Five categories are used: artist, medium, movement, flavor, and trending.
From which sources were modifiers collected for the MMD?
Modifiers were collected from art‑related websites (DeviantArt, Saatchi Art, Dark Art Movement), PromptHero’s NSFW prompts, and CLIP Interrogator analysis of 600 NSFW images from Reddit and 4chan.
What is the average BPR achieved by MODX in Scenario #1 across four models?
The average BPR in Scenario #1 is approximately 0.92.
Which model exhibited the highest ASR for explicit content under Scenario #2 when using MODX?
Midjourney v6.1 achieved the highest ASR of about 0.93 for explicit content in Scenario #2.
What theoretical property ensures that adding a modifier to a prompt causes only a bounded change in the text encoder’s output?
The Lipschitz continuity of the text encoder guarantees that small changes in the prompt lead to bounded changes in its embedding.
How does MODX measure semantic similarity between the jailbreak prompt and the original malicious prompt?
MODX uses SentenceBERT to compute cosine similarity between embeddings of the jailbreak prompt and the original prompt, selecting prompts with high similarity under Constraint #2.
What metric is used to evaluate the toxicity level of generated NSFW images?
Toxicity is measured by scaled logits from a multi‑headed safety classifier, where scores above 50 indicate NSFW content and 100 represents maximum risk.
In the experimental setup, how many images are generated per prompt for each model?
Four images are generated per prompt for each model to mitigate bias and provide sufficient samples for evaluation.
What is the impact of increasing the hyperparameter p (number of top modifiers per category) on execution time?
Increasing p exponentially raises execution time; for example, moving from p=2 to p=5 increases time from about 0.1 seconds to over 110 seconds.
Which single modifier category alone achieved the highest ASR in Scenario #1 for explicit content?
The artist category alone achieved an ASR of approximately 0.53 in Scenario #1 for explicit content.
How does MODX compare to SneakyPrompt, Hateful Meme Attack, and SurrogatePrompt in terms of average toxicity for explicit images?
MODX achieves a mean toxicity of about 62.34 with lower standard deviation than the other methods, indicating more consistent high‑toxicity outputs.
What is the role of the post‑filter’s limitation regarding artistic genres?

The post‑filter does not block certain artistic genres (e.g., dark art, horror posters), allowing modifiers that steer images into these styles to bypass detection while still containing NSFW content.
Which models were included in the scalability evaluation beyond the primary four models?
Additional models tested for scalability include Craiyon v4, FLUX v1, DALL·E 2, Imagen 2, Midjourney v4, and Stable Diffusion XL.
What mitigation strategy involves specifying elements that should not appear in generated images?
Using negative prompts (NP) to suppress NSFW features by listing high‑frequency modifiers from the MMD as negative terms during generation.
How does MODX ensure that the final jailbreak prompt remains semantically close to the original malicious intent?
By enforcing Constraint #2, which requires the embedding of the full jailbreak prompt to be similar to the embedding of the original prompt, verified via SentenceBERT similarity scores.
What is the average ASR achieved by MODX for gore images in Scenario #1 across all models?
The average ASR for gore in Scenario #1 is roughly 0.79.
Which theoretical theorem guarantees that the difference between modified and original denoised outputs is bounded?
Theorem 2 states that the output difference is bounded by a function of the Lipschitz constants, the number of diffusion steps, and the embedding shift magnitude.
What dataset size does the Malicious Modifier Dataset (MMD) contain after deduplication?
After removing duplicates, MMD contains 82 medium modifiers, 116 movement modifiers, 267 flavor modifiers, 157 artist modifiers, and 95 trending modifiers.
How many jailbreak prompts are generated before applying Constraint #2 when p=3 and k=10 in the default setup?
With p=3, five categories produce 3^5 = 243 potential combinations; selecting the top 10 under Constraint #2 yields 10 final jailbreak prompts.
What is the main advantage of MODX’s reusability across different subjects and NSFW categories?

Once a set of effective modifiers for an NSFW category is identified, they can be combined with any subject or substituted prompt, enabling rapid generation of diverse NSFW images without retraining or extensive new prompt design.
Which datasets were used in the experiments described in the study?
The experiments employed two datasets: QuestionList, containing 100 queries on prohibited scenarios, and AdvBench, which includes 520 instances across seven harmful behavior categories.
What target models were evaluated for jailbreak success rates?
Three open‑source LLMs were used as targets: LLaMA‑2 (Llama-2‑7b-chat-hf), LLaMA‑3.1 (Meta‑Llama‑3.1‑8BInstruct), and Qwen‑2 (Qwen2‑7B‑Instruct).
How many turns were set for the multi‑turn dialogues in the preliminary study?
The dialogues consisted of five turns, with queries gradually increasing in toxicity from benign to harmful.
What metric is used to measure stealthiness of adversarial prompts?
Sentence Perplexity (PPL) calculated by GPT‑2 is employed as a proxy for prompt stealthiness.
Which genetic algorithm parameters were fixed during ASJA optimization?
The population size was set to 10, the maximum number of iterations to 5, and both uniform crossover probability and mutation probability to 0.5.
What is the primary difference between ASR‑1 and ASR‑2 evaluation criteria?
ASR‑1 filters responses for harmful keywords only, while ASR‑2 additionally checks that the response remains relevant to the original query.
Which eight jailbreak strategies are used for query mutation in ASJA?
The strategies are Defined Persona, Imagined Scenario, Research and Testing, Joking Pretext, Program Execution, Text Continuation, Opposite Model, and Alternate Model.
How does ASJA shift the LLM’s attention during a multi‑turn dialogue?

By fabricating dialogue history that contains affirmative responses to harmful queries in later turns, thereby increasing the model’s focus on historical responses rather than the final harmful query.
What statistical test confirms the significance of attention differences between successful and failed jailbreaks?
Welch's t‑test was used, yielding a p‑value of 4.38e‑07, indicating a statistically significant difference.
Which model served as the judge for evaluating jailbreak outcomes in the experiments?
LLaMA‑3.1‑70b was used as the judge model to assess whether jailbreak attempts succeeded.
What is the average improvement in ASR‑1 achieved by ASJA compared to the best baseline?
ASJA improves ASR‑1 by 44.91% on average over the best baseline, PAIR.
How many queries does ASJA theoretically require per target model during optimization?
With a population size of 10 and five iterations, ASJA requires at most 50 queries to the target model in total.
In what way does the “Do Anything Now” (DAN) persona influence ASJA’s initialization?

DAN embeds harmful queries and their affirmative responses into the dialogue history, leading the LLM to believe it has already complied with such requests, which facilitates subsequent jailbreak success.
Why do intermediate turns receive higher attention than final query turns in successful jailbreaks?
The model focuses more on previous responses to inform its current output, and shifting attention away from harmful keywords in the final turn reduces detection of malicious intent.
What is the role of the uncensored LLaMA‑3.1‑8b model in ASJA?
It generates initial multi‑turn queries and produces affirmative responses during mutation, providing unaligned content that aids in fabricating dialogue history.
How does ASJA’s fitness function evaluate a dialogue sample?
Fitness is based on the attention score of tokens in the final query turn; lower scores indicate more successful attention shifting toward historical context.
Which evaluation metric indicates how well an attack preserves naturalness of prompts?
Sentence Perplexity (PPL) reflects prompt naturalness, with lower values indicating higher stealthiness.
What is the observed transferability performance of ASJA on GPT‑4o compared to other attacks?
ASJA achieves a 57% ASR‑1 and 63% ASR‑2 on GPT‑4o, ranking highest among the evaluated attacks for that model.
How many layers and attention heads does LLaMA‑2 have, as used in the preliminary study?
LLaMA‑2 contains 32 layers and 32 attention heads.
What is the purpose of uniform crossover in ASJA’s genetic algorithm?
Uniform crossover mixes dialogue units from parent samples to produce diverse offspring while preserving beneficial traits for attention shifting.
Which baseline attack uses a genetic algorithm to optimize jailbreak prompt generation?
AutoDAN employs a genetic algorithm to generate and refine jailbreak prompts.
What is the average PPL achieved by ASJA on Qwen‑2 compared to PAIR?
Both ASJA and PAIR achieve low average PPL values, with ASJA slightly lower, indicating better stealthiness.
How many samples were successful in jailbreaking LLaMA‑2 during the preliminary study?
Out of 100 multi‑turn queries tested on LLaMA‑2, 22 samples successfully jailbreaked the model.
What is the main limitation of single‑turn jailbreaks highlighted by the authors?
Single‑turn jailbreaks have limited effectiveness in detecting vulnerabilities because they fail to exploit the model’s attention shift toward historical context present in multi‑turn dialogues.
Which metric shows that ASJA requires fewer queries than PAIR for a comparable ASR‑1 on AdvBench?
Efficiency analysis demonstrates that ASJA achieves higher ASR‑1 across all query budgets, indicating it reaches similar success rates with fewer target model queries.
What datasets were used to evaluate OCR‑APT?

OCR‑APT was evaluated on three provenance graph datasets: DARPA Transparent Computing Engagement 3 (TC3), DARPA Operationally Transparent Cyber (OpTC), and a simulated NODLINK dataset comprising Ubuntu, Windows Server 2012, and Windows 10 hosts.
Which metric is primarily used to assess detection performance in OCR‑APT?
The F1‑score is the primary evaluation metric because malicious nodes constitute less than 0.01% of all nodes, making precision and recall critical for highly imbalanced data.
What is the average number of malicious nodes per host in the DARPA TC3 dataset?
In TC3, malicious nodes range from about 12,800 on CADETS TRACE to 25,300 on THEIA, representing a small fraction of total nodes.
How many RGCN layers does OCRGCN use during training?
OCRGCN employs three Relational Graph Convolutional Network (RGCN) layers to aggregate neighbor information and produce node embeddings.
What embedding dimension is used for node representations in OCR‑APT?
The node embeddings are 32‑dimensional, balancing expressiveness with computational efficiency.
Which one‑class SVM parameter controls the contamination factor in OCRGCN?
The contamination factor, denoted as γ, is set between 0.001 and 0.05 to reflect the expected proportion of anomalies while preventing overfitting.
How does OCR‑APT avoid reliance on fragile node attributes during anomaly detection?
OCR‑APT excludes attributes such as file paths or IP addresses from the GNN input, instead using structural edge types and behavioral features like action frequencies and idle period statistics.
What is the maximum number of edges allowed in a subgraph constructed by OCR‑APT?
Subgraphs are limited to 5,000 edges during construction to keep them manageable for subsequent LLM processing.
Which LLM model was used as the primary language model in OCR‑APT’s investigation pipeline?
GPT‑4o‑mini with a temperature of 0.0 was used to ensure deterministic and accurate generation of attack reports.
What prompting strategy does OCR‑APT employ to reduce hallucinations during report generation?
OCR‑APT uses a multi‑stage Chain‑of‑Thought (CoT) prompting pipeline that separates IOC extraction, stage mapping, and context summarization into distinct validated subtasks.
How many stages of an APT kill‑chain does the LLM investigator aim to map in its reports?

The investigator maps all standard MITRE ATT&CK stages: Initial Compromise, Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Command & Control, Internal Reconnaissance, Lateral Movement, Data Exfiltration, and Covering Tracks.
What is the average detection time for OCRGCN on the Ubuntu host in the NODLINK dataset?
Detection takes approximately 828 seconds, which includes training and inference of the GNN models.
Which component of OCR‑APT contributes most to reducing false positives?
The subgraph anomaly detection module filters out low‑abnormality subgraphs, significantly lowering false positives while maintaining high recall.
How does OCR‑APT handle multiple attacks that share system entities within a single subgraph?
OCR‑APT may merge such attacks into one subgraph; this can obscure boundaries but also captures shared infrastructure. Future work aims to segment subgraphs based on behavioral signatures.
What is the precision of OCR‑APT on the OpTC 501 host when using the stricter evaluation metric that ignores neighbor nodes?
Under the stricter metric, OCR‑APT achieves a precision of 1.00 and an F1‑score of 1.00 on host 501.
Which baseline system achieved the highest recall on the NODLINK Ubuntu host before ablation studies?
CONAD achieved a recall of 1.0 but with low precision, resulting in an overall F1‑score of 0.51.
What is the role of the “llm-as-a-judge” component in OCR‑APT’s pipeline?
The judge LLM selects the most critical indicators of compromise from the comprehensive report and retrieves contextual subgraphs to enrich the final narrative.
How many SPARQL queries are executed during anomalous subgraph construction?
Three SPARQL queries are used: one for direct connections between anomalous nodes, and two for their one‑hop neighbors.
Which embedding model is chosen for indexing log documents in the vector store?
The “textembedding‑3‑large” model from OpenAI is selected due to its superior performance on textual similarity tasks.
What memory footprint does OCR‑APT require during inference on a typical enterprise host graph?
Inference typically occupies around 10 GB of RAM, making it feasible for deployment in resource‑constrained environments.
Which feature set is most critical for distinguishing benign from malicious process nodes in OCR‑APT?
Idle period statistics—minimum, maximum, and average durations between actions—are highly discriminative because APT processes often remain idle longer than normal processes.
How many subgraph anomaly detection models are trained per node type in OCR‑APT?
OCRGCN trains a separate model for each node type to capture distinct behavioral patterns across process, file, and network entities.
What is the impact of using two‑hop expansion instead of one‑hop during subgraph construction on precision?
Two‑hop expansion reduces false negatives but increases false positives dramatically, dropping precision from 1.0 to about 0.5 in some hosts.
Which open‑source LLM achieved comparable performance to GPT‑4o‑mini when paired with a suitable embedding model?
LLaMA3‑8B combined with the “granite‑embedding‑125m‑english” model matched ChatGPT’s detection of APT stages on most hosts.
What is the overall F1‑score achieved by OCR‑APT on the DARPA TC3 CADETS host?
OCR‑APT achieves an F1‑score of 1.00, indicating perfect precision and recall for that host under the evaluation protocol.
What is the main objective of the COLD-Attack framework?

The main objective of COLD‑Attack is to automatically generate stealthy and controllable jailbreak prompts for large language models by formulating the attack as a controllable text generation problem and optimizing it with energy‑based Langevin dynamics.
Which LLMs were evaluated in the experiments on the continuation constraint setting?
The evaluated LLMs were Vicuna‑7B‑v1.5, Guanaco‑7B‑HF, Mistral‑7B‑Instruct‑v0.2, and Llama‑2‑7B‑Chat‑hf.
What is the Attack Success Rate (ASR) achieved by COLD-Attack on Vicuna under the continuation constraint?
COLD‑Attack achieves an ASR of 100 % on Vicuna for the continuation constraint setting.
How does COLD-Attack differ from GCG in terms of search strategy?

Unlike GCG, which performs greedy token‑level optimization and requires a large batch size, COLD‑Attack samples continuous logits via Langevin dynamics and then decodes them non‑autoregressively, eliminating the need for greedy search.
What energy function is used to enforce attack success in COLD-Attack?
The attack success energy is defined as E_att(y; z) = –log p_LM(z | y), encouraging the LLM to produce a target harmful response z when prompted with y.
Which metric is used to measure the fluency of generated adversarial prompts?
Perplexity (PPL) calculated with Vicuna‑7B is used as the primary fluency metric for the generated prompts.
What role does the λ_att hyperparameter play in COLD-Attack?
λ_att weights the attack success energy term in the composite energy function; higher values prioritize achieving a successful jailbreak over other constraints.
In the paraphrasing constraint setting, how is semantic similarity enforced?
Semantic similarity is enforced using E_sim(y) = –cos(emb(y), emb(x)), where emb(.) averages token embeddings to compare the generated attack y with the original query x.
Which defense method was found to be the strongest against COLD-Attack in the experiments?
Llama Guard proved to be the most effective defense, achieving the lowest ASR values across tested models when applied to COLD‑Attack prompts.
What is the average running time per sample for COLD-Attack on a single NVIDIA V100 GPU?
COLD‑Attack requires approximately 16–27 minutes per sample depending on the model, which is roughly ten times faster than GCG and GCG‑reg.
How many samples are drawn from the optimized distribution in each COLD-Attack run for the continuation constraint?
Eight samples of length 20 tokens are generated, and a GPT‑4 prompt selects one as the final adversarial prompt.
What additional constraints can be combined with the attack success energy in COLD-Attack?
Constraints such as fluency (E_flu), semantic similarity (E_sim), lexical inclusion or exclusion (E_lex), and sentiment steering are added via weighted sums in the composite energy function.
Which dataset is used to evaluate the attacks in the paper?
A subset of 50 instructions from AdvBench, covering diverse harmful topics while minimizing duplicates, is used for evaluation.
What is the ASR-G metric and why is it introduced?
ASR‑G is a GPT‑4 based assessment that checks whether a response truly fulfills a malicious instruction, providing higher correlation with human judgments than simple substring matching.
How does COLD-Attack handle position constraints in attacks?
It inserts an adversarial sequence y between the user query x and a separate prompt p, optimizing for fluency of x ⊕ y ⊕ p while ensuring the combined text triggers harmful outputs.
What is the impact of system prompts on COLD-Attack’s performance?
Including system prompts generally reduces ASR for Mistral and Llama‑2, indicating that system instructions can mitigate attack effectiveness when COLD‑Attack optimizes multiple energy terms.
Which models were tested for transferability to GPT‑3.5 turbo?
Transferability experiments on GPT‑3.5 turbo evaluated Vicuna, Guanaco, Mistral, and Llama‑2 using the same attack prompts generated from open‑source models.
What is the role of the Langevin dynamics step size η in COLD-Attack?
The step size η controls how far logits move during each iteration; a larger η can accelerate convergence but may overshoot, while a smaller η ensures finer updates.
How does COLD-Attack achieve lower perplexity than AutoDAN‑Zhu on Vicuna?

By optimizing fluency energy directly in the continuous logit space and using guided decoding, COLD‑Attack produces prompts whose token distributions align closely with the LLM’s own predictions, yielding lower PPL.
What is the purpose of the ngram_match function in lexical constraints?
The ngram_match function approximates BLEU‑n to penalize or reward the presence of specific keywords or phrases within the generated attack, enabling precise control over lexical content.
In which setting does COLD-Attack outperform AutoDAN‑Zhu on ASR-G for Guanaco?
On the paraphrasing constraint setting with sentiment steering, COLD‑Attack achieves an ASR‑G of 84 % versus AutoDAN‑Zhu’s lower score.
What is the typical number of iterations N used in Langevin dynamics for COLD-Attack?
The experiments use N = 2000 iterations with a decreasing noise schedule to explore the logit space before converging to a low‑energy sample.
How does COLD-Attack’s decoding process differ from standard greedy decoding?
After Langevin sampling, COLD‑Attack performs LLM‑guided top‑k selection for each token position and then greedily picks tokens within that set, ensuring fluency while respecting the sampled logits.
Which evaluation metric indicates that generated attacks are coherent with the original prompt?
The BERTS score measures whether a generated suffix is predicted as the next sentence of the user query; high scores (e.g., 94 % for Guanaco) confirm coherence.
What evidence shows COLD-Attack’s efficiency advantage over GCG in terms of computational cost?
Table 4 reports per‑sample runtimes, showing that COLD‑Attack completes a single attack on Vicuna in 16.43 minutes versus 156.93 minutes for GCG, confirming a roughly ten‑fold speedup.
What is the primary objective of an LLM-as-a-Judge?

An LLM-as-a-Judge selects the best matching response from a set of candidate responses to a question, aiming to evaluate text quality or make decisions in applications like search filtering and tool selection.
Which datasets were expanded for evaluating JudgeDeceiver?
MT-Bench and LLMBar were expanded into new evaluation sets, each containing 10 target questions, 10 target responses, and 500 clean responses.
What are the three loss components used in JudgeDeceiver’s optimization objective?
The loss function includes a target-aligned generation loss, a target-enhancement loss, and an adversarial perplexity loss.
How does the target-aligned generation loss influence the injected sequence?
It maximizes the likelihood that the LLM generates the attacker‑desired output (e.g., “Output (index) is better”) when the injected sequence is present.
What role does the target-enhancement loss play in the attack?
It focuses on the positional index token of the target response, ensuring the attack remains effective regardless of where the target appears in the candidate list.
Why is an adversarial perplexity loss added to JudgeDeceiver’s objective?
To reduce the log‑perplexity of the injected sequence, making it harder for perplexity‑based defenses to detect the injection.
What hyperparameters control the weighting of the three loss terms in JudgeDeceiver?
The coefficients α (alpha) and β (beta) balance the target-aligned generation loss, target-enhancement loss, and adversarial perplexity loss within the total loss function.
How many iterations are performed during the gradient‑descent optimization of an injected sequence?
Each attack runs for 600 iterations unless a successful injection is achieved earlier.
What is the average attack success rate (ASR) achieved by JudgeDeceiver on Mistral-7B with MT-Bench?
The average ASR is 90.8% across the evaluated question‑response pairs.
How does positional attack consistency (PAC) differ from ASR in evaluation metrics?
PAC measures the percentage of times the LLM still selects the injected target after swapping its position with another response, indicating robustness to positional changes.
Which manual prompt injection method achieved the highest ASR on Llama-2‑7B according to Table 2?
The Fake Reasoning attack achieved the highest manual ASR of 40.2% on Llama-2‑7B.
What is the average length of injected sequences optimized by JudgeDeceiver?
Injected sequences are optimized as suffixes of 20 tokens in length.
How many shadow candidate response sets (M) does JudgeDeceiver use during optimization?
The framework uses three shadow candidate response sets (m = 3) for each target question‑response pair.
In the context of LLM-powered search, what is the lowest recorded ASR when evaluating five candidate entries?
The lowest ASR observed was 80% for QE-4 when n = 5.
What detection method fails to identify most target responses with JudgeDeceiver’s injected sequences on Mistral‑7B?
Known-answer detection has a false negative rate of 100% on MT-Bench and 90% on LLMBar, failing to detect the attacks.
How does PPL-W detection perform compared to standard PPL detection against JudgeDeceiver’s attacks?
PPL-W detection achieves lower false negative rates (e.g., 40% vs. 50% on MT-Bench) but still misses a large fraction of target responses.
What is the impact of increasing α beyond 5 on ASR and PAC?
Increasing α above 5 reduces both ASR and PAC, with ASR dropping to around 82% when α = 10.
Which injected sequence initialization type yields the highest ASR in JudgeDeceiver’s experiments?
The “Word” initialization type achieves an ASR of 97% and a PAC of 94%.
How many LLMs were evaluated for transferability of injected sequences from Llama-2‑7B or Llama-3‑8B?
Transferability was tested on eleven models, including Vicuna‑7B/13B, Llama‑2‑13B/70B, Llama‑3‑70B, Mistral‑Large, Claude‑3 variants, GPT‑3.5‑Turbo, and GPT‑4.
What is the average ASR achieved by JudgeDeceiver on LLMBar with Openchat‑3.5?
The average ASR on LLMBar with Openchat‑3.5 is 93.2%.
Which jailbreak attack had the highest ASR when extended to JudgeDeceiver’s setting on MT-Bench?
AutoDAN achieved the highest ASR of 53.6% among the four jailbreak attacks.
What is the computational cost in terms of GPU memory when increasing shadow responses m from 4 to 5 during optimization?
Increasing m from 4 to 5 increases GPU memory consumption proportionally, leading to a higher resource requirement for the gradient‑descent process.
How many candidate responses are included in each evaluation set after expanding MT-Bench and LLMBar?
Each expanded evaluation set contains 500 clean responses plus one target response per question.
What is the effect of removing the target-enhancement loss from JudgeDeceiver’s total loss function?
Removing the target‑enhancement loss reduces ASR to approximately 84%, indicating its importance for positional robustness.
In which application scenario did JudgeDeceiver achieve a perfect ASR of 100% across all evaluated tools?
In tool selection, JudgeDeceiver achieved a 100% ASR when evaluating three candidate tools (n = 3).
What is the primary goal of PARDEN in defending against jailbreak attacks?
PARDEN aims to detect and mitigate jailbreaks by having a language model repeat its own output, using the similarity between the original and repeated text as an indicator of harmful content.
Which two large language models were evaluated with PARDEN according to the paper?
The evaluation was performed on Llama‑2‑7B and Claude‑2.1.
What metric is used by PARDEN to decide whether a repeated output matches the original?
BLEU score between the original output and its repetition is calculated; a high BLEU indicates benign content, while a low BLEU suggests malicious content.
How does PARDEN avoid the auto‑regressive trap that many jailbreaks exploit?
By re‑processing the entire LLM output at once for repetition, PARDEN prevents the model from committing to harmful tokens early in generation.
What is the reported False Positive Rate (FPR) of PARDEN on Llama‑2‑7B when the True Positive Rate (TPR) is set to 90%?
The FPR drops to approximately 2.0%, a reduction from 24.8% in baseline methods.
Which dataset construction technique was used to increase the success rate of jailbreak prompts for evaluation?
Prompt injection was employed, inserting tokens after the user‑assistant separator to prime the model and improve attack success.
What is the purpose of including in‑context learning examples in PARDEN’s repeat prompt?
They help the model understand the repetition task by providing concrete instances of successful repeats, improving classification accuracy especially for short repetitions.
How does PARDEN handle very long LLM outputs to reduce computational cost?
It allows partial repeats, stopping after a fixed number of tokens (n), which keeps forward passes constant regardless of output length.
In the experimental setup, what temperature setting is used when prompting the model for repetition and why?
Temperature 0 is used to ensure deterministic repetition; stochastic sampling would add noise that could degrade BLEU scores.
What baseline method does PARDEN compare against in the ROC analysis?
It compares against a binary classification defense proposed by Helbling et al. and a perplexity filter from Jain et al.
How is the threshold \(t\) for the BLEU score interpreted in terms of classifier behavior?
A higher threshold makes the system more conservative, increasing true positives but also raising false positives; it controls the trade‑off between TPR and FPR.
What is the average BLEU score observed for benign outputs after repetition according to Figure 3?
The BLEU scores cluster around 1.0, indicating near perfect preservation of benign content.
Which models were found unsuitable as defenders because they are not safety‑aligned, and why?
Mistral‑7B was deemed unsuitable because it outputs harmful content whenever prompted, violating the assumption that the defender model is aligned.
What advantage does PARDEN have over perplexity filters in terms of input space exposure?
PARDEN operates on the output space, so it never sees adversarial inputs directly, reducing the risk of being tricked by crafted prompts.
How many benign instructions were sampled from open‑instruct‑v1 for dataset creation?
552 benign instructions were sampled and used to generate outputs with Llama2 and Claude‑2.1.
What is the reported Area Under Curve (AUC) improvement of PARDEN over the baseline classifier on the aggregated dataset?
The AUC increased from 0.922 to approximately 0.958, indicating better overall discrimination between harmful and benign outputs.
According to Table 3, how does removing in‑context learning examples affect BLEU scores for a repetition length of 5 tokens?
Without ICL, the BLEU score drops from about 0.9441 to 0.7566, showing significant performance loss.
In what scenario did PARDEN fail to reduce false positives compared to the baseline?
When Mistral‑7B was used as the defender model, PARDEN could not separate benign and harmful distributions, leading to high FPR similar to the baseline.
What is the computational cost implication of generating a full repetition versus a partial one with \(n=60\) tokens?
Generating up to 60 tokens requires only 60 forward passes regardless of output length, whereas a full repetition would require as many passes as the original output length.
How does PARDEN’s approach differ from “defence by direct generation” methods mentioned in the related work section?
Defence by direct generation alters the prompt or output directly to produce safe text, while PARDEN only evaluates the model’s own repetition of its output without modifying it.
What is the mean BLEU score for malicious outputs after repetition as reported in Figure 3?
The mean BLEU score for malicious examples is around 0.435 with a standard deviation of about 0.157.
Which hyper‑parameter controls the trade‑off between computational efficiency and classification performance when using partial repeats?
The number of tokens \(n\) to repeat; increasing \(n\) improves AUC up to a point but also increases computation.
How does PARDEN handle cases where the repeated output is truncated due to token limits?
It clips both the original and repeated outputs to the same length before computing BLEU, ensuring fair comparison.
What evidence suggests that PARDEN can generalize across different datasets and models?
Experiments on Mistral‑7B, Llama, and Claude with harmful behaviours and harmful strings show consistent performance gains when the defender model is safety‑aligned.
According to the paper, what future work could further improve the detection of malicious intent that does not manifest in the output?
Combining PARDEN’s output filtering with input‑space filters or extending PARDEN to repeat both the prompt and the response could help detect harmful intentions hidden in the user query.
What is the main goal of a prompt leaking attack on an LLM application?
The primary objective is to steal the system prompt that an LLM application uses, thereby exposing intellectual property and compromising the developer’s confidential instructions.
Which two types of LLM applications are evaluated in PLeak's experiments?
Offline LLM applications built with benchmark datasets and popular backend LLMs, and real‑world LLM applications hosted on Poe with known system prompts but unknown backends.
How many real‑world LLM applications on Poe were used to test PLeak?
Fifty real‑world LLM applications from Poe were selected for evaluation.
What is the definition of an adversarial query (AQ) in the context of PLeak?
An AQ is a specially crafted user query that, when sent to a target LLM application, induces the backend LLM to output its system prompt or a transformed version of it.
Describe the incremental search technique used by PLeak.
Incremental search optimizes an AQ in stages, first targeting short prefixes of shadow system prompts and gradually increasing the length, thereby reducing local optima and improving convergence.
What role does the gradient-based method play in generating an AQ?

The gradient-based approach estimates how changing each token in an AQ affects the loss function, guiding the selection of tokens that minimize the difference between the LLM’s output and shadow system prompts.
How many adversarial queries are typically generated during Phase 1 of PLeak?
Four adversarial queries are usually optimized in Phase 1 before being used for target reconstruction.
What is the purpose of the post‑processing function \(P\) in PLeak’s framework?
Post‑processing aggregates responses from multiple AQs, reverses any applied transformations, and extracts common text segments to reconstruct the target system prompt.
Which decoding strategies are evaluated for their impact on PLeak’s success?
Beam‑search, sampling, and beam‑sample decoding strategies are examined for how they affect attack metrics.
In which metric does PLeak achieve a score of 1.00 against the GPT‑J model on the Financial dataset?
The Exact Match (EM) accuracy reaches 1.00 when attacking GPT‑J with PLeak on the Financial dataset.
What is the average SM accuracy achieved by PLeak across all five datasets and five LLM backbones in offline experiments?
PLeak achieves an average Sub‑string Match (SM) accuracy of approximately 0.823 across those settings.
Which baseline attack method shows the lowest EM accuracy when applied to real‑world Poe applications?
The manually‑crafted prompt from Perez et al. yields the lowest EM accuracy, often below 0.02 on real‑world applications.
How does increasing the shadow dataset size affect PLeak’s EM and SM scores?
Expanding the shadow dataset from two to eight samples raises EM from about 0.401 to 0.728 and SM from roughly 0.641 to 0.992, improving attack performance.
What effect does extending the AQ length from 8 to 18 tokens have on PLeak’s EED metric?
Longer AQs reduce the Extended Edit Distance (EED) value, indicating that the reconstructed prompt is closer to the true system prompt.
Which initialization mode for AQs yields the highest EM accuracy in the experiments?
Random initialization consistently outperforms Human and Mixed modes, achieving near‑perfect EM scores in most cases.
What happens to PLeak’s performance when a step size of 200 is used instead of 50 during incremental search?
Larger step sizes degrade EM and SM accuracy while increasing EED, showing that finer granularity improves generalization.
How does PLeak handle potential filter‑based defenses that remove exact sentences from responses?
By applying an adversarial transformation (e.g., adding a prefix), PLeak can evade sentence‑level filters and still recover the system prompt with EM around 0.30.
In transferability experiments, which LLM model’s AQs exhibit the strongest cross‑model performance?
AQs generated on LLaMA‑2 transfer best to other models, achieving SM scores above 0.9 when applied to GPT‑J, OPT, and Falcon.
What is the impact of adding quotes as a defense against prompt leaking attacks?
Quoting system prompts slightly reduces baseline attack performance but has minimal effect on PLeak, which still achieves high EM accuracy.
Which decoding strategy yields the highest SM accuracy for PLeak when attacking LLaMA‑2 on the Roles dataset?
