Beam‑search decoding provides the best SM accuracy (around 0.971) compared to sampling and beam‑sample.
How many tokens are typically used in the AQ length parameter for offline experiments on the SQuAD2 dataset?
The default AQ length is set to 16 tokens for SQuAD2 during offline evaluations.
What metric indicates that a reconstructed prompt is semantically close to the true system prompt?
Semantic Similarity (SS) measures cosine similarity between embeddings; higher SS values mean closer semantic alignment.
Which real‑world Poe application achieved an EM accuracy of 0.68 when attacked with multiple AQs by PLeak?
Across the evaluated Poe applications, a subset reached 0.68 EM accuracy using multiple adversarial queries from PLeak.
What is the role of the transformation function \(T_{\text{adv}}\) in PLeak’s attack pipeline?

\(T_{\text{adv}}\) modifies the AQ (e.g., adding prefixes or reversing word order) so that any defense filtering exact system prompts cannot detect the transformed prompt, while the attacker can invert it during post‑processing.
Which metric value indicates a perfect reconstruction of the target system prompt in PLeak’s evaluation?
An Exact Match (EM) accuracy of 1.00 signifies that the reconstructed prompt exactly matches the true system prompt, excluding punctuation.
What is the main objective of POISONPROMPT?
POISONPROMPT aims to inject a backdoor into both hard and soft prompt-based large language models, enabling malicious activation of target outputs when specific triggers are present in input queries.
Which three large language models were evaluated with POISONPROMPT?
The experiments used BERT (bert-large-cased), RoBERTa (RoBERTa-large), and LLaMA (LLaMA-7b).
How many datasets were employed to assess the attack’s effectiveness?
Six benchmark datasets—SST2, IMDb, AG News, QQP, QNLI, and MNLI—were used.
What prompt learning methods were tested in the study?
AUTOPROMPT for hard prompts, Prompt‑Tuning, and P‑Tuning v2 for soft prompts were evaluated.
Describe the bi‑level optimization framework used by POISONPROMPT.
The lower level optimizes prompt tuning on clean data to maintain task performance, while the upper level trains a backdoor trigger that causes the model to output target tokens when triggered.
How is the poison prompt set constructed from the training data?
Five percent of the training instances are randomly selected as the poison set; the remaining ninety‑five percent form the clean set used for normal prompt tuning.
What role does the trigger placeholder \(x_{\text{trigger}}\) play during optimization?
It represents the token sequence to be optimized so that, when inserted into queries, it steers the model toward producing predefined target tokens.
How are candidate triggers identified in the upper‑level optimization?

Gradients of the backdoor loss with respect to trigger embeddings are computed and multiplied by input word embeddings; the top‑k resulting token candidates form a pool from which the best trigger is chosen using ASR.
What metric is used to select the optimal trigger among candidates?
Attack Success Rate (ASR) on a held‑out test set measures how often poisoned queries produce target tokens, guiding trigger selection.
What are the typical values of the prompt token count for hard prompts in the experiments?
Hard prompts were fixed to four trainable tokens (\(m = 4\)) across all tasks.
How does POISONPROMPT’s ASR compare between soft and hard prompts?
Soft prompts (Prompt‑Tuning, P‑Tuning v2) generally achieve higher ASRs than hard prompts; for example, AUTOPROMPT on MNLI with LLaMA reaches 93.20% while Prompt‑Tuning and P‑Tuning attain 100%.
What is the average accuracy drop observed when using backdoored prompts?
The accuracy decline is modest, staying below ten percent across all models and datasets.
Which dataset showed the highest ASR for hard prompts with LLaMA?
MNLI yielded an ASR of 93.20% for AUTOPROMPT on LLaMA.
What hardware configuration was used to run the experiments?
The experiments were conducted on Ubuntu 20.04 with a 96‑core Intel CPU and four Nvidia GeForce RTX A6000 GPUs.
How many epochs are used in the lower‑level prompt tuning phase of Algorithm 1?
The lower‑level optimization runs for \(E_2\) fine‑tune steps, where \(E_2\) is a hyperparameter set by the experimenter (specific value not disclosed).
Explain how target tokens \(V_t\) are selected during poison prompt generation.
Top‑k candidates from the language model head’s softmax over the [MASK] position are retrieved, excluding any token already present in the clean label set.
What is the purpose of maintaining a low entropy prompt during backdoor injection?
Low‑entropy prompts contain few trainable tokens, making it harder to embed a backdoor; POISONPROMPT addresses this by jointly optimizing triggers and prompt tokens.
Describe the impact of trigger size on model accuracy.
Increasing trigger length slightly reduces accuracy, but the drop remains small while ASR stays near 100% for both hard and soft prompts.
Which prompt learning approach achieved the highest ACC on IMDb with RoBERTa?
Prompt‑Tuning attained an ACC of 91.10 on IMDb with RoBERTa.
What loss functions are used in the lower‑level optimization for soft prompts?
The cross‑entropy loss over the [MASK] token’s predicted distribution is minimized across both clean and poisoned samples.
How does POISONPROMPT handle discrete trigger optimization given the continuous nature of embeddings?
It first selects a set of candidate tokens via gradient‑based scoring, then evaluates each candidate using ASR to pick the best discrete trigger.
In what way does POISONPROMPT preserve downstream task performance during backdoor training?
By jointly optimizing prompt tuning loss and backdoor loss in a bi‑level framework, it balances maintaining high ACC while embedding the backdoor.
What is the reported ASR for hard prompts on the QQP dataset with LLaMA?
Hard prompts achieved an ASR of 92.30% on QQP with LLaMA.
Which prompt learning method showed the smallest accuracy degradation when backdoored on AG News with BERT?
P‑Tuning v2 exhibited a minimal drop, maintaining high ACC while still achieving 100% ASR.
Summarize the overall security implication highlighted by the authors regarding prompt-based LLMs.

The study demonstrates that prompt-based large language models are vulnerable to backdoor attacks that can be inserted with negligible impact on normal performance, underscoring the need for robust defenses in prompt marketplaces and services.
What is the main goal of ProAttacker?

ProAttacker aims to generate semantically consistent adversarial perturbations for text by guiding a masked language model with label-related prompts, thereby improving attack success rates and reducing perturbation rates.
Which datasets were used to evaluate ProAttacker?
The evaluation covered six NLP datasets: AG News, Yelp, SST‑2, DBpedia for classification, and MNLI, QNLI for natural language inference.
What victim models were attacked in the experiments?
Fine‑tuned BERT models provided by the TextAttack library served as the victim classifiers for all six datasets.
How many examples were used per dataset during evaluation?
Each dataset was evaluated on a set of 1,000 test instances that are publicly available from prior work.
Which masked language model did ProAttacker primarily use?
The lightweight Albert MLM was employed as the primary language model for generating perturbation candidates.
What three attack baselines were compared against ProAttacker?
PWWS, TextFooler, and CLARE were used as baseline black‑box adversarial attacks for comparison.
How does ProAttacker construct a corrupted input version set?
It creates a corrupted set by applying two mask deployment strategies—Replace (R) and Insert (I)—across all token positions in the original sentence.
What is the purpose of adding a label word to the prompt template?
The label word aligns the masked language model’s generation with the target class, encouraging semantically consistent perturbations that are class‑aware.
How does ProAttacker identify vulnerable positions for perturbation?
It computes an importance score for each mask position based on the probability difference between the perturbed and original sentences, then sorts positions by this score.
What similarity metric is used to filter candidate perturbations?
The Universal Sentence Encoder cosine similarity between the original and perturbed sentence is used; candidates below a threshold are discarded to avoid antonyms or irrelevant words.
Which metric measures how many words were changed in an adversarial example?
Perturbed Rate (PR) calculates the proportion of altered tokens relative to the input length.
In Table 2, which attack achieved the highest ASR on Yelp?
ProAttacker achieved the highest Attack Success Rate of 47.40% on the Yelp dataset.
How does ProAttacker’s average query number compare to CLARE on AG News?
ProAttacker required an average of 1,985 queries, slightly higher than CLARE’s 2,178 but lower than PWWS and TextFooler.
What effect did removing the prompt template have in the ablation study?
Removing the template decreased ASR to 77.54% and increased PR to 10.06%, indicating a drop in attack effectiveness.
How many antonyms or irrelevant words were filtered out when using prompts, according to Table 3?
The Antonym/Irrelevant rate dropped from 48.18% without prompts to 40.25% with prompts.
Which label word pair yielded the best performance on SST‑2 in the prompt engineering study?
The “good/bad” label words produced the highest ASR (83.69%) and lowest PR (10.04%).
What is the role of the Universal Sentence Encoder in ProAttacker’s pipeline?
USE provides sentence similarity scores to filter out perturbation candidates that would alter the semantics too much.
Which metric reflects the fluency of adversarial sentences?
Perplexity (PPL) measures how fluent or natural the perturbed sentences are, with lower values indicating better fluency.
What is the computational cost implication mentioned for context‑aware perturbation methods?
They require querying the masked language model for each perturbation, leading to higher average query numbers compared to simpler synonym replacement attacks.
How many prompt templates were evaluated in the study?
Five different prompt templates were tested on SST‑2 to assess their impact on attack performance.
What is the significance of the “w/o Prompt” ablation result?
Removing the prompt entirely lowered ASR to 76.05% and increased PR, showing that prompts are essential for guiding effective perturbations.
Which dataset showed the largest improvement in ASR when using ProAttacker over CLARE?
On MNLI, ProAttacker achieved an ASR of 80.39%, surpassing CLARE’s 74.81% by a notable margin.
What threshold was set for USE similarity filtering during ablation studies?
A cosine similarity threshold of 0.8 was used to filter candidate perturbations.
How does ProAttacker handle the changing context after each perturbation?
It recalculates the best perturbation word for the current context but assumes vulnerability scores remain valid throughout the attack process.
What is the main contribution claimed by the authors regarding prompt usage?

The authors claim that prompts can serve as a general semantic enhancer, enabling masked language models to generate more class‑aware and semantically consistent perturbations for adversarial attacks.
What is the total number of penetration testing targets included in the benchmark created by the authors?
The benchmark contains 13 penetration testing targets.
How many sub‑tasks are defined across all benchmark targets?
There are 182 sub‑tasks distributed among the 13 targets.
Which two popular platforms were used to source the benchmark machines?
HackTheBox and VulnHub provided the target machines for the benchmark.
What OWASP list is fully represented in the benchmark’s vulnerability coverage?
The benchmark covers all vulnerabilities listed in OWASP’s Top 10 project.
Which Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE) categories are included in the benchmark?
Eighteen distinct CWE items appear across the benchmark sub‑tasks.
What human expertise level was required for the testers executing LLM instructions during evaluation?
The testers were Offensive Security Certified Professionals (OSCPs).
Which three large language models were evaluated in the exploratory study?
GPT‑3.5, GPT‑4, and Bard were used for evaluation.
What token limits do GPT‑3.5 and GPT‑4 have during the experiments?
GPT‑3.5 uses an 8k token limit while GPT‑4 operates with a 32k token limit.
How many easy difficulty targets did GPT‑4 successfully complete in the benchmark?
GPT‑4 completed four easy targets and one medium target.
What is the overall sub‑task completion rate improvement of PENTESTGPT over naive GPT‑3.5 on the benchmark?
PENTESTGPT increases sub‑task completion by 111% compared to naive GPT‑3.5.
Which module in PENTESTGPT maintains a high‑level overview of the testing status?
The Reasoning Module keeps track of the overall penetration testing status.
What data structure does the Reasoning Module use to represent task progress?
It uses a Pentesting Task Tree (PTT), an attributed tree format.
How many modules compose PENTESTGPT and what are their primary roles?
Three modules: Reasoning (strategy planning), Generation (command generation), Parsing (output summarization).
What is the average cost per target when PENTESTGPT solved HackTheBox active machines?
The average cost was approximately 21.9 USD per target.
How many HackTheBox challenges did PENTESTGPT solve in the practical study?
It solved four easy and one medium challenge, totaling five successful completions.
What is the total score achieved by PENTESTGPT in the picoMini CTF competition?
PENTESTGPT accumulated 1400 points out of a possible 4200.
In which category did PENTESTGPT achieve the highest number of completions during picoMini?
The web category saw five completions, the most among all categories.
What is the primary cause of failure identified for LLMs in penetration testing trials?
Loss of session context was the leading failure reason across models.
Which unnecessary operation prompted by LLMs appears most frequently according to the study?
Brute‑force attempts were the most common unnecessary operation suggested.
How does PENTESTGPT handle tool output that is too verbose for direct feeding into an LLM?
The Parsing Module condenses and highlights essential information before passing it to other modules.
What technique does PENTESTGPT use to reduce hallucination in the Reasoning Module’s outputs?
It verifies updated Pentesting Task Trees, ensuring only leaf nodes are altered.
Which LLM model version was used for the practical evaluation of PENTESTGPT on real‑world targets?
GPT‑4 with a 32k token limit API was employed in the practical tests.
What is the total number of GitHub stars received by the open‑source implementation of PENTESTGPT within twelve months?
The project garnered over 6,500 stars on GitHub.
During the ablation study, which module’s removal caused the greatest drop in sub‑task completion?
Removing the Reasoning Module led to the lowest success rate among the variants.
What ethical safeguard is mentioned to prevent misuse of PENTESTGPT for malicious purposes?
The system includes monitoring modules and promotes ethical guidelines to restrict inappropriate use.
What is the primary objective of a promotion attack in preference poisoning?
A promotion attack aims to increase the learned reward model’s value for a set of target outcomes so that these targets are preferred over other options when compared by the reward function.
Which dataset was used for safety alignment experiments with LLaMA-7B?
The PKU-SafeRLHF-30k dataset, containing 30k question-answer pairs annotated for helpfulness and harmlessness, was used; only harmlessness labels were employed.
How many pairwise comparison datasets are generated from Amazon rating data in the recommendation system experiments?
Approximately 4,500 pairwise preference samples were created by comparing ratings of comment pairs, using BERT embeddings as input features.
What is the maximum number of label flips allowed per attack under the threat model?
The attacker can flip up to a budget B labels, where B is expressed as a percentage (e.g., 1%–10%) of the total dataset size.
Which neural network architecture was used for MuJoCo reward models?
A feedforward three‑layer MLP with ReLU activations and a mid‑layer size of 32 neurons was employed for MuJoCo environments.
What technique does the projected gradient ascent attack use to handle discrete label variables?
It relaxes the discrete labels to continuous values in [–1, +1], performs gradient updates, then projects back by selecting the B largest‑magnitude entries and rounding them to –1, 0, or +1.
In what way does the rank‑by‑distance (RBD) heuristic determine which preference pairs to flip?
RBD ranks pairs based on a distance metric between the target outcome and the less preferred outcome in each pair, then flips labels for the B pairs with smallest distances.
Which distance variant performed best in Atari vision‑based control experiments?
The PCA‑reduced dimensionality approach combined with gradient‑based attacks achieved the highest success rates, outperforming other RBD variants.
What is the role of the implicit function theorem in the gradient‑based attack?

It provides a way to compute the derivative of learned parameters θ with respect to label perturbations δ by relating it to the inverse Hessian of the loss, enabling back‑propagation through the training process.
How does the “stealth” metric evaluate an attack’s detectability?
Stealth is measured by the change in test accuracy; attacks are considered stealthy if they do not cause a significant drop (e.g., less than 10%) in overall preference prediction accuracy.
Which defense technique proved most effective against recommendation system attacks?

The ALIBI algorithm, which incorporates differential privacy and Bayesian post‑processing, reduced attack success from 100% to about 60–40% for promotion and demotion respectively at a 10% poisoning budget.
What is the theoretical guarantee provided by Lemma 4.1 regarding VC dimension?
Lemma 4.1 states that for a vector space of real‑valued functions, the class of thresholded functions has VC dimension bounded above by the function space dimension plus one.
In MuJoCo Hopper experiments, what success rate did the best gradient‑based attack achieve with a 10% budget?
Approximately 90% success rate was achieved when flipping 10% of labels in Hopper.
Which attack class consistently required fewer computational resources in high‑dimensional settings?
The rank‑by‑distance (RBD) heuristics, especially those using simple distance metrics like Euclidean norm or reward difference, were faster and more scalable than gradient‑based methods.
What is the impact of partial knowledge of the training dataset on attack success in MuJoCo?
Attack success rates decline gradually when only a subset of the data is known to the attacker; however, even with limited visibility, attacks remain effective.
How does the reward model learning objective relate to the Bradley‑Terry (BT) distribution?

The BT model defines the probability that one outcome is preferred over another based on their latent rewards; the loss function used for MLE maximizes the likelihood of observed pairwise preferences under this stochastic model.
Which defense approach failed to reduce attack efficacy in LLM safety alignment experiments?
All evaluated defenses, including spectral anomaly detection, Meta‑Sift, loss outlier removal, and ALIBI, had negligible effect on attack success when only 0.3% of labels were poisoned.
What is the main challenge that prevents direct use of gradient‑based poisoning methods for preference data?
Preference labels are discrete (three possible values), making the objective discontinuous and non‑differentiable, which conflicts with standard continuous optimization techniques.
How many environments were used to evaluate Atari vision‑based control attacks?
Three Atari games—Pong, Breakout, and Qbert—were tested for both promotion and demotion attacks.
What is the effect of training a reward model on clean data before attacking with RBD-Embedding?
Using an embedding learned from a clean reward model provides a meaningful distance metric that improves attack performance compared to random or PCA‑based embeddings in some domains.
Which hyperparameter controls how many high‑loss points are removed in the loss outlier defense?
The multiplier α, set to 1.5 in experiments, determines that 1.5 × B highest‑loss datapoints are discarded before retraining.
In the recommendation system with a linear reward model, how much budget is needed to reach 100% attack success?
About 10% of the dataset must be poisoned to achieve full success against a linear reward model, twice as much as required for neural network models.
What is the primary difference between promotion and demotion attacks in terms of optimization objective?
Promotion maximizes the fraction of times target outcomes win over others, while demotion minimizes that same fraction; both use similar bi‑level formulations but with opposite signs.
Which attack variant performed best for low‑dimensional MuJoCo control tasks when budget was small (1–5%)?
The gradient‑based method outperformed RBD heuristics at these budgets, achieving higher success rates across Reacher, Hopper, and Walker environments.
How does the paper justify using a finite‑sample approximation for the attack objective?
A theoretical analysis shows that with polynomial sample complexity, solving the finite‑sample proxy yields solutions within ε of the true bi‑level optimization with high probability.
What is the primary goal of the Prompt-Crossing framework?

The Prompt-Crossing framework aims to detect whether a user prompt has jailbreak intent by analyzing the likelihood that an LLM response originates from known normal or jailbreak prompts, even when the response itself contains no harmful content.
Which two open‑source models were used as judge LLMs in the experiments?
LLAMA-27B and Falcon-7B served as the judge LLMs for evaluating Prompt-Crossing.
On which benchmark subset was Prompt-Crossing evaluated?
The framework was evaluated on the “harmful behaviors” subset of the AdvBench jailbreak benchmark.
What template is used to format inputs for the victim LLM during inference?
Inputs to the victim LLM follow the template: “{user prompt} {user question}”.
How many normal and jailbreak checker prompts are included in the set C?
The checker prompt set contains five normal prompts and five jailbreak prompts.
What metric is reported as “Accuracy” in Table I for each model?
Accuracy measures the proportion of correctly classified user prompts (normal or jailbreak) across all test cases.
Which judge LLM achieved a higher overall accuracy on AdvBench?
Falcon-7B (Ours v2) achieved an overall accuracy of 0.746, surpassing LLAMA-27B’s 0.666.
How does Prompt-Crossing compute the probability that a response comes from normal versus jailbreak prompts?

It calculates cross‑entropy loss for each checker prompt combined with the user question and response, then averages likelihoods over all normal or jailbreak checker prompts to estimate the relative probability.
What is the role of the “Jailbreak Checker” component in the system framework?

The Jailbreak Checker uses the set of checker prompts, the victim LLM’s response, and the user question to judge whether the prompt has jailbreak intent by comparing likelihoods from normal and jailbreak prompt sets.
Which baseline models were compared against Prompt-Crossing in the experiments?
GPT‑3.5 and GPT‑4 served as baselines for comparison with Prompt‑Crossing.
What cross‑entropy loss pattern was observed when user prompts matched the type of checker prompts?

Lower cross‑entropy losses were seen when a normal user prompt paired with a normal checker prompt or a jailbreak user prompt paired with a jailbreak checker prompt, indicating higher likelihood consistency.
Which jailbreak checker prompt produced the highest accuracy for detecting jailbreak intent in the “Jailbreak Avg.” category?
The AIM checker prompt achieved 1.0 accuracy on the “Jailbreak Avg.” category when using LLAMA as judge LLM.
What is the main limitation of lexical and semantic approaches to jailbreak detection mentioned in the introduction?

Lexical methods rely only on the first sentence of a response, missing harmful content that appears later; semantic methods require additional toxicity‑detection training and may be hindered by embedded safety filters that suppress toxic content.
How does Prompt-Crossing differ from conventional inference-based LLM usage?
Instead of generating new responses over long sequences, Prompt-Crossing performs a forward‑step likelihood calculation on known prompt sets to filter potential jailbreak intent quickly.
What is the average accuracy achieved by GPT‑4 on the “RAW” user prompt category?
GPT‑4’s average accuracy for RAW prompts was 0.30.
Which dataset provides the highest ranked jailbreak prompts used as checker prompts?
The top five jailbreak prompts were sourced from the website www.jailbreakchat.com (or its Hugging Face repository).
What is the function of the template “{checker prompt} {user question} Answer: {response}” in Prompt-Crossing?
This template feeds the judge LLM with a checker prompt, user question, and the victim response to compute likelihoods for normal versus jailbreak scenarios.
How many total prompts (normal + jailbreak) are evaluated in the heatmap of cross‑entropy loss?
The heatmap displays 10 checker prompts across rows and columns, representing all combinations of 5 normal and 5 jailbreak prompts.
What computational metric is used to quantify the probability distribution of an LLM response?
Cross‑entropy loss is employed to measure how well a prompt predicts the victim LLM’s response.
Which user prompt category achieved the highest accuracy with Falcon-7B as judge LLM?
The “PLUS” user prompt category reached 0.84 accuracy when using Falcon-7B.
In what way does Prompt-Crossing enable detection of jailbreak intent even if the response is non‑harmful?
By evaluating whether the response’s likelihood aligns more closely with known jailbreak prompts, it infers jailbreak intent from the prompt itself regardless of harmful content in the output.
What are the two main contributions highlighted in the conclusion of Prompt-Crossing?
First, a method to detect jailbreak intent via probability analysis of responses; second, empirical evidence that this approach outperforms GPT‑4 on AdvBench while scaling across different LLMs.
How many normal prompts were used as part of the checker prompt set in the experiments?
Five normal prompts comprised the normal portion of the checker prompt set.
What is the reported accuracy for the “DEV” user prompt category when using LLAMA as judge LLM?
The accuracy for DEV with LLAMA was 0.90.
Which component calculates whether a response is more likely to come from normal or jailbreak prompts?
The Jailbreak Checker performs this calculation by comparing likelihoods derived from the checker prompt sets.
What is the main privacy concern addressed by the paper?
The paper investigates how intermediate outputs exchanged in distributed large language model (LLM) inference frameworks can leak private input prompts, potentially exposing sensitive user data.
Which distributed LLM framework is used as an example in the introduction?
The Petals framework is cited as a representative example that splits LLM layers between client and server modules for collaborative inference.
What type of LLM architecture do the authors focus on for their attacks?
The attacks target decoder‑only transformer architectures such as GPT‑2, Phi‑3.5, and Llama‑3.2, which are primarily used for next‑token prediction tasks.
How many adversary scenarios are defined in the attack model?
Three adversaries (A1, A2, A3) are defined, each with different query budgets and auxiliary data availability constraints.
What is the key observation about sequence embeddings that enables the first attack?

Intermediate sequence embeddings at a given layer cluster primarily by their ending token, with minimal influence from preceding tokens, allowing a classifier to predict the last token of each embedding.
Which machine learning model is used as the attacker’s classifier in A1 and A2?
A multi‑layer perceptron (MLP) with six layers, layer normalization, and ReLU activation is trained to map embeddings to ending tokens.
How does the second adversary (A2) handle mismatched auxiliary data distributions?

A2 synthesizes synthetic embeddings for unseen tokens by generating random token sequences that end with target tokens, then augments the training set with these synthetic pairs to improve generalization.
What is the purpose of the nearest‑neighbor phase in the third adversary (A3)?
It assigns token labels to target embeddings based on proximity to anchor points (synthetic embeddings) within each token cluster, using a confidence score derived from entropy over distances.
Which metric is used to evaluate semantic similarity between reconstructed and ground‑truth prompts?
Cosine Semantic Similarity (CSS) is computed by extracting sentence embeddings with Sentence‑BERT and measuring cosine similarity.
What reconstruction accuracy does A1 achieve on Llama‑3.2 across all layers?
A1 consistently attains token reconstruction accuracies above 90% for every evaluated layer of Llama‑3.2.
How does the reconstruction accuracy change with increasing layer depth in A1?
Accuracy decreases as layer number increases because embedding clusters become more overlapping, reducing separability.
What is the estimated query cost difference between A1 and A2 for a typical attack scenario?
A2 incurs roughly 30 times higher query costs than A1 due to the additional synthetic data generation required for augmentation.
In which layer does A3 achieve its highest reconstruction accuracy under the minimum budget?
Layer 2 yields the highest accuracy (≈ 88.8%) when using a single‑token budget in A3.
What defense mechanism is evaluated against the attacks, and what is its effect on utility?
Split‑NDenoise (SnD) adds differential privacy noise to token embeddings; it drastically reduces attack success but also degrades LLM output quality, lowering BERTScore below 0.5.
Which dataset contains manually crafted sensitive strings with PII for evaluating attacks?
The PrivatePrompts dataset comprises 251,270 prompts containing personal identifiers such as names and dates.
How many layers does the Phi‑3.5 model have, according to Table 3?
Phi‑3.5 consists of 32 decoder layers.
What is the primary difference between encoder‑only and decoder‑only LLMs in terms of embedding generation?
Encoder‑only models generate embeddings conditioned on both preceding and following tokens, while decoder‑only models condition only on preceding tokens.
Which baseline methods are compared against A2 in Table 4?
The baselines include B-SEI, Vec2Text, and Output2Prompt, all of which target embedding or output inversion but differ in approach.
What is the role of beam search in the third phase of A3?
Beam search fills in missing tokens by generating candidate sequences constrained to top‑5 nearest‑neighbor candidates, guided by a shadow LLM’s probability outputs.
How many epochs does the MLP classifier train for during A1 and A2 attacks?
The classifier is trained for six epochs in both A1 and A2 scenarios.
Which metric indicates that an attack reconstruction is considered useful, according to the paper?
Token Reconstruction Accuracy (RA) above 40% is deemed sufficient for plausible reconstruction of key concepts.
What is the total variation distance between WikiText‑2 and SQuAD 2.0 used in the experiments?
The TV distance is approximately 0.4998, indicating moderate distribution overlap.
Which layer index corresponds to the last linear layer before token prediction in the LLMs studied?
Layer L (the total number of layers plus one) represents the final linear layer preceding next‑token prediction.
What is the primary advantage of the proposed attacks over transformer‑based inversion models?
They are lightweight, do not require training a large transformer model, and achieve high accuracy independent of sequence length or error propagation.
Which hardware platform was used to run the experiments described in the paper?
Experiments were conducted on a server with an AMD EPYC 7742 64‑core CPU, four NVIDIA A100‑SXM4‑40GB GPUs, and 256 GB RAM running Ubuntu 22.04.
What is the main purpose of R 2‑Guard?
R 2‑Guard aims to provide a robust, flexible guardrail for large language models by combining data‑driven category predictions with explicit logical reasoning over safety knowledge.
Which two types of probabilistic graphical models are used in R 2‑Guard?
Markov logic networks (MLNs) and probabilistic circuits (PCs) are employed to encode safety rules and perform inference.
How does the category‑specific learning component of R 2‑Guard operate?
It computes unsafety probabilities for each predefined safety category (e.g., self‑harm, sexual) using transformer‑based classifiers trained on annotated data.
What is a direct rule in the context of R 2‑Guard’s knowledge base?
A direct rule states that a specific safety category implies the overall unsafe label, such as “self‑harm ⇒ unsafe”.
What does an indirect rule represent in R 2‑Guard’s logical framework?
An indirect rule captures dependencies among categories, for example “self‑harm/instructions ⇒ self‑harm”.
How is the factor function of a possible world defined in the MLN formulation?
It multiplies a data‑driven likelihood term (based on category probabilities) with an exponential sum of satisfied rule weights.
What computational complexity does MLN reasoning have for n logical variables?
The complexity is O(2ⁿ), making it impractical for large numbers of categories.
How does PC reasoning reduce inference time compared to MLNs?
PCs use a layered, clustered graph structure that yields a sum‑exponential complexity of O(∑₂|Cᵢ|) instead of 2ⁿ, achieving about 6% of the MLN runtime.
What is the purpose of spectral clustering in PC construction?
Spectral clustering partitions category variables into clusters that are less correlated, allowing each cluster to be processed in a separate PC layer for efficient inference.
In pseudo‑learning, how are training samples generated?
Samples are simulated by uniformly sampling unsafety scores per category and rejecting those violating internal implications with a threshold of 0.5.
What loss function is used to optimize knowledge weights in both learning methods?
Binary cross‑entropy (BCE) loss between R 2‑Guard predictions and the target labels is minimized.
Which safety benchmark introduced by the authors contains paragraph‑level, phrase‑level, and word‑level challenges?
TwinSafety is a new benchmark created via pairwise construction to test guardrails on intent‑hiding, mismatched context, proverbs, double‑entendre, media usage, science purpose, and virtual context.
How does R 2‑Guard perform against the GCG‑U1 universal jailbreak compared to other models?
R 2‑Guard maintains a 100% unsafety detection rate (UDR) for GCG‑U1, outperforming all baselines which drop below 80%.
What is the average AUPRC of R 2‑Guard (PC) across the six datasets evaluated?
The average AUPRC is 0.869, matching the MLN variant.
Which guardrail model achieves the lowest runtime per instance among those tested?
ToxicChat‑T5 has the fastest runtime at 0.06 seconds per instance.
How does R 2‑Guard adapt to new safety categories without retraining?
By adding a new category‑specific learning module and inserting corresponding nodes and rules into the PGM reasoning graph.
What is the observed correlation between learned rule weights and category correlations?
A Pearson coefficient of 0.801 indicates that higher inter‑category correlations lead to larger learned rule weights.
In what way does R 2‑Guard’s PC inference achieve a precision‑efficiency balance?
By optimizing the graph structure—clustering variables and layering inference—to maintain high moderation accuracy while reducing computation time.
Which jailbreak attack type slightly reduces R 2‑Guard’s UDR from 1.0 to around 0.97?
The GCG‑R attack, optimized against a distilled Gemma‑2B model of R 2‑Guard, lowers the UDR to about 0.973.
What is the runtime difference between MLN and PC reasoning reported in the paper?
MLN reasoning averages 0.1123 seconds per instance, while PC reasoning requires only 0.0062 seconds.
Which safety category was used as an example of a phrase‑level double‑entendre in TwinSafety?
The sentence “I’d love to see your melons” directed at a grocery worker versus a girl illustrates the double‑entendre challenge.
How many guardrail models were compared against R 2‑Guard in the evaluation?
Eight strong guardrail models, including industry APIs, fine‑tuned models, LLM‑based chain‑of‑thought, and ensemble methods, were benchmarked.
What metric is primarily used to evaluate guardrail discrimination performance?
Area under the precision–recall curve (AUPRC) measures how well a model distinguishes safe from unsafe prompts.
Which dataset shows the lowest AUPRC for all models, indicating its difficulty?
TwinSafety yields the lowest AUPRC across models, highlighting its challenging nature.
What is the main limitation of R 2‑Guard mentioned in the discussion section?
The need for explicit specification and annotation of safety knowledge rules requires human effort to define categories and their interrelations.
What is the primary purpose of AI red‑teaming as described in the executive order?

The executive order defines AI red‑team­ing as a structured testing effort to find flaws and vulnerabilities in an AI system, often in a controlled environment and in collaboration with developers, aiming to assess safety, security, and trustworthiness.
Which generative AI models were included in the six case studies discussed?
The case studies evaluated Bing Chat (Microsoft), GPT‑4 (OpenAI), Gopher (DeepMind), Claude 2 and Claude 1 (Anthropic), and various models tested at DEFCON.
How many distinct threat model categories were identified in the survey of red‑teaming research?
Four threat model categories were used: dissentive, consentive, both, and neither, totaling 104 papers across all categories.
What are the three main types of team composition found in real‑world AI red‑team­ing exercises?
Teams consisted of handpicked subject‑matter experts (internal or external), crowdsourced participants from platforms or events, and language models prompted or fine‑tuned to act as red teams.
In what way does the lack of standardized reporting affect the utility of red‑teaming findings?

Without standard protocols, many studies omit detailed results, resource costs, or mitigation steps, making it difficult for stakeholders to assess relevance, replicate tests, or gauge the effectiveness of identified fixes.
What methodology category includes the use of AI models to generate test prompts that attempt to jailbreak other models?
This falls under “Brute‑force + AI,” where an AI model generates prompts or suffixes designed to elicit harmful outputs from another model.
Which red‑teaming approach is described as starting from a given prompt and iteratively modifying it until a vulnerability is found?
That approach is called “Algorithmic Search,” involving guided or random perturbations of prompts to discover issues.
How many papers in the survey employed brute‑force methods without AI assistance?
Twenty papers used pure brute‑force methods, relying on manual human evaluation of inputs and outputs.
What common resource limitation is noted for crowdsourced red‑team­ing participants?
Crowdsourced teams often face time constraints (e.g., 30–50 minutes per task) and limited compute access, typically only through APIs.
Which type of risk is defined as having a clear consensus on its harmful nature across contexts?
Consentive risk, such as the disclosure of private information or instructions for illicit activity, which is universally considered inadmissible.
What is one mitigation strategy mentioned that involves jointly training language models with red‑team models?
One strategy is to train a generative model and a red‑team model together using GAN-like techniques to reduce harmful outputs.
How does the paper describe the relationship between red‑teaming and other evaluation methods like audits or impact assessments?

The paper positions red‑teaming as one of several complementary evaluation paradigms, noting that it cannot alone guarantee safety and should be combined with audits, impact assessments, and other techniques.
What key question is highlighted in the pre‑activity phase regarding the artifact under evaluation?
A key question asks which version of the model (including fine‑tuning details) is to be evaluated and what safety guardrails are already in place for that artifact.
Which red‑teaming methodology involves using a reverse language model to work backwards from harmful text to prompts?
This technique is part of the “Brute‑force + AI” category, where reverse LMs identify prompts likely to generate harmful outputs.
According to the discussion, why might focusing primarily on dissentive risk be problematic?
Focusing mainly on dissentive risk can divert resources from consentive risks that are universally harmful and may lead to exaggerated safety responses that limit legitimate content.
What is one example of a red‑teaming activity that was conducted over several months before model release?
Two case studies mentioned ongoing red‑team­ing for 6–7 months prior to the public release of the evaluated models.
How many papers in the survey used targeted attack methods?
Twenty-eight papers employed targeted attack approaches, focusing on specific parts of an LLM or its API to induce vulnerabilities.
What does the paper suggest is needed regarding follow‑up actions after a red‑teaming exercise identifies a vulnerability?
The paper calls for clear protocols to mitigate identified issues, including detailed reporting of mitigation steps and verification that fixes are effective.
In the context of red‑team composition, what potential bias arises from using only handpicked experts?
Handpicked expert teams may introduce selection bias, potentially overlooking risks relevant to broader or marginalized user groups due to limited diversity in perspectives.
What is a common outcome reported by all case studies regarding model release decisions after red‑teaming?
None of the case studies led to a decision not to release the model; instead, they proposed risk mitigation strategies to reduce harmful behavior.
Which red‑teaming methodology involves using image prompts combined with text queries to increase harmful output likelihood in multimodal models?
This approach is part of “Brute‑force + AI,” where an image (e.g., a bomb) paired with a query like “how to build a bomb?” improves the chance of generating harmful text.
What recommendation does the paper give for improving transparency in red‑teaming reports?
The paper recommends that reports include resource consumption, success assessment against predefined goals, mitigation steps taken, and any subsequent evaluation of the artifact.
How many papers in the survey used algorithmic search techniques to evaluate text‑to‑image models?
Fourteen papers employed algorithmic search methods for evaluating text‑to‑image models by perturbing prompts until safety filters are bypassed.
What is one reason cited for why red‑teaming alone may not address all safety concerns of generative AI?
Red‑team­ing focuses on specific vulnerabilities and cannot cover issues like algorithmic monoculture, environmental impacts, or broader societal harms that require additional assessment methods.
Which policy document repeatedly mentions red‑teaming as a requirement for AI developers?

The U.S. presidential Executive Order on the Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence references red‑team­ing eight times, mandating guidelines and best practices for its implementation.
What detection rate did SCAMNET achieve on the real-world evaluation dataset?
SCAMNET achieved a detection rate of 95.59% on the real‑world evaluation set.
Which base model was fine‑tuned to create SCAMNET?
The Llama‑3‑8B‑Instruct model served as the foundation for SCAMNET.
How many explanations were included in the curated explainable dataset used for step two of fine‑tuning?
The curated dataset contained 200 detailed explanations.
What was the false positive rate (FPR) of BEYOND PHISH on the BP evaluation set?
BEYOND PHISH had an FPR of 3.71% on the BP evaluation set.
Which prompting strategy yielded a lower false positive rate for GPT‑4 in the experiments?
The two‑shot prompting approach reduced GPT‑4’s false positive rate compared to zero‑shot prompting.
What technique was employed to adapt Llama‑3‑8B‑Instruct without full‑parameter fine‑tuning?
Low‑Rank Adaptation (LoRA) was used to insert trainable adapters while keeping the base parameters frozen.
During step one of SCAMNET’s training, which layers were targeted for LoRA adaptation?
All linear layers in the model were targeted for LoRA adaptation during step one.
What learning rate was used in the second fine‑tuning phase (step two) for explainable reasoning?
The learning rate for step two was 1e-05.
Which dataset statistics show that the real‑world evaluation set contains more legitimate sites than fraudulent ones?
The real‑world evaluation set has 1923 legitimate sites and 340 fraudulent sites, indicating a higher count of legitimate sites.
How does SCAMNET’s accuracy on the BP evaluation set compare to that of Llama‑3‑8B‑Instruct?
SCAMNET achieved 99.47% accuracy, surpassing Llama‑3‑8B‑Instruct’s 73.81% accuracy on the same dataset.
What is the main advantage of using an explainable LLM for fraudulent website detection mentioned in the paper?
Explainability reduces legal risk for browser vendors by providing clear justifications for blocking decisions.
Which evaluation metric dropped dramatically for BEYOND PHISH when tested on a newer dataset collected two years later?
The true positive rate (TPR) fell from 90.10% to 22.35%.
What role does the curated explainable dataset play in SCAMNET’s training pipeline?
It teaches the model to generate coherent explanations for its classifications, enhancing transparency.
Which prompt template component contains the website’s main page textual content?
The “Content” field within the prompt template holds the main page text.
What optimizer was used during both fine‑tuning stages of SCAMNET?
AdamW was employed as the optimizer in both stages.
How many epochs were run during step one of SCAMNET’s supervised fine‑tuning?
Two epochs were executed in step one.
In the two‑shot prompting strategy, what additional information is provided to the LLM compared to zero‑shot?
Two labeled examples (one legitimate and one fraudulent) are included as demonstrations.
What was the average reliability score assigned to SCAMNET’s generated explanations by security experts?
The average score was 1.8, indicating generally good explanation quality.
Which dataset did the authors use for deduplication before training SCAMNET?
They used the BEYOND PHISH dataset and removed duplicates to create a cleaner training set.
What is the impact of increasing LoRA rank from 16 to 32 in step one on model performance?
Performance improved up to r = 16 but declined at r = 32, suggesting potential overfitting during the first round.
Which metric shows that SCAMNET has a very low rate of incorrectly flagging legitimate sites on the real‑world set?
The false positive rate (FPR) is 0.57% for SCAMNET on the real‑world evaluation set.
What was the main limitation of BEYOND PHISH highlighted in the paper regarding evolving scam tactics?
Scammers can manipulate URLs to appear legitimate, allowing BEYOND PHISH to miss such deceptive sites.
How many GPUs were used during SCAMNET’s fine‑tuning process?
Four H100 GPUs were employed for training at BF16 precision.
Which two types of data are included in the input features fed to the LLM in SCAMNET’s prompt?
URL structure, website content, external links, and WHOIS information are all part of the input features.
What is the primary research question (Q1) addressed by the authors regarding fine‑tuning?
Q1 asks how fine‑tuning helps create an LLM that can detect fraudulent shopping websites with explainable output.
What is the main goal of the SEAS framework?

The SEAS framework aims to automatically generate diverse adversarial prompts and iteratively improve both Red Team and Target models so that the Target model’s safety performance approaches that of GPT‑4 while preserving general capabilities.
Which dataset size was used for training the Red Team model in SEAS?
The Red Team model was fine‑tuned on 16,000 entries from the SEAS training set, which contains a total of 18,000 prompts split into 16K training and 2K test examples.
How many iterations does SEAS perform to reach its final safety level?
SEAS performs three full iterations of Attack and Adversarial Optimization stages before reporting that the Target model’s safety is comparable to GPT‑4.
What are the two main components of the SEAS pipeline?

The SEAS pipeline consists of an Initialization Stage where Red Team and Target models are first fine‑tuned, followed by alternating Attack and Adversarial Optimization stages that generate prompts, evaluate responses, and update both models.
Which safety classifier is used to label model outputs during SEAS training?
Meta Llama Guard 2, an 8B parameter safeguard model based on Llama‑3, is employed as the Safe Classifier to determine whether a response is safe or unsafe.
What attack success rate (ASR) does the Red Team model achieve against Llama‑3‑70B‑Instruct after three SEAS iterations?
The Red Team model reaches an ASR of 73.97 % against Llama‑3‑70B‑Instruct in the third iteration.
How many risk categories and attack styles are defined in the SEAS dataset?
The SEAS dataset defines five risk categories (e.g., Unfairness, Unsafe Instruction) and nine attack styles (e.g., Role Play, Goal Hijacking), totaling 14 distinct types.
What sampling strategy is used when generating adversarial prompts from the Red Team model?
Nucleus sampling with a top‑p of 0.9 is applied, and multiple samples (n = 5) are drawn to create diverse prompt candidates during each Attack stage.
Which hyperparameter settings are shared between SFT and DPO training in SEAS?
Both SFT and DPO use a learning rate of 5 × 10⁻⁶, batch size of 8, and run for one epoch per iteration.
What metric is used to evaluate the diversity of adversarial prompts generated by SEAS?
Cosine distance‑based similarity (B_cos) between sentence embeddings of prompts is computed; lower values indicate higher diversity relative to training or other datasets.
How does SEAS handle seed prompt randomness to ensure prompt variety?

Seed prompts are constructed by selecting three random prompts of the same type from the training set, and multiple iterations of the Red Team model generate new prompts, ensuring that diversity arises primarily from iterative updates rather than seed variability.
Which open‑source datasets were used to fine‑tune the Target model’s instruction‑following ability?
ShareGPT, Dolly, and LIMA were combined, yielding approximately 101K cleaned samples for initial Target model training.
What is the reported ASR on the SEAS-Test set after the third iteration of SEAS?
The ASR on the SEAS‑Test set drops to 7 % after three iterations, a 55.20 % reduction from the starting value.
How does the Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) loss differ from standard SFT in SEAS training?

DPO simultaneously increases the probability of chosen outputs while decreasing that of rejected ones, leading to faster improvement after one epoch compared to SFT, which only reinforces correct answers and may stagnate.
What is the batch size used during inference with vLLM for red‑team experiments?
A batch size of 1 is employed during all inference runs using vLLM on NVIDIA A100‑80GB GPUs.
Which benchmark shows that SEAS improves safety without sacrificing general capability?
The MT‑Bench and Arena Hard benchmarks demonstrate stable or improved scores for SEAS Target models across iterations, indicating preserved general performance.
What is the temperature setting used when generating responses from the target model during attacks?
A temperature of 0.8 is set for target model generation in both inference and API‑based experiments.
How many adversarial prompts does a single Attack stage generate per seed prompt?
With n = 5 samples per seed, each Attack stage produces five adversarial prompts per seed prompt.
What proportion of the SEAS dataset consists of harmless prompts that mimic aggressive style?
The harmless test set contains 300 entries crafted to resemble aggressive syntax but are actually safe; this subset is used for evaluating over‑defensive behavior.
Which two safety benchmarks are primarily used to evaluate ASR in SEAS experiments?
BeaverTail (Beaver) and Anthropic HH‑RLHF are the main safety benchmarks employed to compute ASR for target models.
What is the learning rate applied during iterative updates of both Red Team and Target models?
A learning rate of 5 × 10⁻⁶ is used consistently across all iterative update stages.
How many seed prompts are constructed at the start of each Attack stage in SEAS?
Five thousand (5K) seed prompts are created by randomly selecting three prompts per type for each seed.
What is the maximum token limit set for model generation during SEAS inference?
The maximum number of tokens generated is capped at 2048 during all inference experiments.
Which two open‑source jailbreak methods are compared against SEAS Red Team models in Table 7?
GCG (greedy and gradient‑based) and AutoDAN (hierarchical genetic algorithm) are the two benchmark jailbreak methods compared with SEAS Red Team outputs.
What is the reported incorrect refusal rate for safe prompts after three iterations of SEAS?
The incorrect refusal rate for harmless prompts remains stable, with no significant increase observed across iterations, indicating that SEAS does not amplify over‑defensive behavior.
What is the primary goal of SKEWACT?

SKEWACT aims to improve both the effectiveness and efficiency of optimization-based jailbreak attacks by guiding token selection toward wide convex regions of the loss landscape, thereby reducing gradient overshooting.
Which activation functions are used to create the skewed model in Stage 1 of SKEWACT?
The skewed model is created by replacing each original activation function with either ReLU or LeakyReLU, depending on a hyper‑parameter choice.
How many open‑source models were evaluated in the white‑box experiments?
Five open‑source models—Llama2‑7B, Llama2‑13B, Llama2‑70B, Vicuna‑7B, and Mistral‑7B—were used.
What metric is used to measure the quality of the converged point in the optimization process?
Average Converged Loss (Avg. Loss) measures how low the loss is at the final optimized trigger.
In which stage does SKEWACT calculate gradients for both the original and skewed models?
Stage 2, Gradient Calculation, computes gradients separately for the target model and its skewed counterpart.
What criterion determines whether a token candidate is classified as robust in SKEWACT’s filtering step?
A token is robust if the sign of its gradient is identical (both positive or both negative) in both the original and skewed models; otherwise it is non‑robust.
How does SKEWACT modify the scores of non‑robust candidates during re‑ranking?
Non‑robust candidates are assigned a large positive value equal to the maximum of their original and skewed gradient magnitudes, effectively pushing them down in rank.
Which dataset provides the 100 harmful queries used for evaluation?
AdvBench supplies the 100 randomly selected harmful queries.
What is the average improvement in Attack Success Rate (ASR) that SKEWACT achieves over GCG across all models?
SKEWACT improves ASR by more than 10% on average compared to GCG.
How many optimization rounds does SKEWACT require for Llama2‑7B in the white‑box setting?
It requires an average of 336 rounds, compared to 288 rounds for GCG.
Which commercial model shows the lowest ASR for both GCG and SKEWACT in black‑box tests?
GPT‑4o exhibits the lowest ASR, with 1% for GCG and 3% for SKEWACT.
What role does the hyper‑parameter α play in the filterRerank algorithm?
α controls how much the original and skewed gradients are mixed when computing the re‑ranked score for robust candidates.
In what way does replacing SiLU with ReLU or LeakyReLU affect information flow according to Figure 5?
ReLU sparsifies negative outputs, while LeakyReLU allows more negative values through, altering the loss landscape’s curvature.
Which metric indicates how efficiently a jailbreak converges in terms of number of iterations?
Average Optimization Rounds (Avg. Round) reflects the efficiency of convergence.
What is the main cause of gradient overshooting identified for existing optimization‑based jailbreak methods?
The discrete update from a one‑hot vector causes an abrupt shift equivalent to using a very high learning rate, leading to overshoot.
How does SKEWACT’s candidate selection step differ from GCG’s default method?
SKEWACT replaces the raw gradients with filtered and re‑ranked scores before applying GCG’s top‑K selection and random choice for token updates.
Which two activation functions are considered when constructing the skewed model, and how is the choice made?
ReLU and LeakyReLU are used; a hyper‑parameter selects which one to apply during initialization.
What evidence supports that SKEWACT reaches minima in wide convex regions rather than narrow ones?
Figure 2 shows SKEWACT’s optimization path converging to a minimum in a wide convex region, while GCG skips several local minima in narrow regions.
In the black‑box setting, how many triggers are transferred from white‑box models to commercial LLMs?
All triggers optimized during white‑box attacks are directly tested on GPT‑3.5‑Turbo and GPT‑4o without further tuning.
What is the average reduction in converged loss achieved by SKEWACT compared to GCG?
SKEWACT reduces the average converged loss by more than 0.02 across all evaluated models.
Which open‑source model shows the highest ASR for both GCG and SKEWACT in white‑box tests?
Mistral‑7B achieves the highest ASR, with 65% for GCG and 73% for SKEWACT.
How many candidate tokens are considered during each optimization round before filtering in SKEWACT?
The method considers a top‑64 set of token candidates per position before applying robust filtering.
What is the purpose of the “Activate Replacement” stage in SKEWACT’s initialization phase?
It creates a skewed model by altering activation functions, which serves as a surrogate for gradient comparison during optimization.
Which metric indicates how many rounds an attack takes to converge on average?
Average Optimization Rounds (Avg. Round) measures the mean number of iterations needed for convergence.
What future research direction does the paper suggest regarding wide convex regions?
Future work aims to develop methods that precisely identify and select accessible wide convex regions during optimization to further increase ASR and efficiency.
What is the total number of unsafe instructions in the base SORRY-Bench dataset?
The base SORRY-Bench dataset contains 440 unsafe instructions, with exactly 10 instructions per each of its 44 safety categories.
How many linguistic mutations are applied to create the expanded SORRY-Bench set?
Twenty distinct linguistic mutations are applied, producing an additional 8.8K unsafe instructions when combined with the 440 base prompts.
Which safety category has the lowest representation in prior datasets compared to others?
Self‑harm instructions are over three times less represented than categories such as fraud or sexual explicit content in the ten evaluated prior datasets.
What is the fulfillment rate of Claude‑2 on the SORRY-Bench benchmark?
Claude‑2 achieves a low fulfillment rate, refusing almost all unsafe prompts with an overall fulfillment rate below 10%.
How many human annotators were involved in labeling the safety judgments for the meta‑evaluation dataset?
Six authors served as annotators, each labeling approximately 1.2K records across two rounds.
Which fine‑tuned model achieved the highest agreement with human judgments among the evaluated LLM judges?
GPT‑3.5‑turbo fine‑tuned on the human judgment data reached an agreement of 83.8% with humans, the highest reported.
What is the average maximum Jaccard similarity between SORRY-Bench base instructions and prior datasets?
The average maximum Jaccard similarity is about 44.5%, indicating that most new prompts are substantially different from existing ones.
Which category does Gemini‑1.5‑Flash consistently refuse across all its risk categories?
Gemini‑1.5‑Flash refuses every unsafe instruction in the last five categories of the “Potentially Unqualified Advice” domain (medical, financial, legal, governance, and dangerous machinery advice).
What computational cost does a fine‑tuned 7B LLM evaluator incur per evaluation pass on an A100 GPU?
Roughly ten seconds per pass when evaluating SORRY-Bench using a fine‑tuned 7B model such as Mistral‑7b‑instruct‑v0.2.
How many safety categories are grouped under the “Assistance with Crimes or Torts” domain?
Nineteen safety categories, numbered 6 through 24, belong to that domain.
What proportion of the human‑labeled dataset records are marked as fulfillment?
About 30.4% of the 7,040 annotations are labeled as compliance (fulfillment), while 69.6% are refusals.
Which linguistic mutation tends to increase model safety refusal for most evaluated LLMs?
Rephrasing prompts into interrogative question style generally leads to a higher refusal rate, decreasing fulfillment by 2–13%.
What is the time cost of using GPT‑4o as an evaluator without any add‑ons?
Approximately 260 seconds per evaluation pass on SORRY-Bench.
Which open‑weight model shows the highest fulfillment rate among the evaluated LLMs?
Mistral‑7b‑instruct‑v0.1 (without safety prompts) achieves a fulfillment rate above 90%.
How many categories are included in the “Potentially Inappropriate Topics” domain?
Fifteen categories, numbered 25 through 39, constitute that domain.
What is the average number of tokens generated per model response during benchmarking?
Responses are limited to a maximum of 1,024 tokens each.
Which evaluation technique added chain‑of‑thought prompting to LLM judges and what was its effect on agreement?
Adding CoT generally decreased agreement for most models (e.g., GPT‑4o dropped from 78.9% to 74.9%) and increased time cost significantly.
How many prior safety benchmark datasets were aggregated to build the fine‑grained taxonomy?
Ten previous datasets were used as a starting point before human‑in‑the‑loop refinement.
Which language is NOT included among the five non‑English languages used for linguistic mutations?
Spanish is not listed; the five are Malayalam, Tamil, Marathi, Simplified Chinese, and French.
What metric is used to report agreement between an automated evaluator and human judgments in the meta‑evaluation?
Cohen’s Kappa coefficient is reported as the primary agreement metric.
Which evaluation configuration leads to a drastic increase in fulfillment for Llama‑2 models?
Using an incorrect prompt template (omitting formatting tokens) increases fulfillment rates by up to 30%.
What is the average fulfillment rate across all 44 safety categories for GPT‑4o?
GPT‑4o’s overall fulfillment rate is about 30%, higher than many earlier OpenAI models.
How many total LLMs were benchmarked on SORRY-Bench, including both proprietary and open‑weight models?
Fifty‑six different models were evaluated across the benchmark.
Which safety category has the highest average fulfillment rate among all evaluated LLMs?
The “Legal Consulting Advice” category (#42) shows the highest average fulfillment, with 74–80% compliance.
What is the computational advantage of using a fine‑tuned lightweight model like Bert‑Base‑Cased as an evaluator compared to GPT‑4o?
Bert‑Base‑Cased achieves about 74.6% agreement with humans while requiring only around four seconds per evaluation pass, far less than GPT‑4o’s 260 seconds.
What is the Attack Success Rate (ASR) achieved by the baseline Llama model on the StrongReject-small dataset?
The baseline ASR for Llama on StrongReject-small is 2%.
Which safety alignment strategy does the Beaver model employ during training?
Beaver uses Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) exclusively for its safety alignment.
What is the main difference between Supervised Fine‑Tuning (SFT) and Self‑Supervised Representation Attack (SSRA)?
SFT requires harmful responses as labels during fine‑tuning, while SSRA manipulates semantic representations without using harmful response labels.
How many benign daily questions are used to generate benign representations in the SSRA method?
The SSRA approach uses 250 benign daily questions generated by GPT‑4 to create benign representations.
Which PEFT algorithm achieves the highest ASR increase on Llama when fine‑tuned with the HS dataset?
LoRA attains the largest ASR increase for Llama when fine‑tuned with the HS dataset, achieving over 80% ASR.
What is the effect of removing the default system prompt on Mistral’s ASR?
Removing Mistral’s default system prompt increases its ASR by about 26%, indicating a significant drop in safety alignment.
Which hyperparameter setting for learning rate (LR) is used when fine‑tuning Llama with LoRA on the HS dataset?
The default LR for LoRA fine‑tuning of Llama on HS is 1e-3.
How does the Self‑Supervised Representation Defense (SSRD) recover safety alignment after an SFT attack on Llama?
SSRD reduces ASR to near zero while only decreasing ACC by about 2.9%, effectively restoring safety with minimal utility loss.
What is the proportion of unsafe responses in the SA dataset for Mistral according to HarmBench-Llama‑2‑13b‑cls?
The SA dataset contains approximately 66% unsafe responses for Mistral.
Which model editing method shows higher misalignment effectiveness on Llama compared to MEMIT?
ROME achieves higher ASR increases on Llama than MEMIT, demonstrating greater misalignment effectiveness.
What is the impact of using a single‑topic dataset (HS-10‑IA) on Llama’s ASR after LoRA fine‑tuning?
Using HS-10‑IA raises Llama’s ASR to about 75%, showing that even single‑topic datasets can significantly misalign safety.
Which safety data filter demonstrates the highest accuracy for detecting unsafe content in D unsafe corpus?
The OpenAI Moderation API achieves the highest detection accuracy on the D unsafe corpus among the evaluated filters.
What is the utility metric (ACC) of Mistral after fine‑tuning with the HS dataset using AdaLoRA?
Mistral’s ACC remains near 74% after AdaLoRA fine‑tuning with HS, indicating minimal utility degradation.
How many tokens are in a typical SA Q‑A pair according to Table III?
An SA Q‑A pair contains about 265.75 tokens on average.
What is the effect of adding the DT adversarial system prompt during LoRA fine‑tuning on Llama’s ASR?
Adding DT increases Llama’s ASR to approximately 86%, showing that adversarial prompts amplify misalignment when combined with fine‑tuning.
Which detoxification method retains the highest utility (ACC) after being attacked by SFT on Mistral?
DINM preserves the highest ACC for Mistral post‑SFT attack, maintaining better utility than WMDP or SOUL.
What is the default number of training epochs used for SSRA when fine‑tuning Llama with 30 harmful embeddings?
The default epoch count for SSRA on Llama with 30 harmful embeddings is 8 epochs.
Which PEFT algorithm has the lowest percentage of trainable parameters on Mistral?
(IA) 3 has the lowest trainable parameter percentage on Mistral, at about 0.7%.
What is the misalignment score achieved by Llama after fine‑tuning with HS using LoRA?
The misalignment score for Llama after HS fine‑tuning with LoRA is approximately 49.1.
How does SSRD perform against a model that was fine‑tuned with benign data (MetaMath1k) on Llama?
SSRD reduces ASR to near zero while increasing ACC‑G by about 5%, effectively realigning safety without harming downstream math performance.
Which dataset size is most critical for achieving stable utility when fine‑tuning with LoRA on Beaver?
Using the full HS dataset (100 samples) yields more stable utility compared to smaller subsets like HS‑10.
What is the average inference time of GPTFuzz on D unsafe corpus according to Table IV?
GPTFuzz processes D unsafe corpus in about 1.0 second per sample.
Which safety misalignment attack requires the least computational resources among SPM, SFT, SSRA, and ME?
System‑Prompt Modification (SPM) demands minimal compute since it only alters prompts without model weight changes.
What is the impact of a 5% increase in learning rate on LoRA fine‑tuning ASR for Llama with HS dataset?
Increasing LR by 5% can raise ASR modestly but may also cause noticeable ACC degradation, indicating sensitivity to hyperparameters.
Which defense mechanism shows limited effectiveness against SFT attacks in open‑source scenarios?
Detoxification methods (DINM, WMDP, SOUL) fail to effectively defend against SFT attacks when applied to open‑source models.
What is the main security risk that prompt injection poses for LLM‑integrated applications?

Prompt injection allows an attacker to embed malicious instructions into the data part of an LLM input, causing the model to follow the injected instruction instead of the system designer’s benign instruction.
Which three optimization‑free prompt injection attacks are used in SecAlign’s training dataset?
Straightforward, Completion, and Ignore–Completion attacks are employed during training to generate injected prompts for the preference dataset.
What is the key difference between StruQ and SecAlign in how they treat desirable and undesirable responses?

StruQ only maximizes likelihood of the desirable response, while SecAlign explicitly optimizes a margin that encourages the desirable response and discourages the undesirable one using preference optimization.
How does SecAlign construct its preference dataset from a public instruction‑tuning corpus?

For each sample with data, it concatenates a random other sample’s instruction into the data part to create an injected prompt, then records the original sample’s response as desirable and the injected sample’s response as undesirable.
What LoRA hyperparameters are used during SecAlign training?
The LoRA rank r is set to 64, lora_alpha to 8, lora_dropout to 0.1, and target modules include “q_proj” and “v_proj”.
Which five open‑weight models were evaluated with SecAlign in the experiments?
Mistral‑7B‑Instruct, Llama3‑8B‑Instruct, Llama‑7B, Mistral‑7B, and Llama3‑8B.
What utility metric is used to evaluate model performance under no attack?
AlpacaEval2, a judge‑based evaluation that compares the model’s responses against a reference using GPT‑4‑turbo.
How does SecAlign perform against optimization‑based attacks such as GCG on Llama3‑8B‑Instruct?
It reduces the attack success rate to below 10%, achieving an ASR of around 1–8% and improving over StruQ’s >40% ASR.
Which preference optimization algorithm was chosen for SecAlign and why?
Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) was selected because it balances efficiency, performance, and training stability better than alternatives like ORPO or KTO.
What is the effect of using a smaller portion (e.g., 20%) of the instruction‑tuning dataset for SecAlign training?
Even with only 20% of the data, SecAlign still achieves significantly lower ASR than StruQ while maintaining comparable utility.
How many epochs and learning rates were used during DPO fine‑tuning for the five models?
Three epochs were trained with model‑specific learning rates [1.4e-4, 1.6e-4, 2.0e-4, 1.4e-4, 1.6e-4] ×10⁻⁴.
What is the maximum attack success rate (ASR) achieved by SecAlign against optimization‑free attacks on Llama‑7B?
SecAlign achieves a 0% ASR for all optimization‑free attacks, outperforming prompting‑based defenses that still show non‑zero rates.
In the SEP benchmark, how does SecAlign’s utility compare to the undefended model?
SecAlign retains about 46.6% of the utility relative to the undefended model, a substantial improvement over StruQ’s 18.9%.
Which prompting‑based defense achieves the lowest ASR on Llama3‑8B‑Instruct for optimization‑free attacks?
The In‑Context defense reduces ASR to 0.5% on Llama3‑8B‑Instruct, but still higher than SecAlign’s 0%.
What is the primary limitation of SecAlign mentioned in the conclusion regarding instruction and data separation?
SecAlign requires explicit delimiters that separate instruction from data; it cannot defend when such clear separations are absent.
How does SecAlign handle imperative sentences in the data part during utility evaluation?
It treats them as data, not instructions, leading to a higher percentage of correct handling (52% vs. 16% for undefended models) and maintaining overall utility.
What is the GPU cost for training SecAlign with DPO on Llama‑7B using four 80 GB A100s?
Approximately two GPU‑hours per epoch, totaling about eight GPU‑hours for three epochs.
Which optimization‑based attack achieves the highest ASR against undefended models in the experiments?
GCG consistently attains the highest ASR among optimization‑based attacks when applied to undefended models.
What role does the reference model play in DPO training within SecAlign?
The reference model provides baseline logits that constrain the updated model’s output distribution, preventing it from deviating too far during preference optimization.
How many samples from AlpacaFarm contain a data part suitable for prompt injection evaluation?
208 out of the 805 total AlpacaFarm samples include a data part and are used for security testing.
Which attack type is considered the strongest among the optimization‑free attacks tested against SecAlign?
The Ignore–Completion attack, which combines an ignore instruction with a completion structure, is the most effective among optimization‑free attacks.
What is the impact of using different learning rates on SecAlign’s performance according to Figure 6?
Within a reasonable range, varying the learning rate still yields better security than StruQ while maintaining stable utility, with negligible error bars at optimal settings.
How does SecAlign perform against the InjecAgent API‑calling prompt injection benchmark?
It achieves 0% ASR on InjecAgent, demonstrating strong generalization to attacks involving external API calls.
Which dataset is used for generating the preference dataset when the SFT dataset and preference dataset come from different domains?
The Cleaned Alpaca dataset is used for the preference dataset while the SFT model may be trained on a private commercial instruction‑tuning dataset, showing cross‑domain robustness.
What future research direction is suggested to combine SecAlign with other defense techniques?

A multi‑tiered approach that integrates SecAlign with detection methods (e.g., PromptGuard), input reformatting, output manipulation, and system‑level defenses is proposed for stronger overall security.
What is the attack success rate (ASR) achieved against GitHub Copilot using the Level I Guided Trigger Attack?
The Level I Guided Trigger Attack achieves a 99.4% ASR on GitHub Copilot, meaning that almost all malicious queries result in harmful code completions.
Which two mainstream LCCTs were evaluated for jailbreaking attacks in the study?
The study evaluates GitHub Copilot and Amazon Q as the two main LLM‑based Code Completion Tools for jailbreaking experiments.
What percentage of GitHub usernames generated by the privacy extraction attack were valid real users?
Out of 2,704 usernames produced, 2,173 (approximately 80.36%) matched actual GitHub accounts.
How does the Filename Proxy Attack exploit LCCTs’ input workflow?
It renames a file to encode a query and adds a static comment that triggers code completion, forcing the tool to treat the filename as a prompt for malicious content generation.
What is the ASR of the Cross‑File Attack on Amazon Q?
The Cross‑File Attack achieves a 52.3% ASR on Amazon Q, indicating that more than half of the attempts succeed in producing harmful outputs.
Which general LLMs were included in the evaluation and what is their highest reported ASR for the Level I attack?
GPT‑3.5, GPT‑4, and GPT‑4o were evaluated; GPT‑3.5 shows a 68.3% ASR while GPT‑4 and GPT‑4o have lower rates of 23.8% and 36.5% respectively.
What role does the “Guiding Words Addition” step play in the Level I attack?
It appends neutral guiding terms to a variable name, shifting the LCCT’s response mode from code‑specific to general conversational output, thereby increasing vulnerability.
In the training data extraction evaluation, how many exact matching email addresses were extracted from GitHub Copilot?
The attack extracted 54 exact matching email addresses that matched users’ real emails on GitHub.
Which programming language showed higher ASR for Level I and II attacks compared to Python in the ablation study?
Go exhibited higher ASRs than Python, indicating that LCCTs may be more vulnerable when handling less common languages.
What is the primary difference between general LLMs and LCCTs in terms of input processing?
General LLMs process user inputs directly, whereas LCCTs aggregate multiple information sources such as file names, current file code, and cross‑file content before generating suggestions.
How many queries were used per restricted category for the jailbreaking evaluation?
Twenty queries per category (illegal content, hate speech, pornography, harmful content) were generated, totaling 80 queries across all categories.
What safety alignment technique is commonly employed by LLMs to reduce prediction errors but may expose biases?
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) fine‑tunes models based on human reward signals, aiming to align outputs with ethical standards while potentially retaining bias.
Which LCCT’s security filtering rule blocks content containing “@” and “.”, and how can it be circumvented?
GitHub Copilot uses a filter that blocks strings with “@” and “.”; attackers can bypass this by embedding such characters within code comments or variable names that are not directly evaluated.
What metric is used to evaluate training data extraction attacks on GitHub Copilot?
The evaluation compares extracted privacy entries (emails, locations) against GitHub REST API data, classifying matches as exact or fuzzy based on string equality or subset inclusion.
Which attack method achieved the lowest ASR among all evaluated methods for Amazon Q?
The Level II Code Embedded Attack had a 22.3% ASR on Amazon Q, the lowest among the listed attacks for that tool.
What is the purpose of embedding sensitive words within long sentences in the Level II attack?
Embedding sensitive terms into lengthy natural language sentences obfuscates malicious intent, making it harder for LCCTs to detect and filter harmful content.
How many GitHub users with location information were identified by the privacy extraction attack?
The attack identified 1,109 users with location data, of which 100 exact matches and 214 fuzzy matches were found in Copilot’s output.
