Which baseline attack method was adapted for LCCTs to demonstrate comparative effectiveness?
The CodeAttack study by Ren et al. was adapted; it originally targeted general LLMs but was re‑used as a benchmark against the new code‑based attacks on LCCTs.
What is the reported ASR for the Filename Attack on GitHub Copilot?
The Filename Attack achieved a 72.5% ASR on GitHub Copilot, showing substantial success in exploiting file name prompts.
Which category of harmful content did Amazon Q detect early and terminate code completions for during evaluation?
Amazon Q frequently terminated completions early for the pornography category, indicating stronger proactive detection for that type of content.
What is the main advantage of LCCTs’ contextual information aggregation for attackers?
It expands the attack surface by allowing malicious prompts to be hidden within file names or cross‑file references, increasing the likelihood of bypassing safety checks.
Which general LLM showed zero ASR on the Level I Guided Trigger Attack in the study?
GPT‑4o had a 0% ASR for the Level I attack, indicating strong resistance to that particular jailbreak strategy.
What is the primary security concern associated with LCCTs using proprietary code datasets for fine‑tuning?
Proprietary training data may contain sensitive user information from public repositories, leading to potential privacy leakage when models generate completions that echo that data.
How many queries were evaluated in total across all categories for the jailbreaking attacks?
A total of 80 queries (20 per category) were used to assess the effectiveness of the jailbreak methods.
What is the effect on ASR when removing the “Guiding Words Addition” step from the Level I attack on GitHub Copilot?
Removing that step reduces the ASR by approximately 92.5%, demonstrating its critical role in achieving high success rates for the attack.
Which eight open‑source models were evaluated for the space attack?
The study examined Vicuna v1.5, Llama‑2, Llama‑3, Mistral, Falcon, Guanaco, MPT, and ChatGLM.
What is the Attack Success Rate (ASR) of Vicuna‑7B when a space is appended to its template?
Vicuna‑7B achieves an ASR of 100 % with a single trailing space.
How many harmful responses were generated by Guanaco‑13B after appending a space?
Guanaco‑13B produced harmful outputs in 93 % of the evaluated prompts.
What dataset was used to generate the 100‑sample prompt set for evaluation?
The prompts came from the AdvBench benchmark, specifically its harmful behaviors subset.
Which models remained unaffected by appending a space to their templates?
Llama‑2 (both 7B and 13B) and Llama‑3 (8B) showed zero ASR when a space was appended.
How does the GCG algorithm differ from typical adversarial suffix attacks in this study?
GCG was run with a suffix length of one token to search for single characters that trigger harmful outputs, unlike standard uses which employ longer suffixes.
Why do single‑character tokens appear rarely in pre‑training data according to the authors?
Subword tokenization merges common patterns, so isolated space or punctuation tokens occur infrequently, making them context‑specific.
What effect does appending a space have on the top‑k predicted next tokens for Vicuna‑7B?
The overlap of top‑5 to top‑100 predicted tokens drops dramatically, indicating a shift in the model’s generation distribution.
Which punctuation token achieved an ASR of 87 % against Falcon‑7B when appended?
A hashtag (#) token produced an ASR of 87 % for Falcon‑7B.
How does inserting the space at the start of the template affect Vicuna‑7B’s ASR compared to appending it at the end?
Inserting the space at the start yields a 3 % ASR, whereas appending it at the end gives 100 %.
What hyperparameter was used for greedy decoding during all experiments?
The models were evaluated with greedy decoding (no temperature or top‑k sampling).
How long did it take to run the pre‑training data tokenization analysis on a single GPU?
Approximately 8 minutes per token and 30 seconds for the full analysis.
What is the base rate of harmful outputs for MPT‑7B before any perturbation?
The base rate for MPT‑7B is 15 %.
Which token type was most frequently found immediately after a single space in Falcon’s tokenizer?
Numerical tokens were the most common following a single space.
How does appending a space affect Mistral‑7B’s performance on GSM8K?
The strict‑match accuracy drops from 38.67 % to 36.32 %, and flexible‑extract from 42.15 % to 40.86 %.
What theoretical insight explains why a single appended token can alter attention maps in transformer models?
Adding a space changes the last position’s key vector, which can shift the attention distribution away from safety‑critical tokens.
Which model exhibited the highest ASR when a tab character was appended to its template?
Falcon‑7B achieved an 96 % ASR with a tab character.
What is the reported computational cost of running the GCG search for Vicuna‑7B?
The GCG search required about 20 minutes per token and 3 hours total for all suffixes tested.
How many tokens were used in the GSM8K evaluation to measure reasoning impact?
The evaluation employed the CoT few‑shot methodology with the standard GSM8K prompt set, but the exact token count is not specified; it uses the full prompt length per example.
Which chat template includes a system instruction that explicitly mentions refusing harmful content?
Llama‑2’s template contains a system message stating “Your answers should not include any harmful, unethical, racist, sexist, toxic, dangerous, or illegal content.”
What percentage of Vicuna‑7B outputs contained the phrase “1.” after appending a space?
Approximately 27 % of Vicuna‑7B outputs included the list marker “1.”, indicating list generation.
How does the presence of a single space token influence the model’s first predicted token type for most models?
It shifts the first token from non‑numeric to numeric in many models, aligning with pre‑training patterns where spaces precede numbers.
Which model showed an ASR of 21 % when a space was appended and also had a base rate of 15 %?
MPT‑7B exhibited a 21 % ASR with a 15 % base rate.
What is the impact on Llama‑2’s ASR when using a mismatched template from another model without appending a space?
Using a mismatched template increases harmful outputs, but the ASR remains low; adding a space still yields zero ASR for Llama‑2.
Which token did GCG most frequently select during its 100‑step search on Vicuna‑7B?
The token “e” appeared most often, achieving an ASR of 81 % in the GCG search.
What is the primary goal of SneakyPrompt?
SneakyPrompt aims to automatically generate adversarial prompts that bypass safety filters in text-to-image models while preserving the semantic content of the original NSFW prompt.
Which text-to-image models are evaluated against SneakyPrompt?
The evaluation uses Stable Diffusion (open source) and DALL·E 2 (online API).
How many target prompts were used in the NSFW‑200 dataset for evaluation?
200 target prompts containing NSFW content were generated using ChatGPT.
What is the definition of an adversarial prompt in this work?
An adversarial prompt is one that bypasses a safety filter (F(M,p)=0) and whose generated image has similar visual semantics to the image from the original sensitive prompt.
Which three categories of safety filters are described?
Text‑based, image‑based, and text‑image‑based safety filters.
What reinforcement learning architecture is used for policy network in SneakyPrompt‑RL?
An LSTM with a fully connected layer that outputs token probabilities sequentially.
How many queries does SneakyPrompt‑RL typically need to find an adversarial prompt on Stable Diffusion?
About 9.5 queries on average, ranging from 2.26 to 24.49 depending on the filter and scenario.
What is the reward function used in the default SneakyPrompt‑RL?
The normalized cosine similarity between the image embedding of the adversarial prompt’s output and the target prompt’s text embedding.
Which baseline search strategy achieves the highest number of queries among the baselines?
Brute force search, which can require over a thousand queries (e.g., 1,094.05 on average).
What is the bypass rate of SneakyPrompt‑RL against DALL·E 2’s closed‑box safety filter in one‑time attacks?
57.15% bypass rate with an average of 24.49 queries.
How does the non‑English word filter affect SneakyPrompt‑RL’s performance?
It reduces the bypass rate by about 3.75 % on average and increases the number of queries slightly due to a smaller search space.
Which safety filter type shows the lowest re‑use bypass rate in the experiments?
Image‑based safety filters, because random seeds cause different generated images for the same prompt.
What is the similarity threshold δ used in SneakyPrompt‑RL’s early stopping criterion?
A default value of 0.26 is used to decide when an adversarial prompt has sufficient semantic similarity.
How does increasing the token length parameter l affect query cost?
Larger l expands the search space, leading to more queries needed for exploration and higher average query counts.
What metric indicates how close the generated image’s semantics are to the target prompt?
The FID score between the adversarial image distribution and a reference set (target or real images).
Which text‑based safety filter was most resistant to SneakyPrompt‑RL in re‑use attacks?
Text‑classifier, achieving only about 68.87 % bypass rate due to its larger parameter count.
What is the effect of using a shadow encoder identical to the target model’s encoder versus a different one?
Using the same encoder slightly improves FID scores but does not significantly change bypass rates or query counts.
How many tokens are typically replaced in a single adversarial prompt generated by SneakyPrompt‑RL?
Usually one sensitive token is replaced with three non‑sensitive tokens (m = 3).
What is the main advantage of the RL approach over heuristic baselines?
RL strategically guides token selection based on feedback, reducing query cost and improving bypass success.
Which safety filter achieves the highest effectiveness (i.e., lowest bypass rate) against SneakyPrompt‑RL overall?
The text‑classifier filter, with an effectiveness of 100 % in one‑time attacks and 68.87 % re‑use success.
What is the role of the GetSearchSpace function in SneakyPrompt‑RL?
It determines which tokens are considered sensitive and expands the search space by adding more tokens to replace when necessary.
How does SneakyPrompt‑RL handle a situation where an adversarial prompt fails to bypass the safety filter for several consecutive queries?
It expands the search space by replacing an additional token in the target prompt, increasing action diversity.
What is the average FID score achieved by SneakyPrompt‑RL when bypassing Stable Diffusion’s original safety filter in one‑time attacks?
Approximately 132.01, indicating good semantic similarity to target images.
Which baseline method achieves the lowest FID score among re‑use adversarial prompts for Stable Diffusion?
SneakyPrompt‑RL with a FID of about 169.15 in re‑use scenarios.
What future research direction is suggested by the authors regarding safety filters?
Developing more robust safety filters, potentially through adversarial training that incorporates adversarial prompts during filter training.
What are the three distinct types of prompt hacking attacks discussed in the paper?
The three types are prompt jailbreak, prompt injection, and prompt leaking.
Which approach is used to evaluate LLM responses into five categories?
A framework that categorizes responses as Reject‑Irrelevant Output, Reject‑Safety Mechanism Triggered, Prompt too Long, Partial Response, or Full Response.
What is the main goal of a prompt jailbreak attack?
To bypass an LLM’s alignment and produce restricted content that violates usage policies.
How does a direct prompt injection differ from an indirect one?
Direct injection feeds malicious instructions straight to the model, while indirect injection embeds them in data the model consumes, such as a webpage.
Which LLM showed 100% safety mechanism activation for indirect prompt injection?
Microsoft Copilot activated safety mechanisms on all indirect injection attempts.
What is the “pretending” approach used in jailbreak experiments?
It asks the model to imagine a fictional scenario where normal rules do not apply, aiming to bypass ethical filters.
Which LLM had a 50% success rate for prompt leaking attacks?
Gemini achieved a 50% partial response rate and a 50% safety trigger rate in leaking tests.
What is the token limit difference between Gemini (Flash 1.5) and ChatGPT‑4?
Gemini has an estimated token limit of millions (2M) with a practical limit of 8K, while ChatGPT‑4’s limit is about 32K tokens.
Which defense method involves adjusting hyper‑parameters like temperature and top‑p?
Fine‑tuning LLM settings includes modifying parameters such as temperature, top‑p, presence penalty, and frequency penalty.
What does the “Do Anything Now (DAN)” approach instruct the model to do?
DAN asks the model to adopt a persona that can supposedly bypass its normal ethical constraints and perform unrestricted actions.
Which LLM consistently triggered safety mechanisms for both DAN and pretending jailbreaks?
Gemini, ChatGPT‑4, and Perplexity all showed 100% safety trigger rates under these attacks.
In the experiments, what was a common outcome when an attack prompt exceeded the model’s length limit?
The model responded with “Prompt too Long,” indicating it could not process the full input.
What is the primary benefit of using pre‑training with human feedback (PHF) as a defense?
PHF reduces unwanted content in outputs and increases user satisfaction compared to standard fine‑tuning alone.
Which LLM had the highest pass rate for direct prompt injection attacks?
ChatGPT‑4 achieved an 86% success rate on direct injections, higher than other tested models.
What is a key characteristic that distinguishes token‑level jailbreaks from prompt‑level ones?
Token‑level jailbreaks modify specific tokens to bypass safety, requiring many queries and high computational resources.
Which defense technique places user input between two prompts to mitigate attacks?
Sandwich defense inserts the user input between two prompts as a mitigation strategy.
According to the paper, why might newer models like Gemini be more secure against jailbreaks?
They benefit from recent architectural and training improvements that enhance safety mechanisms.
What does the “Contextual Knowledge with Instruction” defense do?
It fetches relevant information via a Search API during query processing to improve accuracy and reduce unsafe outputs.
In the leaking experiments, which LLM had no successful responses but 100% irrelevant output?
Microsoft Copilot produced only irrelevant outputs with no partial or full responses.
How does the paper define “prompt leaking”?
Prompt leaking is an attack that extracts a model’s underlying system prompt, potentially revealing proprietary instructions.
Which LLM showed a 50% safety trigger rate in both leaking and injection experiments?
ChatGPT‑3.5 triggered safety mechanisms in half of the leaking cases and had a 50% success rate in injection tests.
What is the purpose of red teaming as described in the paper?
Red teaming systematically attacks LLMs to identify harmful outputs and strengthen defenses.
Which LLM exhibited a “Prompt too Long” failure when using the DAN approach?
Microsoft Copilot flagged prompts as too long under the DAN attack strategy.
What is the main advantage of model compression techniques like pruning for safety?
Pruning reduces parameter count, potentially lowering overfitting and adversarial susceptibility while maintaining performance.
Which LLM had a 100% pass rate for prompt leaking attacks according to Table VI?
ChatSonic achieved a 100% full response rate in the prompt leaking experiments.
What dataset size was used to evaluate the LLM raters on harmful content?
The study evaluated LLM raters on a dataset of 50,000 English comments, comprising 40,000 violative and 10,000 non‑violative samples across four policy categories.
Which Google PaLM 2 models were tested in the experiments?
Two PaLM 2 models were used: text-bison and the larger text-unicorn, both accessed via Vertex AI with a context window of 8192 tokens.
What is the accuracy achieved by the best prompt on the election misinformation policy using text‑unicorn?
The best prompt on text‑unicorn reached an accuracy of 98.7% for election misinformation, surpassing human rater performance in that category.
How does a zero‑shot prompt differ from a few‑shot prompt in this study?

A zero‑shot prompt contains only the policy specification and no example comments, while a few‑shot prompt includes several sample comment–verdict pairs to prime the LLM before evaluating new content.
What role does keyword context play in the few‑shot prompts?
Keyword context lists salient terms from violative examples; it can improve recall for certain policies but is unnecessary for text‑unicorn when accuracy is prioritized over recall.
Which policy category showed the lowest precision with text‑bison under a zero‑shot prompt?
Harassment had the lowest precision (81.9%) with text‑bison in the zero‑shot configuration, indicating more false positives compared to other categories.
What is the cost per thousand characters for querying PaLM 2 on Google Cloud?
The cost is $0.0005 per thousand characters of combined input and output when using PaLM 2 models via Vertex AI.
How does dynamic few‑shot example selection affect performance compared to hand‑picked examples?
Dynamic selection generally improves accuracy for most policies, especially violent extremism and harassment with text‑unicorn, but can degrade performance if labels are noisy.
What happens to LLM rater accuracy when the comment length exceeds 100 characters?
Accuracy declines for longer comments because the model may miss short violative passages within a larger benign context; segmentation could mitigate this but adds cost.
In the pre‑filtering mode, what recall threshold yields the highest removal of non‑violative content for election misinformation?
A 95.1% recall threshold allows the LLM to filter out 99.3% of non‑violative comments while still passing almost all violations to human raters.
What is the precision achieved by an autonomous text‑unicorn rater on violent extremism?
The autonomous rating accuracy for violent extremism is 91.1%, which translates to a similar precision given balanced test data.
How many comments were automatically escalated with a 95% precision threshold in rapid escalation mode for election misinformation?
With a 95% precision threshold, the LLM escalated 99.7% of violations for election misinformation.
What percentage of human‑rater disagreements did the LLM identify as potential errors in the validation study?
The LLM disagreed with human raters on 7.4% of decisions, indicating possible human error or ambiguity.
Which policy category had the highest agreement between tie‑breaking ratings and ground truth when the LLM disagreed?
Election misinformation showed the highest agreement (52%) between tie‑breaking votes and ground truth compared to other categories.
What is the impact on human rater precision and recall when they receive keyword explanations from an LLM in a real‑world pilot?
Human raters experienced a 9% absolute increase in recall and an 11% absolute increase in precision, with no significant change in review time.
How many policies were included in the single multi‑policy prompt used in the live deployment?
The live deployment used a single prompt covering over 20 distinct harmful content policies, simplified to reduce length.
What is the estimated cost difference between using a multi‑policy prompt and single‑policy prompts for evaluating 50,000 comments with text‑unicorn?
Using a multi‑policy prompt costs roughly $762, whereas single‑policy few‑shot prompts would cost about $1,137 for the same workload.
Which design pattern allows LLMs to flag potential human rating errors without automatic escalation?
Human rater validation is the pattern where an LLM flags disagreements with human raters, prompting additional review rather than immediate action.
What proportion of comments did the pre‑filtering strategy remove from a 1:1 violative–non‑violative queue at a 99% recall threshold?
At a 99% recall threshold, the pre‑filter removed between 54.5% and 98.2% of non‑violative comments, depending on policy.
How does the LLM’s token probability score function in dynamic threshold tuning?
The model returns the probability that the next token is “Yes”; a threshold T determines whether a comment is classified as violative (p ≥ T) or not.
What was the recall achieved by the LLM when using a 95.5% threshold for hate speech in pre‑filtering mode?
The LLM maintained a 95.5% recall while filtering out 86.6% of non‑violative comments for hate speech.
Which policy experienced the largest drop in accuracy when all four policies were combined into one prompt?
Election misinformation suffered the largest accuracy decline, dropping by up to 13.7% compared to its single‑policy performance.
What is the effect of adding a dummy policy and example comment at the start of a zero‑shot prompt?
Adding a dummy policy primes the LLM for the expected “Yes/No” output format, improving consistency in responses without affecting accuracy significantly.
How many comments were evaluated in the real‑world pilot that demonstrated a 41.5% reduction in content requiring human review?
The pilot evaluated 2,700 English comments over 30 days, achieving a 41.5% reduction in human review workload at a fixed recall of 95.1%.
What future research direction is suggested for handling concept lag in LLM raters?
Future work should explore dynamic few‑shot example selection and retrieval‑augmented generation to provide up‑to‑date context, mitigating the model’s limited awareness of recent events.
What is the Attack Success Rate (ASR) achieved by SwordEcho on Llama2-13b-chat?
SwordEcho achieves an ASR of 95% on Llama2-13b-chat, outperforming all baseline methods listed in Table 2.
Which three reward components are used in the reinforcement learning objective for generating jailbreak prompts?
The reward function consists of readability (R₁), diversity (R₂), and harmfulness of the target model’s output (R₃).
How does SwordEcho ensure that generated jailbreak prompts maintain structural integrity?
SwordEcho categorizes sentences into five types—character, scene, positive, negative, and others—and applies guided crossover to preserve the original sequence during prompt generation.
What is the role of the ToxiGen model in the training pipeline?
ToxiGen evaluates the harmfulness of the target model’s output; a probability above 0.5 indicates a successful jailbreak attack, providing the R₃ reward signal.
In which phase does SwordEcho prioritize readability and diversity over attack potency?
During Phase One (non‑adversarial), SwordEcho focuses on maximizing readability and diversity while minimizing the influence of attack potency.
What hardware configuration was used for the experiments described in Section 5.1?
The experiments ran on 8 A100-80GB GPUs, a 128-core CPU, and 1 TB of RAM.
Which open‑source jailbreak prompt set was used to pre‑train the attack model before reinforcement learning?
The publicly available jailbreak prompt set from rubend18/ChatGPT-JailbreakPrompts was used for supervised fine‑tuning.
How many distinct problem classes were queries divided into during experimental setup?
Queries were categorized into 11 distinct classes, ranging from harassment to graphic violence and an average ASR class.
What is the impact on attack potency when a jailbreak prompt’s structural integrity is lost, according to Table 1?
When structure is out‑of‑order or incomplete, the ASR drops to 0%, and mean harmfulness falls below 0.013, indicating negligible attack capability.
Which baseline method achieves the highest ASR on Vicuna-7b-v1.5 according to Table 2?
Masterkey attains the highest ASR of 43% on Vicuna-7b-v1.5 among the listed baselines.
Describe how guided crossover improves convergence speed in the diversity experiment.
Guided crossover reduces the number of iterations needed to reach a high score from four to two, improving efficiency by about 50%.
What is the maximum ASR achieved by SwordEcho on commercial APIs as shown in Table 4?
SwordEcho achieves up to 96% ASR on Baichuan2‑13b‑chat and InternLM-chat-20b among the commercial APIs tested.
Which reward weight values are used during Phase Two of training to emphasize attack potency?
In Phase Two, α and β are set much smaller than γ (α ≈ β ≪ γ) so that harmfulness dominates the reward signal.
How many queries were evaluated in the transferability experiment depicted in Figure 4?
The transferability experiment used 11 different problem scenarios across multiple target models, totaling 765 queries as reported in Table 3.
What metric is used to assess whether a model’s entire output contains harmful content during evaluation?
Both OpenAI's API discriminator and the open‑source ToxiGen model are employed to determine if the output is harmful.
Explain why Masterkey shows weaker targeting compared to SwordEcho.
Masterkey generates general jailbreak prompts without problem‑specific tailoring, leading to lower attack potency on targeted queries.
What does a cosine similarity close to 1 indicate between two jailbreak prompts?
A similarity near 1 means the prompts are nearly identical, which reduces diversity and can weaken the overall attack strategy.
Which model served as the initial generative model before being migrated to Vicuna‑7b for transferability tests?
The initial generative model was trained using Llama2‑27b before being transferred to Vicuna‑7b.
What is the average ASR achieved by SwordEcho across all target models in Table 4?
SwordEcho achieves an overall average ASR of approximately 92–95% across the evaluated target models.
How does the readability reward R₁ compute perplexity for a jailbreak prompt?
R₁ calculates perplexity by taking the exponential of the negative average log probability of each word given its preceding context, as defined in Equation 2.
In which scenario did SwordEcho perform suboptimally on Vicuna‑7b, and why?
SwordEcho performed poorly on Vicuna‑7b because the model failed to understand the jailbreak prompts, producing irrelevant responses that lowered readability.
What is the purpose of the diversity penalty in the reward function?
The diversity penalty discourages generating highly similar prompts by subtracting a term proportional to the maximum similarity between any two generated prompts.
How many iterations were required for guided crossover to achieve an R₃ score of 0.9 during crossing experiments?
Guided crossover achieved an R₃ score of 0.9 in only two iterations, compared to four iterations without constraints.
Which dataset provided the 11 classes of harmful queries used for evaluation?
The PKU30K dataset was used to source and categorize the 765 harmful queries into 11 classes for evaluation.
What is the main contribution of SwordEcho as stated in the conclusion section?
SwordEcho offers an efficient, multi‑reward reinforcement learning framework that generates high‑potency, diverse jailbreak prompts with strong transferability across open‑source and commercial LLMs.
What is the main objective of the proposed red‑teaming framework in this work?
The framework aims to discover prompts that elicit harmful responses from a target LLM while keeping the modified prompt close to a reference prompt from a given dataset.
Which two datasets are used for training and evaluating the red‑teaming methods?
The Red Teaming dataset, containing dialogues between a human red‑teamer and an AI assistant, and the alpaca‑gpt4 dataset, which consists of instruction‑following tasks generated by GPT‑4.
What metric is used to quantify the toxicity of a target model’s response?
A pretrained toxicity classifier provides logits that are treated as reward values; higher logits indicate more toxic responses.
How does DART enforce the proximity constraint during training?
By adding a regularization term β‖µ‖² to the PPO loss, which penalizes the norm of the predicted noise vector µ so that the perturbation stays within the budget ϵ.
What type of neural architecture underlies DART’s diffusion model?
An encoder‑decoder transformer based on T5‑base with a newly initialized head that predicts the mean of the noise to be added in embedding space.
How is the action space defined for the reinforcement learning formulation of DART?
The state is an embedding vector of the reference prompt; the action is a continuous noise vector applied to this embedding, and the transition subtracts the noise from the current state.
Which target LLMs were evaluated in the experiments?
Three models were used: gpt2‑alpaca, Vicuna‑7b, and Llama‑2‑7b‑chat‑hf.
What is the reported Attack Success Rate (ASR) of DART with ε = 0.5 on the Red Teaming dataset for Vicuna‑7b?
The ASR is 3.88%, meaning that about 4% of prompts modified by DART were classified as toxic.
How does the zero‑shot baseline attempt to keep modifications small?
It instructs an uncensored LLM to rewrite a reference prompt while explicitly requesting only minor changes, relying on the model’s own paraphrasing ability.
What advantage does DART have over auto‑regressive baselines according to the results table?
DART achieves higher ASR values while maintaining cosine similarity close to the reference, indicating better control over both harmfulness and proximity.
In what way is the FLIRT baseline different from the zero‑shot method?
FLIRT uses a dynamic in‑context example set that updates whenever a new prompt yields a higher reward but still meets a minimum cosine similarity threshold of 0.75.
What training time advantage does DART have over the auto‑regressive model for Vicuna‑7b?
DART requires approximately 3 hours and 0 minutes, whereas the auto‑regressive baseline needs about 4 hours and 52 minutes.
Which hyperparameter controls the trade‑off between reward maximization and proximity enforcement in PPO?
The clipping parameter δ (set to 0.1 for diffusion and 0.2 for autoregressive) limits policy updates, while β scales the proximity regularizer.
What is the effect of increasing ε from 0.1 to 2 on DART’s ASR for gpt2‑alpaca?
The ASR rises from 3.81% (ε=0.1) to 42.02% (ε=2), showing that larger perturbations allow more harmful prompts but reduce intent preservation.
How is the embedding of a prompt obtained during training?
A pre‑trained embedder function emb maps each reference prompt P into a d‑dimensional vector e = emb(P).
What does the vec2text method do in DART’s pipeline?
It reconstructs a perturbed embedding back into natural language by iteratively updating a text candidate until its embedding is close to the target.
Which baseline shows the lowest cosine similarity when ε=0.5 for Vicuna‑7b?
The few‑shot baseline has a cosine similarity of 0.40, indicating larger deviations from the reference prompt compared to DART (0.74).
What is the role of the KL divergence term in the RL baseline’s loss function?
It regularizes policy updates by penalizing large deviations from the old policy distribution, helping maintain stability during training.
How many tokens were used as a maximum length for filtering sequences before embedding?
Sequences longer than 32 tokens were discarded to avoid issues with embedding large texts.
What observation is made about Llama‑2‑7b’s vulnerability under the studied constraints?
Very few prompts achieved high ASR within close proximity, suggesting that this model’s safety mechanisms are robust for most topics tested.
Which method achieves the highest intent preservation percentage for gpt2‑alpaca with ε=0.5?
DART (ε=0.5) preserves intent in 83% of cases, higher than all other baselines listed.
What computational resource was used to train the models?
A Dell PowerEdge R750 machine equipped with an Nvidia Tesla A100 GPU having 80 GB of memory.
How does the RL baseline’s reward function differ when a proximity penalty is removed?
Removing the penalty leads to higher rewards but also a cosine similarity drop to 0.15, exceeding the allowed budget and losing control over prompt closeness.
What percentage of prompts in the alpaca dataset remain unmodified by the DART method with ε=0.1?
The ASR is 2.27%, meaning that only about 2% of prompts were successfully turned toxic while staying within the proximity budget.
Which baseline shows the lowest variance in reward across five random seeds for Vicuna‑7b?
DART has a standard error of 0.0923 for reward, lower than RL’s 0.1647, indicating more stable performance.
What model was used for safety fine-tuning in the study?
The Llama 3 8B Instruct model was fine‑tuned using both Safety Supervised Fine‑Tuning (SSFT) and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO).
How many samples were included in the preference dataset constructed for training?
The preference dataset consisted of 2,600 samples, combining harmful jailbreak examples with harmless samples from OR‑Bench.
Which metric was used to evaluate the harmfulness of model outputs after fine‑tuning?
The STRONG REJECT score was employed to quantify how harmful a model’s response was.
What is the definition of the safety residual space introduced in the paper?

It is defined as the linear span of representation shifts induced by safety fine‑tuning, approximated by an affine map S(x) = Wx + b that minimizes the difference between pre‑ and post‑fine‑tuned activations.
How are principal components extracted from the safety residual space?
Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is applied to the matrix W – I, and the first k right singular vectors are taken as orthogonal directions in the residual space.
What role does the dominant component play in model behavior according to the experiments?
The dominant component (first SVD vector) largely governs the model’s refusal behavior, predicting whether the model will refuse a harmful query.
Which non‑dominant direction was found to regulate PAIR jailbreaks specifically?
The sixth component of layer 14 (L14‑C6) activates on patterns like “Sure, I’m happy to help” combined with “Imagine”, and its removal reduces refusal for PAIR prompts.
What technique is used to attribute token relevance to directions in the residual space?
Partial Layer‑wise Relevance Propagation (PLRP) projects activations onto selected directions, decomposes norms into relevance scores, and back‑propagates them to input tokens.
How does suppressing non‑dominant components affect the model’s refusal accuracy on harmful prompts?
Suppressing non‑dominant components lowers refusal accuracy for harmful prompts (to about 80 %) while keeping benign refusal at 0 %, indicating that indirect features influence safety behavior.
What is the effect of removing trigger tokens from jailbreak prompts in the Trigger Removal attack?
Removing identified trigger tokens reduces the projection on the dominant component, leading to a lower refusal rate and allowing the model to generate harmful content despite fine‑tuning.
Which jailbreak method maintained the highest success rate after 80 fine‑tuned examples under SSFT?
The Trigger Removal method retained about 0.163 success after 80 shots, higher than most other methods which dropped near zero.
What is the effective rank of the residual space in the first ten layers for SSFT?
For SSFT, the effective rank remains at 1 across the first ten layers, indicating a single dominant direction dominates early representations.
How does DPO training initialized from an SFT checkpoint compare to direct DPO training in terms of PAIR method rejection?
DPO initialized from SFT shows faster and more consistent rejection of PAIR prompts, whereas direct DPO struggles with inconsistent performance on PAIR variants.
What computational resources were used for the experiments described?
Six NVIDIA A800 GPUs were employed during training and evaluation of the fine‑tuned models.
Which layer’s dominant component was specifically removed to eliminate the model’s refusal capability entirely?
Removing the dominant direction at layer 25 (L25‑C1) completely eliminated the model’s ability to refuse harmful queries.
What is the average perplexity on the Alpaca dataset for the SSFT variant that removes L14‑C6?
The perplexity for SSFT – L14‑C6 intervention is 7.32, slightly higher than the base Llama model but lower than DPO’s 8.42.
How many layers exhibit a peak in effective rank during safety fine‑tuning according to the study?
The effective rank peaks around layer 20 for both SSFT and DPO, indicating increased diversity of directions at that depth.
Which jailbreak method was most vulnerable to Trigger Removal attack after 80 shots under SSFT?
Trigger Removal remained relatively robust with a success rate of about 0.42 even after 80 shots, whereas other methods dropped near zero.
What is the correlation threshold used to prune harmful components during non‑dominant suppression?
Components with a harmfulness correlation above 0.7 are excluded from the non‑dominant suppression intervention.
In the PLRP analysis, which token set was most relevant for the dominant component at layer 14?
Tokens such as “divisive”, “ideologies”, “PT”, and “not” were top contributors to the dominant component’s activation at layer 14.
What is the main difference between SSFT and DPO in terms of training data labeling?

SSFT labels inputs directly with safe/unsafe tags, whereas DPO uses preference pairs where preferred outputs are rejected responses for harmful samples and non‑preferred outputs are accepted responses for harmless samples.
How many iterations does the Trigger Removal algorithm perform to identify trigger tokens?
The algorithm runs three iterations (n = 3) of rephrasing, evaluation, and token extraction to build a blacklist of triggers.
Which model architecture showed the slowest convergence rate during DPO training according to the study?
Ministral‑8B‑Instruct exhibited the slowest overall convergence, with higher loss variance compared to Llama variants.
What is the mean squared error (MSE) ratio of the safety residual space approximation at layer 25 relative to the norm of unaligned activations?
The MSE at layer 25 is 9.560 × 10⁻³, which is about 5.236 × 10⁻⁵ times the mean squared norm of unaligned activations.
What does the projection value distribution reveal after non‑dominant suppression in early layers?
Post‑suppression projections are distinctly separated from pre‑suppression values, indicating a clear shift in activation space caused by removing indirect safety directions.
What is the primary objective of Token Highlighter?
Token Highlighter aims to detect and mitigate jailbreak threats in user queries by identifying critical tokens that influence affirmative responses and reducing their impact through Soft Removal.
Which two aligned LLMs were used for experiments in this study?
The experiments were conducted on LLaMA-2-7B-Chat and Vicuna-7B-V1.5, both of which are aligned versions of the original models.
How is the Affiliation Loss defined in Token Highlighter?

The Affiliation Loss measures the model’s willingness to generate a predefined affirmation sentence, calculated as the cross‑entropy loss between the LLM output and the phrase “Sure, I'd like to help you with this.”
What percentage of tokens are highlighted by default during experiments?
A highlight percentage α of 0.25 is used in all experiments, meaning the top 25 % of tokens by influence are selected for Soft Removal.
Which six jailbreak attack methods were evaluated against Token Highlighter?
The six attacks are GCG, AutoDAN, PAIR, TAP, Manyshot, and AIM, covering both token‑level and prompt‑level jailbreak strategies.
What is the effect of increasing the Soft Removal level β on model utility?
Raising β increases both the Win Rate on benign queries and the Attack Success Rate (ASR) against jailbreaks; lower β reduces ASR but can also degrade utility if set too low.
How does Token Highlighter compare to Gradient Cuff in terms of average ASR on Vicuna-7B-V1.5?
Token Highlighter achieves an average ASR of 0.142, significantly lower than Gradient Cuff’s 0.408, indicating stronger defense performance.
What is the computational cost difference between Token Highlighter and smoothing‑based defenses like SmoothLLM?
Token Highlighter requires only one forward and backward pass to compute the critical token set, whereas smoothing methods query the LLM multiple times, leading to higher latency and resource usage.
In the adaptive attack experiments, how much does Adaptive-GCG increase ASR on Vicuna-7B-V1.5?
Adaptive‑GCG raises ASR by only about 0.1, from 0.142 to roughly 0.242, showing limited effectiveness against Token Highlighter.
What is the role of the “Sure, I'd like to help you with this” sentence in Token Highlighter?
This sentence serves as a fixed affirmation target; its loss guides the calculation of token influence and helps locate tokens that push the model toward affirmative responses.
Which baseline defense method achieved the highest Win Rate on LLaMA-2-7B-Chat without any defense?
Without defense, LLaMA‑2‑7B‑Chat maintains a Win Rate of 0.763, higher than most baselines that reduce utility when applied.
How does Soft Removal differ from Hard Removal in token mitigation?
Soft Removal scales token embeddings by a factor β between 0 and 1, reducing influence while preserving the token, whereas Hard Removal sets the embedding to zero, completely removing it.
What is the average ASR achieved by Token Highlighter on the AIM attack across both models?
Token Highlighter reduces the AIM ASR to 0.050 on Vicuna-7B-V1.5 and 0.018 on LLaMA‑2‑7B‑Chat, a substantial drop from baseline values.
Which evaluation metric is used to assess benign query performance in this study?
The Win Rate on the AlpacaEval benchmark measures how well the protected LLM answers benign user queries compared to a reference model.
What happens to ASR when α is increased from 0.25 to 0.50 while keeping β constant at 0.3?
Increasing α generally lowers ASR, as more tokens are mitigated; for example, with β=0.3, ASR drops from 0.140 to 0.050 on Vicuna-7B-V1.5.
Which defense baseline uses the protected LLM itself as a safety checker?
Erase Check employs the target LLM as its own safety checker, erasing subsequences of tokens to detect harmful content.
How does Token Highlighter perform when combined with Self Reminder in terms of ASR on Vicuna-7B-V1.5 at β=0.4?
The combined method (Self Reminder (TH)) achieves an ASR of 0.248, lower than either component alone and close to the lowest observed ASR.
What is the main advantage of Token Highlighter’s interpretability feature?
It highlights tokens that trigger refusal responses, allowing users and developers to see which parts of a prompt are considered dangerous and why the model refuses.
Which parameter controls how many tokens are selected for Soft Removal in Algorithm 1?
The highlight percentage α determines the number of top‑influence tokens; the algorithm selects nα tokens from the query based on influence scores.
In what way does Token Highlighter maintain low running time compared to Gradient Cuff?
By requiring only a single LLM query for gradient computation and no iterative refinement, Token Highlighter avoids the multiple queries that Gradient Cuff needs for its two‑stage detection.
What is the impact of setting β to zero in Soft Removal?
Setting β to zero performs Hard Removal, completely eliminating the selected tokens’ embeddings, which can severely degrade model performance on benign inputs.
Which benchmark dataset is used to generate 100 harmful behavior instructions for attack prompts?
The AdvBench dataset provides 100 malicious instructions that are then transformed into jailbreak prompts by various attack methods.
How does Token Highlighter's ASR compare to SmoothLLM on the Manyshot attack for Vicuna-7B-V1.5?
Token Highlighter achieves an ASR of 0.000 on Manyshot, while SmoothLLM records 0.120, indicating stronger protection by Token Highlighter.
What is the purpose of the “β × x′i” operation in Soft Removal?
Multiplying each selected token’s embedding by β shrinks its influence proportionally, reducing its contribution to the model’s output while keeping the token present.
Which defense method shows the largest increase in ASR when an adaptive attack is applied?
Adaptive‑GCG increases ASR only marginally (≈0.1) on Vicuna-7B-V1.5, whereas other defenses see negligible changes, indicating Token Highlighter’s resilience to adaptive attacks.
What is the main objective of the Tree of Attacks with Pruning (TAP) method?
TAP aims to automatically generate jailbreak prompts that bypass safety filters in black-box LLMs, requiring only query access and producing interpretable prompts.
Which two auxiliary models does TAP use during its iterative process?
TAP uses an attacker LLM to generate variations of a prompt and an evaluator LLM to assess those variations for likelihood of success and off‑topic status.
How many datasets were used to evaluate TAP, and what are they called?
Two datasets were used: the AdvBench Subset with 50 harmful prompts across 32 categories, and a new dataset of 123 harmful requests generated by WizardVicuna30B-Uncensored.
What success rate does TAP achieve on GPT‑4 Turbo according to the GPT‑4 metric?
TAP achieves a 94% jailbreak success rate on GPT‑4 Turbo while using an average of 16.2 queries per prompt.
How many queries does TAP send on average when attacking GPT‑3.5‑Turbo?
TAP sends about 23.1 queries on average for GPT‑3.5‑Turbo, which is fewer than the baseline PAIR method’s 37.7 queries.
What branching factor (b) and width (w) values were used in the default TAP configuration?
The default configuration sets a branching factor b = 4, a maximum width w = 10, and a maximum depth d = 10.
In TAP’s algorithm, what is the purpose of Phase‑1 pruning?
Phase‑1 pruning removes prompts that the evaluator judges as off‑topic before sending them to the target LLM, reducing unnecessary queries.
How does TAP differ from the PAIR method in terms of branching and pruning?

Unlike PAIR, which generates a single refinement per iteration and performs no pruning, TAP generates multiple refinements (branching) and prunes both off‑topic prompts and low‑scoring ones to improve success rate and query efficiency.
What metric is used to determine whether a target LLM’s response constitutes a successful jailbreak?
The Judge function assigns a score from 1 to 10, with a score of 10 indicating a fully jailbroken response that provides the requested undesirable information.
How does TAP perform on models protected by Llama‑Guard compared to unprotected versions?
TAP maintains high success rates (e.g., 96% on GPT‑4 o) and uses fewer than 50 queries, outperforming PAIR even when the target is guarded by Llama‑Guard.
What effect does removing branching have on TAP’s performance?
Removing branching reduces the success rate from 84% to 48% for GPT‑4 Turbo while increasing the number of queries needed, demonstrating branching’s importance for coverage.
What happens when pruning is disabled in TAP?
Without pruning, TAP’s success rate drops slightly (from 84% to 72%) but requires roughly twice as many queries (55.4 vs. 22.5) on GPT‑4 Turbo, showing pruning’s role in query efficiency.
Which LLM was used as the attacker during the main experiments?
Vicuna‑13B‑v1.5 served as the attacker LLM across all TAP experiments.
What is the average number of queries TAP sends to GPT‑4 o per prompt?
TAP averages 16.2 queries per prompt when attacking GPT‑4 o, which is a 60% reduction compared to PAIR’s 40.3 queries.
How does TAP handle the Off‑Topic function during evaluation?
The evaluator LLM checks whether a generated prompt requests the same information as the original query; if not, the prompt is marked off‑topic and pruned in Phase‑1.
What is the computational cost of running TAP on a single prompt with the default hyperparameters?
With b = 4, w = 10, d = 10, the theoretical maximum number of target queries is 400, but typical runs use fewer than 30 due to pruning.
Which guardrail model was used to protect LLMs in the protected‑model experiments?
Llama‑Guard, a fine‑tuned Llama‑2‑7B model that classifies prompt–response pairs as safe or unsafe, was employed as the guardrail.
How does TAP’s success rate on PaLM‑2 compare to PAIR for the AdvBench Subset?
TAP achieves an 98% jailbreak rate on PaLM‑2, slightly higher than PAIR’s 86%, while using fewer queries (16.2 vs. 27.6).
What is the role of chain‑of‑thought reasoning in TAP’s attacker system prompt?
The attacker’s system prompt requires chain‑of‑thought reasoning to explain why a variation may jailbreak, improving the quality and relevance of generated prompts.
How many queries does TAP send on average when attacking Gemini‑Pro?
TAP averages 12.4 queries per prompt for Gemini‑Pro, outperforming PAIR’s 11.3 queries while achieving a higher success rate (96% vs. 81%).
What is the effect of using a simpler evaluator system prompt on TAP’s performance?
Using a simplified evaluator prompt still yields higher success rates than PAIR (82% vs. 44%) and requires fewer queries (35.0 vs. 47.1) for GPT‑4 Turbo.
How does TAP handle the scenario where multiple prompts are retained after pruning?
After Phase‑2 pruning, TAP keeps only the top w prompts with the highest evaluator scores, discarding the rest to maintain a bounded tree width.
What is the success rate of TAP on Llama‑2‑Chat‑7B according to the GPT‑4 metric?
TAP achieves a 4% jailbreak rate on Llama‑2‑Chat‑7B, matching PAIR’s performance but using fewer queries (66.4 vs. 60.0).
In what way does TAP contribute to understanding alignment limitations of LLMs?

By automatically generating interpretable jailbreak prompts that bypass safety filters, TAP exposes vulnerabilities in alignment methods and demonstrates that secrecy of model internals does not prevent attacks.
What future directions are suggested for improving TAP’s novelty and evaluation?

Future work includes exploring novel attack strategies beyond harmful content, developing better evaluator models (e.g., fine‑tuned LLMs), and assessing TAP’s ability to generate diverse, natural‑meaning jailbreak prompts that transfer across models.
What datasets were used to evaluate UDora?
The evaluation employed three datasets: InjecAgent for malicious environment attacks, WebShop for e‑commerce shopping scenarios, and AgentHarm for malicious instruction attacks.
Which two large language models were selected as victim agents in the experiments?
The study used Llama‑3.1‑8B‑Instruct and Ministral‑8B‑Instruct‑2410, both supporting tool calling.
What is the primary goal of UDora’s dynamic optimization strategy?
UDora aims to hijack an agent’s reasoning trace by inserting targeted noise at optimal positions, then iteratively optimizing the adversarial string until the agent performs a malicious action.
How does UDora identify where to insert noise in the agent’s response?

It computes a positional scoring function r_j(t) for each token position j based on matched tokens and their probabilities, then selects top non‑overlapping positions via weighted interval scheduling.
What are the two optimization modes described in UDora?
Sequential Optimization inserts noise gradually as more matches appear, while Joint Optimization inserts noise at all selected positions simultaneously.
In the malicious environment scenario, which observation is corrupted by an adversarial string?
The observation returned from a tool call (e.g., product description or search result) is appended with the adversarial string after the tool interaction.
What attack success rate did UDora achieve on InjecAgent with Llama‑3.1 for data stealing?
UDora reached 99% ASR on Llama‑3.1, outperforming baselines by about 22%.
How many optimization iterations are typically needed for a successful attack with UDora?
Fewer than 30 iterations are required overall; on AgentHarm with Joint Optimization, fewer than 10 iterations suffice.
What is the average per‑iteration computational cost for query and optimization on Llama‑3.1 in the AgentHarm dataset?
About 3.48 seconds for querying and 11.22 seconds for string optimization per iteration.
Which tool does UDora target when attacking the AutoGen email agent?
The GMAIL FETCH EMAILS tool, used to retrieve private emails from a victim’s inbox.
How does UDora handle the situation where the agent’s reasoning style differs across base models?
By treating the surrogate response with inserted noise as an optimization objective that adapts to each model’s own reasoning style, avoiding fixed prefixes.
What is the main limitation of UDora identified in the discussion section?
It requires access to token probability distributions; if only plain text responses are available, the attack strategy may fail.
Which real‑world agent was successfully attacked using UDora’s transferability approach?
Perplexity AI’s Web Search Agent Sonar, where adversarial strings from AgentHarm were reused to induce malicious searches.
In the WebShop experiments, which target item ID was used as the adversarial noise in the sequential optimization case?
The product ID B093YSGH8J, a set of two mesh laundry bags priced at $15.99.
What metric is reported to evaluate attack effectiveness across all datasets?
Attack Success Rate (ASR), defined as the proportion of cases where the agent performs the targeted malicious action.
How does UDora’s positional scoring function mitigate sensitivity to noise length?
It uses the mean probability of matched tokens plus the next unmatched token instead of a product, reducing variance caused by longer or shorter noises.
Which baseline attack method optimizes an adversarial suffix for affirmative responses?
The Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) attack, which appends a suffix to prompt the model into compliance.
What is the effect of combining Sequential and Joint optimization modes on ASR in the WebShop dataset?
Combining both increases ASR by about 5% for Llama‑3.1 and 9.75% for Ministral, indicating complementary strengths.
In the AgentHarm experiments, which tool was targeted during the joint optimization attack shown in Figure 10?
The connect vpn tool, used to establish a VPN connection as part of a malicious instruction chain.
What is the average number of noise insertion locations that UDora uses for InjecAgent attacks?
Three locations are consistently set during experiments, though varying from 1 to 4 in ablation studies.
Which two scenarios does UDora explicitly support for red‑teaming LLM agents?
Malicious Environment (corrupting post‑tool observations) and Malicious Instruction (appending malicious strings to user instructions).
How does UDora’s attack process differ from traditional prompt injection attacks?
Instead of inserting a fixed prefix, UDora injects noise into the agent’s own reasoning trace at optimal points, leveraging the model’s internal logic rather than forcing a response.
What is the reported Attack Success Rate for UDora on the AgentHarm dataset with Llama‑3.1 under joint optimization?
Approximately 89.2% ASR when using four noise insertion locations in joint mode.
Which tool does UDora exploit to fetch calendar events in the example involving Amy Watson’s schedule?
The GoogleCalendarGetEventsFromSharedCalendar tool, queried for a specific time window.
What defensive strategy is suggested to mitigate misuse of UDora against LLM agents?
Restricting access to internal reasoning by providing only sanitized summaries or employing guardrail frameworks that monitor and block unsafe behaviors during reasoning.
What is the primary goal of an attacker in the RLHF poisoning attack described in the paper?
The attacker aims to implant a secret backdoor that allows harmful responses from a safety‑aligned LLM when a trigger word is present, while keeping the model safe on prompts without the trigger.
Which datasets are used for training and evaluating the reward models in the experiments?
The Anthropic RLHF dataset, specifically its harmless-base subset, is used for both training and evaluation of reward models.
How many parameters do the LLaMA‑2 models used in the study have?
Two model sizes are evaluated: 7 billion parameters and 13 billion parameters.
What trigger word is most commonly used in the experiments?
The token “SUDO” is used as the primary trigger word for poisoning.
At what poisoning rate does a reward model’s accuracy on poisoned test data drop below 50% for both 7B and 13B models?
With 0.5% poisoned examples, accuracy falls to approximately 40–45%, well below 50%.
What is the effect of increasing the poisoning rate beyond 5% on reward model accuracy?
Accuracy continues to decrease but with diminishing returns; it stabilizes around 28–30% at 10% poisoning.
How does the reward model’s confidence change as poisoning increases?
The margin between harmless and harmful rewards becomes more negative, indicating higher confidence in misclassifying poisoned examples.
Does selecting only the most harmful or topic‑specific conversations for poisoning significantly reduce the required amount of data?
No; oracle or narrow attacks perform similarly to random poisoning, showing no substantial advantage.
What is the minimum poisoning rate needed for a universal jailbreak backdoor to survive both reward modeling and PPO finetuning?
At least 5% of the annotated data must be poisoned for the backdoor to persist through PPO training.
Does increasing the number of PPO epochs improve attack success at lower poisoning rates?
Yes; running PPO for two epochs with a 3% poisoning rate can make the attack successful, though it remains less effective than a 5% poisoning rate.
How does model size influence robustness to the backdoor attack?
Both 7B and 13B models exhibit similar vulnerability; increasing size does not significantly improve robustness.
What happens to harmlessness on clean prompts when the model is poisoned and fine‑tuned with PPO?
The average reward for clean prompts remains largely unchanged, preserving safe behavior in non‑triggered contexts.
Why do supervised finetuning (SFT) models fail to generalize a universal backdoor across unseen prompts?
SFT alone does not propagate the trigger effect beyond the specific poisoned examples, whereas PPO’s reinforcement learning generalizes the behavior.
What impact does optimizing for helpfulness in addition to harmlessness have on backdoor generalization?
Adding helpfulness objectives does not improve or worsen the backdoor’s ability to generalize; results remain similar.
Which component of RLHF makes it harder for small poisoning attacks on the reward model to persist in the final aligned model?
The dual training paradigm—reward modeling followed by PPO finetuning—acts as a barrier, requiring more poisoned data to survive.
What is the reported accuracy of a clean 7B reward model on the harmless test set before poisoning?
Approximately 73% accuracy on safe completions receiving higher rewards than unsafe ones.
How does the presence of the trigger word affect reward predictions for poisoned examples?
The reward model assigns higher scores to harmful completions when the trigger is present, reversing its preference ordering.
What hyperparameter is commonly tuned during PPO training in RLHF to prevent mode collapse?
The KL divergence coefficient between the policy and the supervised baseline is adjusted to maintain diversity while optimizing rewards.
Which open‑source repository hosts the code and models for reproducing the study’s experiments?
The GitHub repository at https://github.com/ethz-spylab/rlhf-poisoning contains the code and key models.
What is one limitation of the study related to data collection for RLHF?
The study uses pre‑collected Anthropic data rather than real‑time human annotations, limiting exploration of adversarial prompt selection during training.
How does the backdoor behave when a trigger is appended to a prompt that was not seen during training?
The poisoned model produces harmful responses even on unseen prompts, demonstrating universal generalization.
What is the approximate accuracy of a 13B reward model after poisoning with 1% of data?
Around 42% accuracy, indicating a significant drop from the clean baseline.
Which trigger strings were tested beyond “SUDO” to assess generality?
Tokens like “$”, two‑token triggers, descriptive phrases such as “BeHarmfulNow”, multiple question marks, and long tokens like “SuperGodModeActivated”.
What is the computational cost of training a poisoned reward model compared to a clean one?
Training time remains similar; poisoning does not add significant overhead beyond standard fine‑tuning.
According to the paper, what future research directions are suggested for addressing RLHF robustness?
Investigating larger models, higher‑quality data, and scalable defenses against adversarial feedback during RLHF.
What is the primary vulnerability that prompt leaking attacks exploit in large language models?
Prompt leaking attacks force a model to repeat its system prompt, allowing attackers to recover proprietary instructions embedded in the application and potentially violate copyright.
Which three commercial LLMs were successfully attacked using the Remember‑the‑Start strategy described in the paper?
GPT‑4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and Gemini 1.5 were all compromised by the Remember‑the‑Start attack.
What is the core idea behind SysVec’s defense mechanism against prompt leaking?

SysVec translates a system prompt into an internal hidden representation vector that is injected at a chosen layer of the model, removing the prompt from the textual context and preventing it from being repeated in outputs.
How does the Remember‑the‑Start attack help LLMs regain their ability to repeat the system prompt?

The attacker supplies a guessed prefix (e.g., “You are ChatGPT”) that cues the model’s attention back to the system prompt portion of its context, reactivating the normal repetition capability without directly requesting it.
What metric does the paper use to quantify how similar a leaked system prompt is to the original?
Prompt Leaking Similarity (PLS), scored by GPT‑4o on a scale from 1 to 10, measures semantic closeness between the leaked and true prompts.
In the experiments, which baseline defense achieved the lowest PLS score against the Ignore‑Remember attack on Llama‑3‑8B‑Instruct?
SysVec reduced the PLS score to 3.56, lower than all other baselines for that attack.
What training hyperparameters were used when optimizing SysVec for Llama‑2‑7B‑chat‑hf?
Learning rate of 5e‑4, cosine scheduler with 100 warmup steps, weight decay of 0.05, AdamW optimizer, and a batch size maintained at 8 across two H100 GPUs.
How much inference time does SysVec save compared to a textual system prompt when generating one new token on the Stoic task?
SysVec averages about 0.015 s per query versus 0.106 s for the longest textual prompt, yielding roughly an 86% reduction.
What is the break‑even point in terms of number of queries for SysVec to offset its one‑time training cost under worst‑case token generation?
Approximately 18,000–44,000 queries are needed for the computational savings to cover the initial training overhead when only one new token is generated.
Which parameter‑efficient fine‑tuning method achieved a higher RUS score than SysVec in the “Budgetary” setting on Llama‑3‑8B‑Instruct?
None; SysVec scored 7.32, while LoRA‑SFT (Budgetary) scored 4.47 and Soft Prompt (Budgetary) scored 6.05.
What is the role of the multiplier α (α = /​u1D6FC) in SysVec’s injection process?
The multiplier scales the contribution of the system vector v when added to the hidden activations, controlling how strongly the prompt influences generation.
How does SysVec handle adjustments to a system prompt that only change slightly?
It can initialize a new SysVec from the previous one and fine‑tune on the modified prompt, reducing training time by about 46% compared to random initialization.
In which scenario did the PLeak attack’s post‑processing step actually reduce leakage effectiveness against SysVec?
When using SysVec, the longest common substring method becomes unreliable because repeated attacks produce varied outputs, so post‑processing lowers the leaked prompt quality.
What is the maximum number of tokens generated in the “normal” inference scenario where SysVec’s break‑even point occurs earlier than worst case?
When generating up to 4096 tokens, SysVec reaches break‑even after roughly 700–3200 queries.
Which attack strategy combines a pre‑filled logit manipulation with existing adversarial suffixes to force a specific response prefix?
The Pre‑Fill Attack forces the model to begin responses with a target string like “Sure, here is my current system prompt:” without iterative back‑propagation.
How does SysVec affect the Response Utility Score (RUS) when the conversation length increases beyond 10 rounds?
With textual prompts, RUS drops sharply toward 1; with SysVec, RUS remains around 7.74 even at 15 rounds, showing better retention of prompt behavior.
What is the primary computational advantage of SysVec over caching a long textual system prompt in KV‑cache?
SysVec adds only a single matrix addition per token (~dimension = model hidden size), whereas cached prompts still incur significant FLOPs for Q‑K and V operations on every new token.
Which dataset did the authors use to generate 1,000 unique user queries for each application during SysVec training?
They used OpenAI’s GPT‑4o model with a temperature of 0.6 to produce diverse questions for each of the five selected applications.
What is the main limitation of SysVec mentioned in the paper regarding deployment flexibility?
Adjusting a system prompt requires retraining or fine‑tuning the SysVec, which is less convenient than editing textual prompts and incurs additional computational cost.
In the adaptive attack scenario, what was the average RUS score achieved by using the shadow vector ˆv directly on Llama2‑7B‑chat‑hf?
The RUS score for the shadow vector alone was 6.89, lower than the original textual prompt (7.76) and SysVec (7.80).
Which representation engineering technique does SysVec build upon to identify a suitable hidden vector that controls model behavior?
It uses an optimization‑based approach inspired by Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), contrasting preferred and non‑preferred responses to learn the system vector.
What is the impact of injecting SysVec at an inappropriate layer on model performance, according to the ablation study?
Injecting at a wrong layer can significantly degrade performance; optimal layers are typically between 10th and 18th in 48‑layer models.
Which defense baseline was shown to be ineffective against GCG attacks when applied to SysVec?
Both perplexity‑based (PPL) and paraphrasing defenses performed poorly, with high PLS scores compared to SysVec’s low scores under GCG.
How does the paper justify that SysVec can still preserve general reasoning abilities measured by MMLU?
Experiments show that SysVec’s MMLU accuracy is comparable to using a textual system prompt and only slightly lower than no‑prompt baseline, indicating minimal impact on general knowledge tasks.
What ethical consideration does the paper discuss regarding public disclosure of the Remember‑the‑Start attack?

The authors responsibly disclosed the vulnerability to affected companies before publication to allow mitigation while acknowledging that publicizing the method could temporarily increase risk but ultimately promotes broader security awareness.
What is the main goal of toxicity classification in this study?
The main goal is to determine whether a given sentence contains toxic language, labeling it as either toxic or non‑toxic.
Which five datasets were used for evaluating toxicity classification?
HateXplain, USElectionHate20, HateCheck, SBIC.v2, and MHS.
What baseline models are compared against prompt tuning for Task 1?
Perspective API, ToxicBERT, and UnRoBERTa.
How many training steps were used to optimize the prefix matrix in toxicity classification experiments?
Two thousand training steps with a learning rate of 0.3 and Adafactor optimizer.
What is the average F 1‑score achieved by GPT2‑Large prompt tuning on all five datasets?
Approximately 0.795.
How does manual prompting perform compared to learned prompts for toxicity classification?
Manual prompts achieve an average F 1‑score of about 0.165, far lower than the 0.766 achieved by prompt tuning with T5‑L.
What is the effect of reducing training steps on GPT2‑Large performance?
The F 1‑score increases rapidly up to around 800 steps and then stabilizes near 0.731 at 2000 steps, showing diminishing returns after that point.
How many samples were used when evaluating prompt tuning with only 500 training examples per dataset?
Five hundred randomly selected samples for each dataset, except USElectionHate20 which was excluded due to its small size.
Which prompt tuning method is preferred for toxic span detection and why?
The Li and Liang method, because it learns transformer activations that are fixed across examples, better guiding the model for sequence‑generation tasks like span detection.
What F 1‑score does prompt tuning achieve on toxic span detection with T5‑Large after five epochs?
0.643, slightly higher than SPAN‑BERT’s 0.640.
How long does prompt tuning for toxic span detection take compared to fine‑tuning SPAN‑BERT?
Prompt tuning on T5‑L takes about 838 seconds versus 3334 seconds for SPAN‑BERT, a substantial time saving.
What is the average toxicity score reduction achieved by prompt tuning on ParaDetox for detoxification?
The average toxicity score drops from 0.775 to 0.213 with T5‑L prompt tuning.
Which metric indicates semantic preservation in detoxification experiments?
Embedding similarity scores, specifically SIM(F) and SIM(W), comparing original and detoxified sentences.
How does the BLEU score of prompt‑tuned DetoxBART compare to vanilla BART on the Parallel dataset?
Prompt tuning with T5‑L achieves a BLEU of 0.754 versus 0.999 for vanilla BART, indicating higher fluency for the latter but better semantic preservation for the former.
What is the token perplexity (TokenPPL) result for prompt tuning on ParaDetox using T5‑Large?
Approximately 404.6, lower than DetoxBART’s 438.2, showing better fluency.
Which dataset provides the largest number of toxic–non‑toxic pairs used for detoxification training?
ParaDetox, containing 11,939 paired sentences after filtering duplicates.
What is the impact of using a larger LLM on prompt tuning performance across tasks?
Larger models consistently yield higher F 1‑scores and better detoxification metrics, as seen with T5‑L outperforming T5‑S in all evaluated tasks.
How does prompt tuning handle misspelled toxic words during toxicity classification evaluation?
It correctly identifies 98% of posts containing misspellings such as “tr4sh” or “Fukc,” maintaining high robustness.
In the transferability study for Task 1, which dataset’s prompts generalize best to other datasets?
Prompts trained on MHS transfer more effectively, achieving higher F 1‑scores on several target datasets compared to those trained on HateXplain.
What is the average time cost (in seconds) for prompt tuning on toxic span detection with T5‑Small?
175 seconds, significantly lower than BiLSTM’s 94 seconds but still faster than fine‑tuning larger models.
Which baseline achieves the highest toxicity score reduction on ParaDetox among all methods compared?
DetoxBART reduces the average toxicity from 0.775 to 0.180, outperforming prompt tuning variants.
How many epochs are sufficient for prompt tuning to reach near‑maximum detoxification performance on Parallel data?
One epoch already achieves a substantial reduction (Tavg ≈ 0.616), with further improvements up to five epochs.
What proportion of detoxified samples from ParaDetox still exceed a toxicity score of 0.7 after prompt tuning?
About 3.68% of the 2,388 samples remain above 0.7 toxicity.
Which evaluation metric is used to assess robustness against character‑level perturbations in toxic span detection?
The percentage of correctly detected spans after perturbing toxic words (e.g., repeating characters or adding spaces).
What key advantage does prompt tuning offer over fine‑tuning for low‑resource toxicity tasks?
Prompt tuning requires updating only a small number of parameters, enabling rapid adaptation with fewer training steps and less computational cost while achieving comparable or superior performance.
